Researchers who study creativity have found that the mind needs these quiet gaps in order to make unexpected connections between ideas, the very connections that lead to original thinking.

There is also a link between boredom and creativity in children. Studies suggest that children who are never bored, because they are constantly entertained, may find it harder to invent their own games or entertain themselves. A child staring at the ceiling with "nothing to do" is often, in fact, learning to imagine, to plan, and to enjoy their own company. These are skills that no app can teach, and they may matter more for a child's future than any game played on a screen.

None of this means we should throw away our phones or pretend that technology has no benefits. The point is more balanced. Constant stimulation comes at a hidden cost, and the ability to sit quietly with our own thoughts is a skill worth protecting. Perhaps the next time we find ourselves waiting with nothing to do, the wisest choice is not to reach for a screen, but to allow ourselves, just for a few minutes, to be gloriously, usefully bored.

questions.question: What is the main idea of the passage?
choices: Modern mobile phones have now completely ruined almost everyone daily lives.
Boredom has a hidden value that we risk losing.
Children today are far lazier than in the past.
Waiting for buses and trains is always unpleasant.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — แก่นของบทคือ ความเบื่อมีคุณค่าที่ซ่อนอยู่ และเรากำลังเสี่ยงที่จะสูญเสียมันไปเพราะหน้าจอ ส่วน A/C เกินจริง, D เป็นรายละเอียด

questions.question: According to the passage, what does the brain do when we are bored?
choices: It switches off and stops working entirely.
It becomes anxious and harder to control.
It enters a relaxed state where ideas form.
It remembers only unpleasant past events.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — ย่อหน้าที่ 3 บอกว่าเมื่อเบื่อ สมองไม่ได้ปิดตัว แต่เข้าสู่สภาวะผ่อนคลายที่ความคิดล่องลอยและไอเดียก่อตัว

questions.question: What do studies suggest about children who are never bored?
choices: They may find it harder to entertain themselves.
They almost always grow up to be much more successful than others.
They learn to read at a much younger age.
They prefer playing outdoors to using screens.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — ย่อหน้าที่ 4 ระบุว่าเด็กที่ไม่เคยเบื่อ (เพราะถูกป้อนความบันเทิงตลอด) อาจคิดเกมเองหรือหาความเพลิดเพลินด้วยตัวเองได้ยากกว่า

questions.question: In paragraph 3, the phrase "drift freely" is closest in meaning to:
choices: come to a sudden and complete stop
move without restriction
follow strict rules
disappear forever
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — "thoughts drift freely" หมายถึงความคิดเคลื่อนไปอย่างอิสระ ตรงกับ "move without restriction"

questions.question: What can be inferred about the writer's view of phones?
choices: Phones should be banned for all children.
Phones are useful but can have hidden costs.
Phones have no real benefits at all.
Phones are the only cause of modern boredom.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ย่อหน้าสุดท้ายบอกว่าไม่ต้องทิ้งโทรศัพท์และเทคโนโลยีมีประโยชน์ แต่การกระตุ้นตลอดเวลามีต้นทุนแฝง จึงมองว่ามีประโยชน์แต่มีต้นทุน

questions.question: In paragraph 3, "these moments of mental wandering" refers to times when:
choices: we are working as hard as we possibly can
thoughts drift freely and ideas appear
we are checking messages on a phone
children are being constantly entertained
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ประโยคก่อนหน้าพูดถึงสภาวะที่ความคิดล่องลอยและไอเดียผุดขึ้น ดังนั้นวลีนี้จึงหมายถึงช่วงเวลาที่จิตใจล่องลอยอิสระ

questions.question: How would you best describe the writer's tone?
choices: Angry and accusing
Thoughtful and balanced
Sad, gloomy, and rather without any hope
Joking and silly
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ผู้เขียนชั่งน้ำหนักทั้งข้อดีของเทคโนโลยีและคุณค่าของความเบื่ออย่างมีเหตุผล จึงเป็นน้ำเสียงไตร่ตรองและสมดุล

questions.question: How is the passage mainly organized?
choices: It describes a change, questions it, then gives a balanced view.
It lists historical events in the order they happened.
It compares the lives of two different children.
It gives step-by-step instructions for using a phone.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — บทเริ่มด้วยการเปลี่ยนแปลง (ความเบื่อหายไป) → ตั้งคำถามว่าดีจริงไหม → ปิดด้วยมุมมองสมดุล จึงเป็นโครงแบบนี้

passages.title: Should Cities Ban Private Cars?
passages.passage:
In a growing number of cities around the world, planners are asking a question that once seemed unthinkable: should private cars be banned from city centres altogether? Faced with choking traffic, dirty air, and streets designed for vehicles rather than people, some governments have begun to limit or remove cars from their busiest districts. The idea is bold, and it divides opinion sharply.

Those in favour argue that the benefits are too large to ignore. Cars are a major source of the air pollution that damages health and shortens lives, particularly in dense urban areas. Removing them, supporters say, would lead to cleaner air, quieter streets, and fewer accidents. Space once swallowed by roads and car parks could be returned to parks, cycle lanes, and pedestrian areas. Cities such as those that have pedestrianised their centres often report not only environmental gains but also livelier streets and stronger local businesses, as people linger and spend more when they are not surrounded by traffic.

Critics, however, warn that a ban risks punishing the very people it claims to help. Not everyone can cycle or walk long distances; the elderly, the disabled, and parents with young children may depend on cars. Workers who must carry tools or travel between sites cannot simply switch to a bicycle. Unless public transport is excellent, affordable, and reliable, a ban may simply make life harder for those with the fewest choices, while wealthier residents adapt with ease. There is also the question of businesses that rely on deliveries and customers arriving by car.

The most thoughtful voices in this debate suggest that the answer is not a simple yes or no. A sudden, total ban imposed without preparation would almost certainly fail and provoke anger. But a gradual approach — improving buses and trains first, building safe cycle routes, and then slowly reducing car access — has worked in several cities. The lesson seems to be that cars should not be removed before good alternatives are firmly in place.

Ultimately, the debate over private cars is about more than transport. It forces us to ask what cities are really for, and whom they should serve. A street can be a channel for moving vehicles as quickly as possible, or it can be a public space where people meet, walk, and live. How cities answer that question will shape urban life for generations to come.

questions.question: What is the central question of the passage?
choices: Whether cities should ban private cars from their centres.
Whether the public buses in a city are truly cheaper than private cars.
Whether cycling is healthier than driving a car.
Whether city air is dirtier than country air.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — ทั้งบทถกประเด็น "เมืองควรห้ามรถยนต์ส่วนตัวในใจกลางเมืองไหม" ตัวเลือกอื่นเป็นรายละเอียดย่อยที่ไม่ใช่คำถามหลัก

questions.question: What benefit do supporters of a car ban mention?
choices: Faster delivery of goods to every shop.
Cleaner air and quieter, safer streets.
Higher wages for all city workers.
More parking spaces in the city centre.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ย่อหน้าที่ 2 บอกว่าการเอารถออกจะทำให้ "cleaner air, quieter streets, and fewer accidents" ตัวเลือกอื่นขัดกับเนื้อหา

questions.question: What concern do critics of a ban raise?
choices: It would make city centres far too quiet.
It would reduce the number of city parks.
It may hurt people who depend on cars.
It would lower the price of public transport.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — ย่อหน้าที่ 3 บอกว่าการแบนอาจ "punishing the very people it claims to help" เช่น ผู้สูงอายุ คนพิการ พ่อแม่ที่มีลูกเล็ก ที่ต้องพึ่งรถ

questions.question: In paragraph 3, the word "adapt" is closest in meaning to:
choices: refuse to change their habits in any way at all
adjust to a new situation
complain loudly about
completely give up
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — "wealthier residents adapt with ease" หมายถึงปรับตัวเข้ากับสถานการณ์ใหม่ได้ง่าย ตรงกับ "adjust to a new situation"

questions.question: What approach does the passage suggest is most likely to succeed?
choices: Banning all cars suddenly and completely.
Never reducing the access of private cars in any way whatsoever.
Improving alternatives first, then reducing cars gradually.
Letting each driver decide for themselves.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — ย่อหน้าที่ 4 บอกว่าวิธีค่อยเป็นค่อยไป (พัฒนาขนส่งสาธารณะก่อน แล้วค่อยลดรถ) ได้ผลในหลายเมือง ส่วนการแบนทันทีมักล้มเหลว

questions.question: In paragraph 3, "those with the fewest choices" most likely refers to:
choices: wealthy residents who own several cars
people who cannot easily walk or cycle
city planners who design the streets
tourists visiting the city for a short time
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — บริบทกล่าวถึงผู้สูงอายุ คนพิการ และพ่อแม่ที่มีลูกเล็กซึ่งพึ่งรถ ดังนั้น those with the fewest choices จึงหมายถึงคนที่เดินหรือปั่นจักรยานไม่สะดวก

questions.question: How would you best describe the writer's overall position?
choices: Strongly against banning cars under any conditions.
Balanced, supporting careful and gradual change.
Completely certain that all bans must fail.
Uninterested in the problem of city traffic.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ผู้เขียนเสนอทั้งสองฝ่ายแล้วสรุปว่าควรเปลี่ยนแบบค่อยเป็นค่อยไปและรอบคอบ จึงเป็นจุดยืนสมดุลที่หนุนการเปลี่ยนอย่างระมัดระวัง

questions.question: How is the passage mainly organized?
choices: It presents one side only and ignores the other.
It gives arguments for, then against, then a middle path.
It tells the story of one driver's daily journey.
It lists cities in order of their population size.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — บทเสนอฝ่ายสนับสนุน → ฝ่ายค้าน → แล้วเสนอทางสายกลาง (ค่อยเป็นค่อยไป) จึงเป็นโครงแบบนี้

questions.question: What deeper question does the writer say the debate raises?
choices: How fast cars should be allowed to travel.
What cities are really for and whom they serve.
Which city has the largest number of cars.
How much a bus ticket should really cost.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ย่อหน้าสุดท้ายบอกว่าการถกเรื่องรถบังคับให้เราถามว่า "what cities are really for, and whom they should serve" จึงตอบข้อ B

passages.title: Why the Ocean Matters More Than We Think
passages.passage:
When we think about the air we breathe, we usually picture forests. Trees are often called the lungs of the planet, and protecting them is rightly seen as essential. Yet there is another, less visible system that produces even more of the oxygen we depend on: the ocean. Scientists estimate that around half of the oxygen on Earth comes not from forests, but from the sea.

The source of this oxygen is not large plants but tiny organisms called phytoplankton. These microscopic life forms float near the surface of the ocean, where there is enough sunlight for them to carry out photosynthesis, just as plants do on land. As they turn sunlight and carbon dioxide into energy, they release oxygen into the water and the air above it. Although each one is far too small to see, together they form the base of nearly all life in the sea, providing food for tiny creatures that are in turn eaten by fish, and eventually by the largest animals in the ocean.

The ocean does more than supply oxygen. It also absorbs a large share of the carbon dioxide that humans release by burning fuels. Without this service, the warming of our planet would be even faster and more dangerous than it already is. In a very real sense, the ocean acts as a giant shield, slowing down climate change and giving us time to act.

Unfortunately, this shield is under threat. As the ocean absorbs more carbon dioxide, its water gradually becomes more acidic, which harms shellfish, coral reefs, and many other forms of life. Rising temperatures and pollution add further pressure, and in some regions the delicate balance that phytoplankton depend on is already beginning to change. If phytoplankton populations were to fall sharply, the effects would reach far beyond the sea, touching the air we breathe and the climate we live in.

Protecting the ocean, then, is not only about saving whales or keeping beaches clean, important as those goals are. It is about protecting a system that quietly keeps the whole planet alive, one that we have only recently begun to understand. The next time we look out at the sea and see nothing but empty water, it is worth remembering that beneath the surface lies one of the most important life-support systems on Earth.

questions.question: What is the main idea of the passage?
choices: Forests alone produce absolutely all of the oxygen found on the planet.
The ocean is a vital life-support system for Earth.
Whales are the most important animals in the sea.
Beaches must be kept clean for tourists to enjoy.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — แก่นของบทคือ มหาสมุทรเป็นระบบค้ำจุนชีวิตที่สำคัญของโลก (ผลิตออกซิเจน + ดูดคาร์บอน) ส่วน A ผิด, C/D เป็นรายละเอียดเล็กน้อย

questions.question: What produces about half of Earth's oxygen, according to the passage?
choices: Large trees growing in tropical forests.
Tiny ocean organisms called phytoplankton.
Coral reefs found in warm shallow seas.
Whales and other large sea animals.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ย่อหน้าที่ 1–2 ระบุว่าออกซิเจนราวครึ่งหนึ่งมาจาก phytoplankton สิ่งมีชีวิตจิ๋วในทะเล ไม่ใช่ต้นไม้

questions.question: Besides oxygen, what important service does the ocean provide?
choices: It absorbs a large share of carbon dioxide.
It produces most of the world's fresh water.
It blocks harmful sunlight from reaching land.
It creates all of the planet's strong winds.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — ย่อหน้าที่ 3 บอกว่ามหาสมุทร "absorbs a large share of the carbon dioxide" ช่วยชะลอภาวะโลกร้อน

questions.question: In paragraph 3, the phrase "a giant shield" suggests the ocean:
choices: blocks all sunlight from the planet
protects Earth by slowing climate change
is made of a very hard and solid metal
is too large for humans to study
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — "acts as a giant shield, slowing down climate change" จึงหมายถึงมหาสมุทรปกป้องโลกด้วยการชะลอการเปลี่ยนแปลงภูมิอากาศ

questions.question: Why does the writer say the ocean's 'shield' is under threat?
choices: Because far too many large cargo ships now sail across its surface.
Because the water is becoming more acidic.
Because forests are growing too quickly.
Because there are not enough whales left.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ย่อหน้าที่ 4 บอกว่ายิ่งดูดคาร์บอน น้ำทะเลยิ่งเป็นกรด ทำร้ายสิ่งมีชีวิต จึงเป็นภัยต่อ 'เกราะ' นี้

questions.question: In paragraph 2, "these microscopic life forms" refers to:
choices: large ocean plants
phytoplankton
coral reefs
shellfish
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ประโยคก่อนหน้าเพิ่งแนะนำ phytoplankton ดังนั้นวลีนี้จึงหมายถึงสิ่งมีชีวิตจิ๋วเหล่านั้น

questions.question: What is the writer's main purpose?
choices: To explain why the ocean is vital and must be protected.
To describe how to go swimming safely in the sea.
To prove that forests are useless to the planet.
To advertise relaxing summer holidays at the seaside beach.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — บทมุ่งอธิบายว่ามหาสมุทรสำคัญต่อชีวิตบนโลกและต้องปกป้อง ไม่ได้สอนว่ายน้ำ ดูถูกป่า หรือโฆษณา

questions.question: How is the passage mainly organized?
choices: It corrects a common belief, then explains the ocean's roles and risks.
It lists sea animals in order of their size.
It tells the story of one scientist's voyage.
It gives clear step-by-step instructions for cleaning up a beach.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — บทเริ่มด้วยการแก้ความเชื่อ (ป่าผลิตออกซิเจนทั้งหมด) → อธิบายบทบาทของมหาสมุทร → แล้วชี้ภัยคุกคาม จึงเป็นโครงนี้

passages.title: The Science of First Impressions
passages.passage:
It takes only a fraction of a second. The moment we meet someone new, our brain begins forming an opinion — about whether the person is friendly or cold, trustworthy or suspicious, confident or nervous. Research suggests that these first impressions form in as little as a tenth of a second, long before we have heard the person say anything meaningful. What is more surprising is how powerfully these snap judgements shape what follows.

From an evolutionary point of view, the speed of first impressions makes sense. For our distant ancestors, the ability to decide quickly whether a stranger was a friend or a threat could mean the difference between safety and danger. There was no time for careful analysis. A brain that judged fast, even if it was sometimes wrong, was more likely to keep its owner alive than one that hesitated. We have inherited this rapid judging system, even though most of us now live in a world where instant threats are rare.

The problem is that first impressions are often inaccurate. A person who appears cold may simply be shy; someone who seems confident may be hiding deep uncertainty. Yet once an impression has formed, it tends to stick. Psychologists call this the "halo effect": if we decide someone is likeable, we unconsciously assume they are also intelligent, honest, and capable, even without evidence. The reverse is also true. A single negative impression can colour everything we later notice about a person, as we unconsciously search for details that confirm what we already believe.

This matters in real life far more than we might think. Job interviews, first dates, and business meetings are all shaped by impressions formed in seconds. Studies show that interviewers often decide about a candidate within the first few minutes, then spend the rest of the interview looking for reasons to justify that early decision.

Understanding how first impressions work does not free us from them entirely, but it can make us wiser. If we know that our snap judgements are fast, powerful, and frequently mistaken, we can learn to hold them more lightly — to give people a second chance, to question our instant reactions, and to remember that the truth about a person usually takes far longer than a tenth of a second to discover.

questions.question: What is the passage mainly about?
choices: How first impressions form, why they persist, and why they mislead.
How to choose and wear the right clothes for an important job interview.
Why our ancestors lived in such dangerous conditions.
How the brain controls the movement of our bodies.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — บทอธิบายว่าความประทับใจแรกเกิดเร็วอย่างไร ทำไมจึงติดทน และทำไมมักผิดพลาด ส่วน B/C/D เป็นรายละเอียดหรือไม่ใช่ใจความหลัก

questions.question: Why does the passage say fast first impressions made sense for our ancestors?
choices: Because they had a great deal of free time.
Because deciding quickly could keep them safe.
Because they enjoyed meeting many new people.
Because they could not see other people clearly.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ย่อหน้าที่ 2 บอกว่าการตัดสินเร็วว่าคนแปลกหน้าเป็นมิตรหรือภัย ช่วยให้บรรพบุรุษอยู่รอด

questions.question: What is the "halo effect" described in the passage?
choices: Assuming a likeable person is also smart and honest.
Forgetting a person's face soon after meeting them.
Judging people only after knowing them for years.
Believing that all strangers are dangerous threats.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — ย่อหน้าที่ 3 อธิบายว่า halo effect คือถ้าเราคิดว่าใครน่าชอบ เราจะเหมาเอาว่าเขาฉลาด ซื่อสัตย์ และเก่งด้วยโดยไม่มีหลักฐาน

questions.question: In paragraph 3, the word "stick" is closest in meaning to:
choices: disappear quickly
remain fixed
change often
become softer
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — "it tends to stick" หมายถึงความประทับใจนั้นติดแน่น/คงอยู่ ตรงกับ "remain fixed"

questions.question: What can be inferred about job interviews from the passage?
choices: Interviewers completely ignore their own first impressions of a candidate.
Early impressions can unfairly shape the whole interview.
Candidates are always judged only on their skills.
Interviews usually last for several long hours.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ย่อหน้าที่ 4 บอกว่าผู้สัมภาษณ์มักตัดสินในไม่กี่นาทีแรก แล้วหาเหตุผลมาสนับสนุน จึงอนุมานได้ว่าความประทับใจแรกมีผลต่อทั้งการสัมภาษณ์อย่างไม่เป็นธรรม

questions.question: In paragraph 3, "The reverse is also true" means that:
choices: a good impression makes us notice good things
a bad impression makes us notice bad things
first impressions never affect our judgement
people always change after we meet them
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ก่อนหน้าพูดถึง halo effect ด้านบวก (ชอบ→คิดว่าดีไปหมด) ดังนั้น 'the reverse' คือด้านลบ: ความประทับใจแย่ทำให้เรามองหาแต่ข้อเสีย

questions.question: What does the writer suggest we should do about first impressions?
choices: Trust them completely because they are always right.
Hold them lightly and give people a second chance.
Avoid meeting any new people at all.
Form them even faster than we already do.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ย่อหน้าสุดท้ายแนะนำให้ "hold them more lightly — to give people a second chance" และตั้งคำถามกับปฏิกิริยาแรกของเรา

questions.question: How is the passage mainly organized?
choices: It explains a phenomenon, its origin, its flaws, and a lesson.
It lists historical events in strict time order.
It compares the experiments of two different famous scientists.
It gives step-by-step instructions for an interview.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — บทอธิบายปรากฏการณ์ (ความประทับใจแรก) → ที่มาเชิงวิวัฒนาการ → ข้อบกพร่อง (มักผิด, halo effect) → ข้อคิด จึงเป็นโครงนี้

passages.title: Street Food and the Soul of a City
passages.passage:
Ask travellers what they remember most about visiting Thailand, Vietnam, or Mexico, and many will not mention museums or shopping centres. They will talk about food eaten on the street: noodles cooked over a roaring flame, grilled chicken sold from a cart, sweet pancakes folded in paper and eaten standing up. Street food is often dismissed as cheap and simple, yet it plays a far larger role in the life of a city than most people realise.

The first thing street food provides is access. A bowl of noodles from a roadside stall costs a fraction of a restaurant meal, which means that students, workers, and families on small budgets can eat well without cooking at home. In many cities, millions of people rely on street vendors for their daily meals. For them, street food is not a tourist attraction; it is the most practical way to stay fed in an expensive urban world.

Street food also supports people at the bottom of the economic ladder. Setting up a stall requires far less money than opening a restaurant, so it offers a path into business for those with little capital: migrants, women supporting families, and workers who have lost other jobs. A single cart can pay for a child's education or a family's rent. When researchers study urban poverty, they often find that street vending is one of the most important safety nets a city has.

There is a cultural side as well. Recipes sold on the street are often passed down through generations, preserving flavours and techniques that formal restaurants have abandoned. The stall becomes a meeting place where office workers, taxi drivers, and students stand side by side, sharing tables and conversation. In this way, street food quietly does something remarkable: it brings together people who would otherwise never meet.

Of course, street food brings challenges, too. Cities must manage crowded pavements, waste, and food safety, and some governments have responded by banning vendors altogether. But many experts argue that the wiser path is to support and regulate street food rather than remove it — providing clean water, simple training, and proper licences. A city that loses its street food does not simply lose cheap meals. It loses jobs, traditions, and a part of its identity that no shopping centre can replace.

questions.question: What is the main idea of the passage?
choices: Street food is dangerous and should be banned from cities.
Street food plays a vital economic, social, and cultural role.
Tourists prefer eating in restaurants rather than on streets.
Cooking at home is always cheaper than buying street food.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — แก่นของบทคือ อาหารริมทางมีบทบาทสำคัญทั้งด้านเศรษฐกิจ สังคม และวัฒนธรรมของเมือง ส่วน A ขัดกับบทสรุป, C/D ไม่ตรงเนื้อหา

questions.question: Why is street food important for people on small budgets?
choices: It lets them eat well at a fraction of restaurant prices.
It is always far healthier than any food cooked at home.
It is served much faster than food in any restaurant.
It allows them to meet famous chefs in person.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — ย่อหน้าที่ 2 บอกว่าอาหารริมทางราคาเพียงเศษเสี้ยวของร้านอาหาร ทำให้นักเรียน คนทำงาน และครอบครัวงบน้อยกินดีได้

questions.question: How does street vending help people with little money start a business?
choices: Banks give free loans to anyone who sells food.
The government pays for all of their equipment.
A stall needs far less money than a restaurant.
Customers always pay extra to support new sellers.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — ย่อหน้าที่ 3 ระบุว่าการตั้งแผงใช้เงินน้อยกว่าการเปิดร้านอาหารมาก จึงเป็นช่องทางเข้าสู่ธุรกิจของคนทุนน้อย

questions.question: In paragraph 3, the phrase "safety nets" is closest in meaning to:
choices: nets used for catching fish in rivers
systems that protect people in hard times
fences built around dangerous machines
rules that stop people from selling food
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — "safety net" ในที่นี้เป็นความหมายเชิงเปรียบเทียบ หมายถึงสิ่งที่รองรับ/คุ้มครองคนยามลำบาก ตรงกับ "systems that protect people in hard times"

questions.question: What can be inferred about banning street vendors completely?
choices: It would make the city much richer and far more famous.
It would harm jobs, traditions, and city identity.
It would improve the taste of restaurant food.
It would help tourists find museums more easily.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ย่อหน้าสุดท้ายบอกว่าเมืองที่สูญเสียอาหารริมทางจะเสียงาน ประเพณี และอัตลักษณ์ จึงอนุมานได้ว่าการแบนทั้งหมดส่งผลเสียเหล่านี้

questions.question: In paragraph 4, "the stall" is described as a meeting place. This emphasizes street food's role in:
choices: bringing different people together
raising the price of city land
teaching tourists to cook noodles
keeping pavements clean and tidy
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — บทบอกว่าแผงอาหารเป็นที่ที่พนักงานออฟฟิศ คนขับแท็กซี่ และนักเรียนมายืนเคียงกัน จึงเน้นบทบาทการเชื่อมคนต่างกลุ่มเข้าหากัน

questions.question: What does the writer suggest cities should do about street food?
choices: Ban it completely from all crowded streets.
Replace it with modern shopping centres.
Ignore it because it cannot be controlled.
Support and regulate it with training and licences.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — ย่อหน้าสุดท้ายเสนอว่าทางที่ฉลาดกว่าคือสนับสนุนและกำกับดูแล (น้ำสะอาด อบรม ใบอนุญาต) แทนการกำจัด

questions.question: How is the passage mainly organized?
choices: It presents several roles of street food, then addresses its challenges.
It tells the story of one noodle seller from early morning to late night.
It compares the street food of two different countries.
It lists the prices of popular dishes in order.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — บทไล่บทบาทของอาหารริมทางทีละด้าน (การเข้าถึง เศรษฐกิจ วัฒนธรรม) แล้วปิดด้วยความท้าทายและทางออก จึงเป็นโครงนี้

passages.title: Should Homework Be Limited?
passages.passage:
Every evening, in millions of homes around the world, the same scene repeats itself: a tired student opens a bag full of homework while parents look on, unsure whether to feel sympathy or to insist that the work gets done. Homework has been part of school life for so long that we rarely question it. Yet in recent years, teachers, parents, and researchers have begun to ask a serious question: does giving students large amounts of homework actually help them learn, or has it become a habit that does more harm than good?

Supporters of homework make reasonable points. Practice, they argue, strengthens new skills; a student who solves ten equations at home will remember the method better than one who solves none. Homework also teaches responsibility and time management, preparing young people for the demands of university and work. For subjects that require memorisation, such as vocabulary, regular review at home is hard to replace.

However, research paints a more complicated picture. Studies suggest that for younger children, homework brings little measurable benefit, while for teenagers the benefits grow but only up to a point. Beyond roughly one to two hours a night, extra homework appears to add stress rather than learning. Overloaded students sleep less, exercise less, and lose time with their families. Some begin to see learning itself as a punishment, which may be the most damaging effect of all, because a student who learns to hate studying carries that feeling far beyond school.

There is also a question of fairness. Homework assumes that every student has a quiet room, a desk, reliable internet, and parents with time to help. In reality, many students study in noisy homes, care for younger siblings, or work part-time jobs in the evening. For them, heavy homework does not measure ability; it measures circumstances. The students who most need school to lift them up are often the ones homework pushes furthest behind.

The answer is probably not to abolish homework entirely, but to use it with far more care. Short, meaningful tasks with clear purposes seem to work better than long lists of repetitive exercises. Several schools that have limited homework report that students arrive more rested and more willing to learn, with no drop in results. Homework, like medicine, may be most effective in the right dose — and dangerous in excess.

questions.question: What is the central question of the passage?
choices: Whether parents should help children with equations.
Whether schools should teach English vocabulary during class time.
Whether large amounts of homework truly help learning.
Whether students should be given part-time jobs.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — บทถกว่า การให้การบ้านปริมาณมากช่วยการเรียนรู้จริงไหม หรือกลายเป็นโทษ ตัวเลือกอื่นเป็นรายละเอียดย่อย

questions.question: What argument do supporters of homework make?
choices: Practice strengthens skills and teaches responsibility.
Homework lets teachers finish lessons more quickly.
Students enjoy homework more than playing games.
Homework keeps parents busy in the evenings.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — ย่อหน้าที่ 2 ระบุว่าฝ่ายสนับสนุนมองว่าการฝึกซ้ำเสริมทักษะ และการบ้านสอนความรับผิดชอบกับการจัดการเวลา

questions.question: According to research, what happens beyond one to two hours of homework a night?
choices: Students start to learn about twice as fast as they did before.
Extra homework adds stress rather than learning.
Test scores rise sharply for all students.
Younger children benefit the most of all.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ย่อหน้าที่ 3 บอกว่าเกินราว 1–2 ชั่วโมงต่อคืน การบ้านเพิ่มความเครียดมากกว่าการเรียนรู้

questions.question: In paragraph 4, the word "circumstances" is closest in meaning to:
choices: a person's natural intelligence
the conditions of a person's life
the rules written by a school
a student's favourite subjects
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — "it measures circumstances" หมายถึงการบ้านวัดสภาพแวดล้อม/เงื่อนไขชีวิตของเด็ก (บ้านเงียบไหม มีโต๊ะไหม) ไม่ใช่ความสามารถ

questions.question: Why does the writer say heavy homework can be unfair?
choices: Because teachers never check it carefully enough.
Because some students lack quiet rooms and free time.
Because homework is printed on expensive paper.
Because parents always do the homework themselves.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ย่อหน้าที่ 4 ชี้ว่าการบ้านตั้งสมมติฐานว่าทุกคนมีห้องเงียบ โต๊ะ อินเทอร์เน็ต แต่จริงๆ หลายคนบ้านเสียงดัง ต้องดูแลน้อง หรือทำงานพิเศษ

questions.question: In paragraph 4, "the ones homework pushes furthest behind" refers to:
choices: students who most need school's support
teachers who assign too much work
parents who cannot help their children
schools that have banned all homework
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — ประโยคก่อนหน้าพูดถึงเด็กที่ต้องการให้โรงเรียนช่วยยกระดับมากที่สุด ดังนั้นวลีนี้จึงหมายถึงเด็กกลุ่มนั้นที่กลับถูกการบ้านถ่วงให้ตามหลัง

questions.question: What solution does the writer favour?
choices: Abolishing homework completely in every school.
Doubling homework so students practise more.
Letting each student decide their own homework.
Giving short, meaningful tasks in the right amount.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — ย่อหน้าสุดท้ายเสนอว่าไม่ควรยกเลิกทั้งหมด แต่ควรให้งานสั้น มีความหมาย และพอดี เปรียบเหมือนยาที่ได้ผลใน "ขนาดที่เหมาะสม"

questions.question: How is the passage mainly organized?
choices: It gives both sides of a debate, then suggests a middle path.
It tells one student's story from morning to night.
It lists school subjects in order of difficulty.
It carefully compares the homework rules used in two named schools.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — บทเสนอฝ่ายสนับสนุน → งานวิจัยและข้อกังวล → แล้วสรุปทางสายกลาง (ให้การบ้านอย่างพอดีและมีความหมาย)

passages.title: Rice: The Grain That Feeds Half the World
passages.passage:
It is easy to take rice for granted. It sits quietly at the centre of the table, the background to almost every meal, so familiar that we hardly notice it. Yet this humble grain feeds more people than any other crop on Earth. More than half of the world's population eats rice every day, and for billions of people in Asia, Africa, and Latin America, a year without rice would be a year of hunger.

Rice is unusual among major crops in the way it is grown. Most varieties spend much of their life standing in water, in the flooded fields known as paddies. The water is not just for the plant's thirst; it also drowns many weeds and pests that would otherwise destroy the harvest, reducing the need for chemicals. This clever system, developed by farmers thousands of years ago, allows the same fields to produce food year after year, sometimes for centuries, without exhausting the soil.

Growing rice has also shaped entire societies. Because flooded fields require careful control of water, rice farming traditionally demanded cooperation: neighbours had to share canals, coordinate planting times, and help each other at harvest. Some historians argue that this need to work together influenced the cultures of rice-growing regions, encouraging strong community ties and shared responsibility. The terraced rice fields carved into hillsides across Asia stand as monuments to generations of collective labour.

Today, however, rice faces serious challenges. The crop is thirsty: producing a single kilogram of rice can require thousands of litres of water, and in many regions water is becoming scarce. Climate change brings further threats, as rising temperatures reduce yields and unpredictable rains disturb planting seasons that farmers have trusted for generations. Meanwhile, the world's population continues to grow, and with it the demand for food.

Scientists are responding with new varieties of rice that need less water, resist disease, and tolerate heat or floods. Farmers are testing techniques that produce more grain with fewer resources, from smarter watering schedules to new ways of preparing the soil. The race is quiet but urgent: the future food security of billions depends on this single plant. The next time a plate of rice appears on the table, it is worth pausing to remember that behind those small white grains lies one of humanity's greatest and most fragile achievements.

questions.question: What is the passage mainly about?
choices: The importance of rice and the challenges it now faces.
The best recipes for cooking rice in different countries.
The reasons why wheat is more popular than rice.
The history of one famous rice farm in Asia.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — บทเล่าความสำคัญของข้าว (อาหารครึ่งโลก วิธีปลูก อิทธิพลต่อสังคม) และความท้าทายปัจจุบัน ส่วน B/C/D ไม่ใช่เนื้อหาหลัก

questions.question: Besides watering the plant, what does flooding the fields do?
choices: It keeps the rice cool during hot weather.
It makes the grains grow twice as large.
It drowns many weeds and harmful pests.
It attracts many kinds of fish that the farmers can later sell.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — ย่อหน้าที่ 2 บอกว่าน้ำท่วมแปลงช่วยกำจัดวัชพืชและศัตรูพืช ลดการใช้สารเคมี

questions.question: How did rice farming influence the cultures of rice-growing regions?
choices: It made farmers prefer to work completely alone.
It encouraged cooperation and strong community ties.
It caused frequent wars over the best fields.
It stopped people from building any canals.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ย่อหน้าที่ 3 บอกว่าการควบคุมน้ำต้องอาศัยความร่วมมือ (แชร์คลอง นัดเวลาปลูก ช่วยกันเก็บเกี่ยว) จึงหล่อหลอมวัฒนธรรมแห่งการร่วมแรง

questions.question: In paragraph 4, the word "scarce" is closest in meaning to:
choices: dirty and unsafe
not enough to meet needs
far too cold for anyone to use
easy to find anywhere
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — "water is becoming scarce" หมายถึงน้ำเริ่มขาดแคลน/ไม่พอใช้ ตรงกับ "not enough to meet needs"

questions.question: Why does the writer call the work of scientists a "quiet but urgent" race?
choices: Because scientists are forbidden from talking about rice.
Because billions of people's food security depends on it.
Because rice fields are always such silent and peaceful places to work.
Because the research must be finished within one year.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ย่อหน้าสุดท้ายบอกว่า "the future food security of billions depends on this single plant" งานจึงเร่งด่วนแม้ไม่เป็นข่าวดัง

questions.question: In paragraph 3, "monuments to generations of collective labour" refers to:
choices: statues of famous farmers in city squares
terraced rice fields carved into hillsides
museums that display old farming tools
canals built by modern engineers
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ประโยคนั้นกล่าวถึง "terraced rice fields carved into hillsides" ว่าเป็นดั่งอนุสรณ์ของแรงงานร่วมหลายชั่วอายุคน

questions.question: What is the writer's main purpose?
choices: To persuade readers that they should stop eating rice altogether.
To explain rice's importance and the threats to it.
To teach farmers how to flood their fields.
To compare rice farming with fishing.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — บทมุ่งอธิบายความสำคัญของข้าวต่อมนุษยชาติและภัยคุกคาม พร้อมความหวังจากงานวิจัย ไม่ได้ชวนเลิกกิน สอนปลูก หรือเปรียบเทียบกับประมง

questions.question: How is the passage mainly organized?
choices: From rice's importance and history to its modern challenges.
From the most expensive rice to the cheapest kind.
As a personal diary describing one rice farmer's long working day.
As a list of countries ranked by rice production.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — บทเริ่มจากความสำคัญ → วิธีปลูกและอิทธิพลทางวัฒนธรรม → ความท้าทายปัจจุบันและทางแก้ จึงเรียงจากความสำคัญสู่ความท้าทาย

passages.title: E-Sports: When Gaming Becomes a Career
passages.passage:
Twenty years ago, telling your parents that you wanted to play video games for a living would have earned you a worried look and a lecture about real jobs. Today, the picture is very different. Competitive video gaming, known as e-sports, has grown into a global industry worth billions of dollars, with professional players, sold-out stadiums, and audiences that rival traditional sports. The question is no longer whether e-sports is real, but what its rise tells us about work, play, and the modern world.

The scale of the industry surprises many people. Major tournaments offer prize pools worth millions, and the best players earn salaries from professional teams, just as footballers do. Matches are broadcast live to millions of viewers around the world, complete with commentators, coaches, analysts, and team managers, all of whom have built serious careers behind the scenes of the games themselves. Universities in several countries now offer scholarships to talented players, treating gaming skill the way they once treated athletic ability.

Being a professional player, however, is far harder than fans imagine. Top competitors practise eight to twelve hours a day, repeating the same movements and strategies until they become automatic. Careers are short: reaction times begin to slow in a player's mid-twenties, and most professionals retire from competition before the age of thirty. The pressure to perform is intense at every tournament, and burnout is common among even the most successful players. In these ways, e-sports resembles gymnastics or swimming more than an easy life of fun.

The industry also creates opportunities far beyond playing. For every professional player, there are many more jobs in event organisation, video production, marketing, coaching, and software development. Students who love games but lack world-class reflexes can still build careers around them — a point that teachers and parents increasingly recognise.

None of this means that every young gamer should dream of going professional, any more than every school footballer should expect to play in a World Cup. The odds are long, and the sacrifices are real. But the rise of e-sports carries a wider lesson: the line between play and work is being redrawn, and skills once dismissed as wasted time can, in the right setting, become genuinely valuable. The wisest response is neither to worship gaming nor to dismiss it, but to understand it.

questions.question: What is the passage mainly about?
choices: The growth of e-sports and what it means for work and play.
The rules of the most popular video game in the world.
The reasons why parents should ban video games at home.
The history of one famous football team's victories.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — บทเล่าการเติบโตของ e-sports เป็นอุตสาหกรรม อาชีพ และบทเรียนที่มันสะท้อนเรื่องงานกับการเล่น ตัวเลือกอื่นไม่ใช่เนื้อหาหลัก

questions.question: How do universities in several countries treat talented players?
choices: They ban them from playing during exams.
They offer them scholarships like athletes.
They force them to study computer science.
They hire them to teach younger students.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ย่อหน้าที่ 2 บอกว่ามหาวิทยาลัยหลายประเทศให้ทุนผู้เล่นที่มีพรสวรรค์ เหมือนที่เคยให้ทุนนักกีฬา

questions.question: Why are professional gaming careers usually short?
choices: Players become bored with games very quickly.
Teams replace players with robots after a few years.
Reaction times begin to slow in the mid-twenties.
Tournaments are held only once every ten years.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — ย่อหน้าที่ 3 ระบุว่าปฏิกิริยาตอบสนองเริ่มช้าลงช่วงกลางวัย 20 ปี ผู้เล่นส่วนใหญ่จึงเลิกแข่งก่อนอายุ 30

questions.question: In paragraph 3, the word "burnout" is closest in meaning to:
choices: a sudden victory in a match
exhaustion from too much pressure
a fire inside a gaming stadium
a new strategy used by teams
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — "burnout" คือภาวะหมดไฟ/อ่อนล้าจากความกดดันและการฝึกหนักต่อเนื่อง ตรงกับ "exhaustion from too much pressure"

questions.question: Why does the writer compare e-sports to gymnastics or swimming?
choices: Because all three sports are played entirely inside the water.
Because all three demand hard training and end early.
Because all three are watched by small audiences.
Because all three were invented in the same year.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — บทเปรียบเทียบเพราะทั้งสามต้องฝึกหนักมากและอาชีพสั้น (เลิกแข่งตั้งแต่อายุยังน้อย) ไม่ใช่ชีวิตสบายอย่างที่แฟนๆ คิด

questions.question: In paragraph 4, "a point that teachers and parents increasingly recognise" refers to the idea that:
choices: games can lead to careers beyond playing
all students should become professional players
homework matters more than any hobby
video production is harder than coaching
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — ประโยคก่อนหน้าบอกว่าคนที่รักเกมแต่ไม่ได้มีปฏิกิริยาระดับโลก ยังสร้างอาชีพรอบๆ วงการได้ (อีเวนต์ การตลาด โค้ช ฯลฯ) นี่คือ point ที่ครูและผู้ปกครองเริ่มยอมรับ

questions.question: What is the writer's overall attitude toward gaming?
choices: Balanced — neither worshipping nor dismissing it.
Hostile — gaming destroys all young people's lives.
Worshipful — everyone should become a gamer.
Indifferent — gaming is too boring to discuss.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — ย่อหน้าสุดท้ายสรุปชัดว่า "neither to worship gaming nor to dismiss it, but to understand it" จึงเป็นท่าทีสมดุล

questions.question: How is the passage mainly organized?
choices: It shows the industry's scale, its hardships, its wider jobs, then a lesson.
It ranks the world's best players from first to last.
It follows a single championship match from its beginning to the very end.
It lists video games in the order they were released.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — บทไล่จากขนาดอุตสาหกรรม → ความยากของอาชีพ → โอกาสงานรอบวงการ → บทเรียนปิดท้าย จึงเป็นโครงนี้

passages.title: Should University Education Be Free?
passages.passage:
Few questions divide opinion about education as sharply as this one: should university be free for every qualified student? In some countries, students pay nothing and even receive support for living costs; in others, a degree leaves graduates with debts that follow them for decades. Both systems produce doctors, engineers, and teachers — yet they reflect very different beliefs about what education is for, and who should pay for it.

The case for free university rests on two main arguments. The first is fairness. Talent is spread across all levels of society, but money is not. When degrees carry heavy fees, bright students from poor families may never apply, or may abandon their studies to work. A nation that allows ability to be wasted in this way, supporters argue, harms not only those individuals but itself. The second argument is that education benefits everyone, not just the graduate. Doctors heal communities; engineers build infrastructure; teachers raise the next generation. Because society as a whole gains, society as a whole should share the cost, just as it does for schools and hospitals.

Opponents respond with equally serious points. Free university is not actually free: the money must come from taxes, including taxes paid by workers who never attended university themselves. Is it fair, they ask, for a factory worker to fund the degree of a future lawyer who will earn far more? There are also practical concerns. When university costs nothing, more students may enrol without clear purpose, lecture halls overflow, and governments facing rising bills may quietly reduce quality. Some economists argue that graduates, who gain higher lifetime earnings, should reasonably repay part of the investment once their income allows.

Between these positions lie compromise systems that many countries now explore. Income-based repayment asks graduates to pay only when they earn above a certain level, protecting those whose careers pay little. Generous scholarships can open doors for poorer students without making degrees free for the wealthy. Free places in fields with severe shortages — nursing, teaching, rural medicine — can direct public money where society needs it most.

In the end, the debate is less about money than about values. A purely private model treats education as a personal purchase; a fully public one treats it as a shared good. Most societies, in practice, are searching for the point between the two — where no talented student is turned away by poverty, and no taxpayer is asked to fund privilege.

questions.question: What is the central question of the essay?
choices: Whether university education should be free for qualified students.
Whether doctors truly earn far more money than engineers usually do.
Whether school exams are harder than university exams.
Whether students should live at home during university.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — บทถกประเด็น "มหาวิทยาลัยควรเรียนฟรีไหม" โดยเสนอทั้งสองฝ่ายและทางประนีประนอม ตัวเลือกอื่นไม่ได้กล่าวถึง

questions.question: What is the fairness argument for free university?
choices: All universities should accept every single applicant.
Rich students deserve to pay less than poor students.
Heavy fees may shut out bright students from poor families.
Exams are deeply unfair to all students who choose to study science.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — ย่อหน้าที่ 2 บอกว่าพรสวรรค์กระจายทุกชนชั้นแต่เงินไม่ ค่าเรียนแพงอาจทำให้เด็กเก่งจากครอบครัวยากจนไม่ได้เรียน

questions.question: What objection do opponents raise about funding?
choices: Universities refuse to accept government money.
Workers without degrees would help fund future high earners.
All government taxes would simply disappear if university were free.
Banks would stop lending money to all students.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ฝ่ายค้านถามว่า ยุติธรรมหรือที่คนงานโรงงานซึ่งไม่ได้เรียนมหาวิทยาลัย ต้องจ่ายภาษีอุดหนุนปริญญาของว่าที่ทนายที่จะรายได้สูงกว่า

questions.question: In paragraph 4, "income-based repayment" means graduates:
choices: pay double if they choose to work abroad
pay all fees before starting their studies
never pay anything back to anyone at all
pay back only when they earn above a set level
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — บทอธิบายว่าระบบนี้ให้บัณฑิตจ่ายคืนเฉพาะเมื่อรายได้เกินระดับที่กำหนด เพื่อปกป้องคนที่อาชีพรายได้น้อย

questions.question: What can be inferred about free places in nursing and rural medicine?
choices: They are offered because no one wants those jobs.
They aim public money at fields society needs most.
They are by far the most expensive degrees for any country to provide.
They are available only to the wealthiest students.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — บทบอกว่าให้ที่เรียนฟรีในสาขาที่ขาดแคลนรุนแรง เพื่อนำเงินสาธารณะไปยังจุดที่สังคมต้องการที่สุด

questions.question: In paragraph 5, "the point between the two" refers to a balance between:
choices: doctors' salaries and engineers' salaries
school education and university education
private purchase and shared public good
city universities and rural universities
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — ประโยคก่อนหน้าเทียบสองโมเดล: การศึกษาเป็นสินค้าส่วนตัว (private) กับเป็นสมบัติร่วมของสังคม (public) จุดกึ่งกลางจึงอยู่ระหว่างสองสิ่งนี้

questions.question: How would you best describe the writer's approach?
choices: Careless, mixing up the two opposing sides.
One-sided, openly mocking every single person who dares to disagree.
Emotional, relying on personal stories only.
Even-handed, presenting both sides and compromises.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — ผู้เขียนเสนอข้อโต้แย้งของทั้งสองฝ่ายอย่างจริงจังเท่าๆ กัน แล้วชี้ระบบประนีประนอม จึงเป็นแนวทางเป็นกลาง

questions.question: How is the essay mainly organized?
choices: A long list of famous universities ranked by their yearly fees.
Question → case for → case against → compromises → values.
A story about one student's journey through debt.
A timeline of education laws in one country.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — บทเริ่มด้วยคำถาม → เหตุผลฝ่ายสนับสนุน → ฝ่ายค้าน → ระบบประนีประนอม → ปิดท้ายด้วยประเด็นเชิงคุณค่า จึงเป็นโครงนี้

questions.question: What does the writer suggest most societies are trying to achieve?
choices: A system where only the rich attend university.
A system where every degree is completely free.
A system where talent is not blocked by poverty.
A system with no universities at all.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — ประโยคสุดท้ายบอกว่าสังคมส่วนใหญ่กำลังหาจุดที่ "ไม่มีเด็กเก่งถูกปิดกั้นเพราะความจน และไม่มีผู้เสียภาษีต้องอุดหนุนอภิสิทธิ์"

passages.title: The Science of Laughter
passages.passage:
Laughter is one of the most familiar sounds in the world, yet also one of the strangest. We laugh at jokes, at funny faces, and sometimes at nothing in particular. We laugh when we are happy, but also when we are nervous or embarrassed. Despite how ordinary it seems, scientists have long been puzzled by a simple question: why do human beings laugh at all, and what purpose could such a curious behaviour possibly serve?

One of the first surprises is that laughter is far older than language and far more social than we tend to assume. Studies show that we are up to thirty times more likely to laugh when we are with other people than when we are alone. Most laughter, in fact, has little to do with jokes. It happens during ordinary conversation, marking moments of agreement, friendliness, and shared understanding. In this sense, laughter is less a response to humour than a kind of social glue that quietly binds people together.

Laughter also appears remarkably early in life. Babies begin to laugh at around three or four months old, long before they can speak or understand a single joke. This suggests that the ability to laugh is built into us from birth, rather than something we must be carefully taught. Researchers have even recorded laughter-like sounds in other animals, such as chimpanzees and rats, when they play or are gently tickled, hinting that the roots of laughter reach deep into our evolutionary past.

What happens inside the body during laughter is equally interesting. A good laugh increases our breathing, relaxes our muscles, and triggers the release of chemicals in the brain that are associated with pleasure and with a reduced sense of pain. Some studies suggest that laughter can lower stress and may even strengthen the immune system, although scientists are careful not to exaggerate these effects or promise more than the evidence allows. What is clear is that laughter feels good, and that this good feeling gently encourages us to seek out the company of others.

Perhaps the most important role of laughter is communication. A shared laugh can ease tension, signal that we mean no harm, and turn nervous strangers into comfortable friends. Yet laughter can also wound, as when a group laughs at someone rather than with them. Like language itself, laughter carries meaning, and that meaning depends entirely on the situation in which it occurs.

Far from being a meaningless reflex, then, laughter is a powerful and ancient form of human connection. It reminds us that we are social creatures, shaped by millions of years of living in groups. The next time you laugh with a friend, it is worth remembering that you are taking part in one of the oldest conversations our species has ever known.

questions.question: What is the passage mainly about?
choices: A list of the funniest jokes ever told.
Why humans laugh and what laughter does.
How to become a professional comedian.
The reasons some people never laugh.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ทั้งบทสำรวจว่าทำไมมนุษย์ถึงหัวเราะและการหัวเราะมีบทบาทอะไร ส่วน A/C/D ไม่ใช่ใจความหลัก

questions.question: How does the amount we laugh change when others are present?
choices: We laugh far less when other people are near.
We laugh about the same whether alone or not.
We are far more likely to laugh around others.
We laugh only when we are completely alone.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — ย่อหน้าที่ 2 บอกว่าเรามีโอกาสหัวเราะมากกว่าถึง 30 เท่าเมื่ออยู่กับคนอื่นเทียบกับอยู่คนเดียว

questions.question: When do babies begin to laugh, according to the passage?
choices: Only after they can understand simple jokes.
Several years after they begin going to school.
Once they are able to speak in full sentences.
At around three or four months of age.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — ย่อหน้าที่ 3 ระบุว่าทารกเริ่มหัวเราะราวอายุ 3–4 เดือน ก่อนพูดหรือเข้าใจมุกตลก

questions.question: In paragraph 2, the phrase "social glue" is closest in meaning to:
choices: a sticky substance used in classrooms
something that binds people together
a rule that forces everyone to laugh
a kind of serious and rare illness
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — "social glue" เป็นคำเปรียบเทียบ หมายถึงสิ่งที่เชื่อมผู้คนเข้าด้วยกัน ตรงกับ "something that binds people together"

questions.question: What can be inferred from laughter-like sounds in chimpanzees and rats?
choices: Animals laugh at exactly the same jokes we do.
Only human beings are truly able to laugh.
Laughter has very old roots in our evolution.
Animals laugh far more often than humans do.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — ย่อหน้าที่ 3 บอกว่าพบเสียงคล้ายหัวเราะในชิมแปนซีและหนู จึงอนุมานได้ว่ารากของการหัวเราะเก่าแก่ย้อนไปในวิวัฒนาการ

questions.question: What happens in the body during laughter?
choices: It releases brain chemicals linked to pleasure.
It permanently cures every kind of disease.
It makes our breathing slow down completely.
It tightens all of the muscles in the body.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — ย่อหน้าที่ 4 บอกว่าการหัวเราะกระตุ้นการหลั่งสารในสมองที่เชื่อมกับความสุขและลดความเจ็บปวด ตัวเลือกอื่นขัดกับบท

questions.question: What is the writer's main purpose?
choices: To warn readers that laughing can be dangerous.
To prove jokes are the only cause of laughter.
To teach readers how to tell much better jokes.
To explain why we laugh and its social role.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — บทมุ่งอธิบายว่าทำไมเราหัวเราะและบทบาททางสังคมของมัน ไม่ได้เตือนภัย พิสูจน์ว่ามุกคือสาเหตุเดียว หรือสอนเล่ามุก

questions.question: How is the passage mainly organized?
choices: It poses a question, then explores several answers.
It lists jokes in order from worst to best.
It tells one comedian's life story in order.
It gives step-by-step instructions for laughing.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — บทตั้งคำถาม (ทำไมเราหัวเราะ) แล้วไล่สำรวจคำตอบหลายด้าน (สังคม วัย ร่างกาย การสื่อสาร) จึงเป็นโครงคำถาม–คำตอบ

passages.title: Why Reading Fiction Makes Us Kinder
passages.passage:
We usually think of reading novels as entertainment, or perhaps as a pleasant way to escape from daily life. Few people imagine that losing yourself in a story could actually change the way you treat the people around you. Yet a growing body of research suggests something quietly remarkable: reading fiction may make us more empathetic — better able to understand and share the feelings of others.

The idea rests on what happens in our minds as we read. When we follow a character through a novel, we do far more than simply track a series of events. We enter that character's thoughts, feel their fears, and see the world through their eyes. To make sense of the story at all, our brains must imagine what it is like to be someone else — a person of a different age, background, or culture from our own. In effect, every novel is an exercise in stepping carefully into another mind.

Psychologists have tested this idea in a number of experiments. In several studies, people who had just read literary fiction performed better on tests measuring their ability to recognise other people's emotions, compared with those who read non-fiction or nothing at all. The effect appeared strongest for serious, character-driven stories, which force readers to work harder to understand complex and realistic people. Light, fast-moving entertainment, by contrast, seemed to have a noticeably weaker effect.

Why should any of this matter beyond the page? Empathy is one of the foundations of kindness and cooperation. People who can imagine how others feel are generally more likely to help, to forgive, and to treat strangers fairly. If fiction strengthens this ability, then reading is not merely a private pleasure but a quiet form of training for living well with other people. Some researchers even suggest that stories may have helped early human societies hold together, by allowing people to share experiences they had never actually lived through themselves.

It would be wrong, of course, to claim that reading novels alone can turn someone into a good person, or that those who dislike fiction must be unkind. Empathy grows from many different sources, and the research in this area is still developing. The effects measured in laboratory experiments are often small and short-lived, and careful scientists rightly urge us not to overstate them.

Still, the basic insight is both simple and hopeful. Every time we open a novel and come to care about a character who does not even exist, we are quietly practising one of the most important human skills of all: the ability to imagine a life that is not our own. In a world that often feels divided, that ability may be far more valuable than we tend to think.

questions.question: What is the main idea of the passage?
choices: Reading novels is mainly a waste of valuable time.
Non-fiction books are always better than novels.
Reading fiction may make us more empathetic.
Only children benefit from made-up stories.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — แก่นของบทคือ การอ่านนิยายอาจทำให้เราเห็นอกเห็นใจผู้อื่นมากขึ้น ส่วน A/B/D ขัดกับเนื้อหา

questions.question: According to the passage, what must our brains do as we follow a character?
choices: Imagine what it is like to be someone else.
Memorise every single word on the page.
Ignore the feelings of the main character.
Compare the book with a television show.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — ย่อหน้าที่ 2 บอกว่าเพื่อเข้าใจเรื่อง สมองต้องจินตนาการว่าการเป็นคนอื่นเป็นอย่างไร

questions.question: What did experiments find about people who read literary fiction?
choices: They became much worse at understanding others.
They did better on tests of reading emotions.
They forgot the whole story almost immediately.
They refused to read any non-fiction afterwards.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ย่อหน้าที่ 3 ระบุว่าคนที่เพิ่งอ่านวรรณกรรมทำแบบทดสอบอ่านอารมณ์ผู้อื่นได้ดีกว่ากลุ่มอื่น

questions.question: In paragraph 4, the word "foundations" is closest in meaning to:
choices: tall walls built around a house
small mistakes or careless errors
enemies that cause serious harm
bases on which something is built
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — "foundations of kindness" หมายถึงรากฐานที่ความเมตตาตั้งอยู่ ตรงกับ "bases on which something is built"

questions.question: Which type of story had the strongest effect on empathy?
choices: Light stories with fast and simple plots.
Books that contain no characters at all.
Serious, character-driven literary fiction.
Short articles about recent news events.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — ย่อหน้าที่ 3 บอกว่าผลแรงที่สุดในเรื่องจริงจังที่ขับเคลื่อนด้วยตัวละคร ซึ่งบังคับให้ผู้อ่านเข้าใจคนที่ซับซ้อน

questions.question: How would you best describe the writer's attitude?
choices: Certain that novels can solve every problem.
Hopeful but careful about the research.
Angry at people who enjoy reading fiction.
Completely uninterested in the whole topic.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ผู้เขียนมองในแง่ดีแต่ก็เตือนว่าผลการวิจัยมักเล็กและสั้น และไม่ควรกล่าวเกินจริง จึงเป็นท่าทีมีความหวังแต่ระมัดระวัง

questions.question: In the final paragraph, "that ability" refers to the ability to:
choices: read very quickly without ever stopping
write long and complicated novels
remember every plot in great detail
imagine a life that is not our own
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — ประโยคก่อนหน้าพูดถึง "the ability to imagine a life that is not our own" ดังนั้น that ability จึงหมายถึงความสามารถนี้

questions.question: How is the passage mainly organized?
choices: It presents an idea, the evidence, and careful limits.
It lists famous novels from oldest to newest.
It tells the story of one writer's whole life.
It gives instructions for writing a good novel.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — บทเสนอแนวคิด (อ่านนิยาย→เห็นใจ) → หลักฐานจากงานวิจัย → ข้อจำกัดที่ต้องระวัง จึงเป็นโครงนี้

passages.title: Should Zoos Still Exist?
passages.passage:
Few institutions divide animal lovers as sharply as the zoo. To some, zoos are places of wonder, where children meet creatures they would otherwise never see and where rare species are protected from extinction. To others, they are little more than prisons, where wild animals live out their lives in cages for the entertainment of human visitors. As our understanding of animals deepens, the question only grows louder: in the modern world, should zoos still exist at all?

Defenders of zoos point first to conservation. A number of species alive today survive only because zoos have bred them in captivity, sometimes returning them to the wild once their natural habitats are safe again. Zoos also fund scientific research and shelter animals whose forests and grasslands are being destroyed. Beyond this, supporters argue that zoos educate. A child who watches a real elephant, they say, is far more likely to grow up caring about its survival than one who has only ever seen a photograph. In a time of environmental crisis, building that sense of connection may matter enormously.

Critics, however, question both of these claims. Only a small fraction of zoo animals belong to endangered species, and fewer still are ever genuinely released into the wild. Many of the most popular attractions, such as big cats, bears, and primates, breed poorly in captivity and may suffer in enclosures that cannot begin to match the space and complexity of their natural homes. As for education, critics ask what lesson is truly learned by watching a bored tiger pace behind a sheet of glass. Perhaps, they suggest, it quietly teaches children that it is acceptable to keep wild animals locked up purely for our amusement.

The most thoughtful arguments tend to focus not on whether zoos should exist, but on what kind of zoos we are prepared to accept. A cramped roadside attraction that exists only to make money is a very different thing from a modern conservation centre with spacious enclosures, expert care, and serious breeding programmes. The single word "zoo" hides an enormous range of quality, and judging them all together may lead both sides to ask the wrong question entirely.

Perhaps the fairest conclusion is that zoos can be justified only when they place the welfare of their animals first and contribute seriously to conservation and education. A zoo that genuinely meets these standards may do real and lasting good; one that does not is very hard to defend. As public attitudes continue to change, the pressure on zoos to prove their worth, or else close their gates, is likely only to grow.

In the end, the debate over zoos forces us to weigh our curiosity, and even our affection, against the freedom of other living creatures. How we choose to answer says a great deal not only about the animals in our care, but about ourselves.

questions.question: What is the central question of the passage?
choices: Whether children enjoy visiting modern zoos.
Whether zoos should still exist today.
Whether elephants are smarter than tigers.
Whether photographs teach better than animals.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ทั้งบทถกประเด็น "ในโลกสมัยใหม่ สวนสัตว์ควรมีอยู่ต่อไปไหม" ตัวเลือกอื่นเป็นรายละเอียดย่อย

questions.question: What do defenders of zoos point to first?
choices: The money that zoos make from tickets.
The fun that visitors have at the zoo.
The role of zoos in conservation.
The number of cafés inside the zoo.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — ย่อหน้าที่ 2 บอกว่าผู้สนับสนุนชี้ไปที่ "conservation" ก่อนเป็นอันดับแรก (เพาะพันธุ์สัตว์ใกล้สูญพันธุ์ คืนสู่ธรรมชาติ)

questions.question: What point do critics make about endangered animals in zoos?
choices: Most zoo animals are endangered species.
All zoo animals are released to the wild.
Zoos always copy animals' natural homes.
Only a small fraction are endangered species.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — ย่อหน้าที่ 3 ระบุว่าสัตว์ในสวนสัตว์เป็นชนิดใกล้สูญพันธุ์เพียงส่วนน้อย และยิ่งน้อยกว่าที่ถูกปล่อยคืนธรรมชาติจริง

questions.question: In paragraph 3, the word "enclosures" is closest in meaning to:
choices: spaces where animals are kept
doctors who treat sick animals

questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — "enclosures" หมายถึงพื้นที่/คอกที่ใช้ขังสัตว์ ตรงกับ "spaces where animals are kept"

questions.question: What does the writer suggest about the single word "zoo"?
choices: All zoos are exactly the same in quality.
Zoos are always cruel places for animals.
It hides a very wide range of quality.
Only large zoos should be allowed to open.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — ย่อหน้าที่ 4 บอกว่าคำว่า "zoo" ซ่อนคุณภาพที่หลากหลายมาก ตั้งแต่แผงข้างทางจนถึงศูนย์อนุรักษ์สมัยใหม่

questions.question: According to the conclusion, when can zoos be justified?
choices: When they make the largest possible profit.
When they put animal welfare and conservation first.
When they keep as many animals as they can.
When they charge visitors the lowest prices.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ย่อหน้าที่ 5 ระบุว่าสวนสัตว์มีเหตุผลรองรับได้ก็ต่อเมื่อเอาสวัสดิภาพสัตว์เป็นอันดับแรกและช่วยงานอนุรักษ์/ให้ความรู้อย่างจริงจัง

questions.question: In paragraph 5, "these standards" refers to:
choices: the rules that visitors follow when buying a zoo ticket
the laws about building new cities
the prices charged at the entrance gate
putting welfare, conservation and education first
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — ประโยคก่อนหน้าพูดถึงการเอาสวัสดิภาพสัตว์เป็นอันดับแรก พร้อมงานอนุรักษ์และการให้ความรู้ ดังนั้น these standards จึงหมายถึงมาตรฐานเหล่านี้

questions.question: How would you best describe the writer's overall position?
choices: Balanced — zoos are justified only under strict conditions.
Certain that every zoo should close immediately.
Completely sure that all of the zoos are entirely harmless.
Uninterested in the welfare of any animals.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — ผู้เขียนเสนอทั้งสองฝ่ายแล้วสรุปว่าสวนสัตว์ชอบธรรมได้เฉพาะเมื่อทำตามเงื่อนไขที่เข้มงวด จึงเป็นจุดยืนสมดุล

questions.question: How is the passage mainly organized?
choices: It tells the life story of one zoo animal.
It gives arguments for, against, then a balanced view.
It lists the world's zoos by their size.
It explains how to build a new zoo step by step.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — บทเสนอฝ่ายสนับสนุน → ฝ่ายค้าน → แล้วสรุปมุมมองสมดุล (ขึ้นกับชนิดของสวนสัตว์และเงื่อนไข)

passages.title: How Migrating Birds Find Their Way
passages.passage:
Every year, billions of birds undertake one of the most astonishing journeys in all of nature. Some travel thousands of kilometres, from the cold north to the warm south and back again, crossing wide oceans, empty deserts, and towering mountains. What amazes scientists most is not the sheer distance, but the accuracy. A small bird may return to the very same garden, year after year, having flown halfway around the world and back. The question is simple, but the answer is not: how on earth do they find their way?

Part of the answer lies in the sun and the stars. Many birds use the position of the sun during the day as a kind of compass, carefully adjusting for the way it moves across the sky from morning to evening. At night, some species navigate by the stars, learning the patterns of the night sky while they are still young in the nest. Like the early human sailors who crossed unknown seas, these birds read the heavens in order to hold their course.

Even more remarkable is the birds' ability to sense the Earth's magnetic field. Our planet behaves like an enormous magnet, and its invisible field points roughly towards the poles. Many birds appear able to detect this field and use it as a built-in compass, allowing them to find the right direction even on cloudy nights when neither the sun nor the stars can be seen. Exactly how they manage this is still not fully understood, but it may involve tiny magnetic particles in their bodies, or even chemical reactions in their eyes that respond to magnetism.

Birds also rely on landmarks and memory. Older, experienced birds appear to remember coastlines, rivers, and mountain ranges, using them as signposts along familiar routes. Smell may play a part too, with some seabirds recognising the particular scent of their home island carried far out on the wind. In other words, birds do not depend on any single method. Instead, they combine several different systems, quietly switching between them as the conditions around them change.

This flexibility helps to explain how migration can be so reliable, yet it also leaves birds surprisingly vulnerable. Bright city lights can confuse birds that are navigating by the stars, and tall buildings and towers often lie directly across their ancient paths. As human beings continue to reshape the planet, scientists increasingly worry that we may be disrupting journeys that have been completed successfully for millions of years.

The navigation of migrating birds remains one of the great wonders of the natural world. That a creature weighing less than a smartphone can cross entire continents and return home, guided by the sun, the stars, and the very magnetism of the Earth, is a powerful reminder of how much skill and intelligence lie hidden in the living world around us.

questions.question: What is the passage mainly about?
choices: Why some birds never leave their gardens.
How migrating birds find their way.
Which birds are the largest in the world.
How sailors used stars to cross the sea.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ทั้งบทอธิบายว่านกอพยพหาเส้นทางได้อย่างไร (ดวงอาทิตย์ ดาว สนามแม่เหล็ก จุดสังเกต) ตัวเลือกอื่นเป็นรายละเอียดย่อย

questions.question: How do many birds use the sun?
choices: As a compass, adjusting for its movement.
To keep their feathers warm and dry.
To find food hidden under the ground.
To frighten away larger hunting birds.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — ย่อหน้าที่ 2 บอกว่านกใช้ตำแหน่งดวงอาทิตย์เป็นเข็มทิศ โดยปรับตามการเคลื่อนที่ของมันบนท้องฟ้า

questions.question: Besides the sun and stars, what can many birds sense?
choices: The colour of distant city lights.
The taste of the ocean water below.
The Earth's invisible magnetic field.
The sound of other birds far away.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — ย่อหน้าที่ 3 ระบุว่านกหลายชนิดรับรู้สนามแม่เหล็กที่มองไม่เห็นของโลกและใช้เป็นเข็มทิศในตัว

questions.question: In paragraph 4, the word "signposts" is closest in meaning to:
choices: birds that lead all the others
maps that are printed on paper
warnings about coming bad weather
markers that help guide the route
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — "signposts" ในที่นี้คือจุดสังเกต (ชายฝั่ง แม่น้ำ ภูเขา) ที่ช่วยนำทาง ตรงกับ "markers that help guide the route"

questions.question: Why does the writer say birds do not depend on a single method?
choices: Because they cannot see in the dark at all.
Because combining systems makes them reliable.
Because the sun is never visible to them.
Because memory is the only thing they use.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ย่อหน้าที่ 4–5 บอกว่านกรวมหลายระบบและสลับใช้ตามสภาพ ความยืดหยุ่นนี้ทำให้การอพยพแม่นยำเชื่อถือได้

questions.question: How can bright city lights affect migrating birds?
choices: They help birds fly much faster at night.
They keep birds warm during cold weather.
They can confuse birds navigating by stars.
They guide birds safely around tall towers.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — ย่อหน้าที่ 5 ระบุว่าแสงไฟเมืองสว่างจ้าทำให้นกที่นำทางด้วยดวงดาวสับสน

questions.question: What is the writer's main purpose?
choices: To explain how migrating birds navigate.
To warn that all birds are disappearing.
To teach readers how to watch wild birds.
To prove birds are smarter than people.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — บทมุ่งอธิบายวิธีที่นกอพยพใช้หาเส้นทาง ไม่ได้เตือนว่านกสูญพันธุ์ สอนดูนก หรือพิสูจน์ว่านกฉลาดกว่าคน

questions.question: How is the passage mainly organized?
choices: It lists bird species from largest to smallest.
It follows one bird's journey hour by hour.
It gives instructions for helping lost birds.
It poses a question, then explains several methods.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — บทตั้งคำถาม (นกหาทางได้อย่างไร) แล้วอธิบายหลายวิธี (ดวงอาทิตย์/ดาว, สนามแม่เหล็ก, จุดสังเกต/ความจำ) จึงเป็นโครงคำถาม–คำอธิบาย

passages.title: How Memory Works, and Why We Forget
passages.passage:
Most of us treat our memory like a video camera, faithfully recording everything that happens so that we can play it back later. The truth is far stranger and more interesting. Memory is not a perfect recording at all, but an active process — one that builds, changes, and sometimes quietly invents. Understanding how it really works can change the way we study, the way we trust our own minds, and even the way we understand who we are.

Scientists often divide memory into stages. Information first enters short-term memory, which holds only a small amount for a few seconds — a phone number we repeat just long enough to dial it. If we pay attention and rehearse, some of this passes into long-term memory, where it may stay for years. But the journey from one to the other is not automatic. Much of what we experience never makes the trip, which is why we forget the vast majority of our daily lives almost as soon as they happen.

Forgetting, however, is not simply a failure. Far from it. If we remembered every face, sound, and detail we ever encountered, our minds would be hopelessly cluttered, unable to find the information that truly matters. Forgetting is the brain's way of clearing space and keeping what is useful. In this sense, a memory that lets go of unimportant things is working exactly as it should.

Perhaps the most surprising discovery is that remembering itself changes our memories. Every time we recall an event, we do not simply read a fixed file; we rebuild it, and small errors can slip in. Over the years, a memory may shift without our noticing, mixing what really happened with what we later imagined or were told. This is one reason why two people can honestly remember the same event in completely different ways, each utterly convinced that their own version is correct.

There is good news for students in all of this. Because memory is strengthened by use, the act of testing yourself — trying to recall information rather than simply re-reading it — is one of the most powerful ways to learn. Spacing study out over several days, instead of cramming everything the night before, also helps memories settle into long-term storage. The brain, it turns out, quietly rewards effort and repetition.

Memory, then, is less like a camera and more like a living story that we tell and retell, editing as we go along. It can fail us, mislead us, and surprise us. Yet it is also the thread that ties our days together and makes us who we are. To understand memory is, in a small but real way, to understand the strange and creative machinery of the human mind.

questions.question: What is the passage mainly about?
choices: A list of tricks for never forgetting anything.
Why video cameras record better than brains.
How memory really works and why we forget.
The reason some people have no memory at all.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — ทั้งบทอธิบายว่าความจำทำงานจริงอย่างไรและทำไมเราจึงลืม ส่วน A/B/D ไม่ใช่ใจความหลัก

questions.question: What does short-term memory hold?
choices: Only a small amount for a few seconds.
Everything we have ever experienced.
Information safely stored for many years.
Only memories from early childhood.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — ย่อหน้าที่ 2 บอกว่าความจำระยะสั้นเก็บข้อมูลปริมาณน้อยได้เพียงไม่กี่วินาที

questions.question: Why is forgetting useful, according to the passage?
choices: It makes us feel sad about the past.
It stops us from learning anything new.
It records every detail we encounter.
It clears space and keeps what is useful.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — ย่อหน้าที่ 3 บอกว่าการลืมช่วยเคลียร์พื้นที่และเก็บสิ่งที่มีประโยชน์ ไม่ให้สมองรกจนหาข้อมูลสำคัญไม่เจอ
