questions.question: Why can two people remember the same event differently?
choices: Because only one of them was really there.
Because memories are stored as perfect files.

Because forgetting never happens to anyone.

questions.question: What helps students learn, according to the passage?
choices: Testing yourself instead of just re-reading.

Avoiding any kind of difficult effort.

questions.question: What is the writer's main purpose?
choices: To warn that memory is completely useless.
To sell a product that improves memory.
To prove cameras are better than minds.

questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — บทมุ่งอธิบายกลไกความจำ การลืม และวิธีเรียนที่ดี ไม่ได้เตือนว่าไร้ประโยชน์ ขายสินค้า หรือพิสูจน์เรื่องกล้อง

questions.question: How is the passage mainly organized?
choices: It tells one person's life story in order.
It corrects a belief, then explains how memory works.
It lists famous scientists from past to present.
It gives steps for building a video camera.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — บทเริ่มด้วยการแก้ความเชื่อ (ความจำ = กล้องวิดีโอ) แล้วอธิบายว่าความจำทำงานจริงอย่างไร จึงเป็นโครงนี้

passages.title: Should Social Media Have an Age Limit?
passages.passage:
For most teenagers today, social media is not an occasional activity but a constant presence, woven into nearly every hour of the day. As concern grows about its effects on young people, governments and parents in several countries have begun to ask a difficult question: should there be a minimum age for using social media, and if so, how on earth should it be enforced?

Those who support an age limit point to mounting evidence that heavy social media use can harm young minds. Studies have linked it to rising rates of anxiety, poor sleep, and low self-esteem, particularly among teenagers who constantly compare themselves with the carefully edited images of others. Children, supporters argue, are simply not ready to handle the pressures of public judgement, addictive design, and exposure to harmful content. Just as we set age limits for driving, drinking, and voting, they say, it is reasonable to protect the young from a technology built to capture their attention for as long as it possibly can.

Opponents, however, raise serious doubts. Banning younger teenagers, they argue, may do more harm than good. Social media is also where young people find friends, explore their interests, and discover communities — including support that some cannot find at home or at school. A strict age limit might cut off the very connections that help vulnerable teenagers the most. There are practical problems too: age limits are notoriously easy to avoid, since a child need only enter a false date of birth. Stricter checks, meanwhile, could force everyone to prove their identity online, raising worrying questions about privacy.

Between these positions lies a more careful debate about responsibility. Critics of a simple ban argue that the real problem lies not with children but with the platforms themselves, which are deliberately designed to be addictive. Rather than punishing young users, they suggest, governments should require companies to make their products safer: removing addictive features for minors, protecting personal data, and limiting harmful recommendations. In this view, the question is less about keeping children out than about forcing companies to behave responsibly.

There is probably no perfect solution. An age limit alone, without education or better-designed platforms, may simply push determined teenagers towards lying about their age or hiding their accounts. Yet doing nothing, while the evidence of harm continues to grow, seems equally unwise. The most sensible path may combine several measures at once: thoughtful age rules, honest education about life online, and real pressure on companies to put young people's wellbeing above their profits.

In the end, the debate over age limits is about far more than technology. It asks how a society should protect its children in a digital world, while still respecting their freedom to grow, to connect, and to learn. It is a question we are only just beginning to answer.

questions.question: What is the central question of the passage?
choices: Whether teenagers should be allowed to drive cars.
Whether social media should have an age limit.
Whether social media companies make enough money.
Whether teenagers sleep more than adults do.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ทั้งบทถกประเด็น "โซเชียลมีเดียควรมีอายุขั้นต่ำไหม และจะบังคับใช้อย่างไร" ตัวเลือกอื่นเป็นรายละเอียดย่อย

questions.question: What do supporters of an age limit point to?
choices: Evidence that social media improves teenage sleep.
Proof that young people dislike social media.
Evidence linking heavy use to anxiety and poor sleep.

questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — ย่อหน้าที่ 2 บอกว่าฝ่ายสนับสนุนชี้หลักฐานที่เชื่อมการใช้หนักกับความวิตกกังวล การนอนแย่ และความนับถือตัวเองต่ำ

questions.question: What concern do opponents raise about a ban?
choices: It might cut off connections that help vulnerable teens.

questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — ย่อหน้าที่ 3 บอกว่าการแบนอาจตัดขาดความสัมพันธ์/ชุมชนที่ช่วยวัยรุ่นเปราะบางที่สุด

choices: in a kind and gentle way

questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — "notoriously easy to avoid" หมายถึงเป็นที่รู้กันทั่วไปว่าหลีกเลี่ยงง่าย ตรงกับ "in a way that is widely known"

questions.question: What do critics of a simple ban believe the real problem is?
choices: That children are naturally badly behaved.
That the platforms are designed to be addictive.
That parents spend too little money on phones.
That schools teach too much about the internet.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ย่อหน้าที่ 4 บอกว่านักวิจารณ์มองว่าปัญหาอยู่ที่แพลตฟอร์มที่ออกแบบให้เสพติด ไม่ใช่ที่ตัวเด็ก

questions.question: What practical problem with age limits is mentioned?
choices: They make phones run much more slowly.
They are extremely expensive for schools.
A child can simply enter a false birth date.

questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — ย่อหน้าที่ 3 บอกว่าการจำกัดอายุเลี่ยงง่ายมาก เพราะเด็กแค่กรอกวันเกิดปลอม

questions.question: In paragraph 4, "their products" refers to the products of:
choices: social media companies

questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — บริบทพูดถึงการบังคับให้ "companies" ทำผลิตภัณฑ์ให้ปลอดภัยขึ้น ดังนั้น their products จึงหมายถึงผลิตภัณฑ์ของบริษัทโซเชียลมีเดีย

questions.question: How would you best describe the writer's overall position?
choices: Certain that all age limits are completely useless.
Sure that banning teenagers solves everything.

questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — ผู้เขียนเสนอทั้งสองฝ่ายแล้วสรุปว่าทางที่ดีคือรวมหลายมาตรการ (กฎอายุ + การศึกษา + กดดันบริษัท) จึงเป็นจุดยืนสมดุล

questions.question: How is the passage mainly organized?
choices: It tells the story of one teenager's day online.
It gives arguments for, against, and a combined path.
It lists social media apps by their size.
It explains how to build a social media app.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — บทเสนอฝ่ายสนับสนุน → ฝ่ายค้าน → มุมเรื่องความรับผิดชอบ → แล้วสรุปแนวทางผสม จึงเป็นโครงนี้

passages.title: The Hidden Life of Trees
passages.passage:
To most of us, a forest looks like a collection of separate trees, each one a silent individual competing with its neighbours for sunlight, water, and space. In recent decades, however, scientists have uncovered a very different picture. Beneath the forest floor, trees are connected, communicate, and even share resources in ways that resemble a single, cooperative community far more than a crowd of strangers.

The key to this hidden world lies underground, in a vast network of fungi. Fine threads of fungus wrap around and link the roots of different trees, forming connections that some scientists have playfully nicknamed the "wood wide web." Through this network, trees can exchange water, sugars, and chemical signals. A large, healthy tree may send sugar through these threads to a smaller, struggling neighbour nearby, while the fungi quietly take a small share of the food in return for their service. It is a partnership in which both sides clearly benefit.

Even more striking is the evidence that trees use this network to communicate. When a tree is attacked by insects, it can release chemical warnings that travel through the air and along the fungal threads, prompting nearby trees to strengthen their own defences before the insects arrive. In this way, a forest behaves a little like a community on alert, quietly passing news of danger from one member to the next.

Scientists have also found that trees can recognise their own offspring. So-called "mother trees" — the oldest and largest in a forest — appear able to direct extra resources towards young trees growing from their seeds, helping them survive in the deep shade of the forest floor. When such a mother tree is dying, it may even release much of its remaining food into the network, as though passing on an inheritance to the next generation.

These discoveries have changed the way many scientists think about forests. A forest, it seems, is not simply a place where trees happen to grow side by side, but a connected system in which cooperation may matter just as much as competition. Damage to one part of this web — through heavy logging, for example — can weaken trees far beyond the area that was actually cut.

It would be easy to romanticise these findings, imagining forests as conscious, caring beings, and scientists are careful to avoid going too far. Trees do not think or feel as we do. Yet the emerging picture is humbling all the same. The quiet woodland we walk through is alive with hidden exchanges and silent connections — a reminder that the natural world is often far more complex, and far more cooperative, than it first appears to the casual eye.

questions.question: What is the passage mainly about?
choices: How to plant a brand-new tree in a garden.
Why forests are full of dangerous insects.
How trees connect, share, and communicate.
Which trees grow the tallest in the world.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — ทั้งบทอธิบายว่าต้นไม้เชื่อมต่อ แบ่งปัน และสื่อสารกันอย่างไร ส่วน A/B/D ไม่ใช่ใจความหลัก

questions.question: What links the roots of different trees together?
choices: A vast underground network of fungi.
Thick ropes tied by forest workers.

questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — ย่อหน้าที่ 2 บอกว่าเส้นใยเชื้อราใต้ดินเชื่อมรากของต้นไม้ต่างๆ เข้าด้วยกัน ("wood wide web")

questions.question: What can a tree do when it is attacked by insects?
choices: Pull its own roots out of the ground.

questions.question: In paragraph 4, the word "offspring" is closest in meaning to:
choices: the leaves of a large tree
a tree's young or its seeds
the insects living in the bark

questions.question: What does the behaviour of "mother trees" suggest?
choices: Old trees take all of the food for themselves.
Trees always compete against their own seeds.
Trees can favour and support their young.
Young trees never survive in the deep shade.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — ย่อหน้าที่ 4 บอกว่าต้นแม่ส่งทรัพยากรพิเศษไปช่วยต้นอ่อนของมัน จึงอนุมานได้ว่าต้นไม้สามารถเอื้อและสนับสนุนลูกหลานได้

questions.question: How can heavy logging affect a forest?
choices: It can weaken trees beyond the area cut.
It makes the remaining trees grow faster.
It has no effect on nearby trees at all.
It improves the health of the whole forest.

questions.question: What is the writer's attitude in the final paragraph?
choices: Certain that trees think exactly like humans.
Angry at scientists who study the forests.
Bored by the whole subject of trees.

questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — ย่อหน้าสุดท้ายทึ่งกับการค้นพบแต่ก็ระวังไม่ทำให้เกินจริง (ย้ำว่าต้นไม้ไม่ได้คิดหรือรู้สึกแบบมนุษย์) จึงเป็นท่าทีทึ่งแต่ระมัดระวัง

questions.question: How is the passage mainly organized?
choices: It lists tree species from tallest to shortest.
It corrects an old view, then reveals hidden connections.
It tells the long life story of one single forest tree.
It gives instructions for cutting down trees.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — บทเริ่มด้วยการแก้มุมมองเดิม (ต้นไม้ต่างคนต่างอยู่) แล้วเผยการเชื่อมต่อที่ซ่อนอยู่ จึงเป็นโครงนี้

passages.title: The Surprising Benefits of Walking
passages.passage:
In an age of intense workouts, fitness trackers, and expensive gym memberships, the humblest form of exercise is often overlooked. Walking requires no special equipment, no membership, and no training at all. It is something most of us do every single day without a second thought. Yet a growing pile of research suggests that this simple activity may be one of the most powerful things we can do for our health, our mood, and even our thinking.

The physical benefits are well established. Regular walking strengthens the heart, helps control weight, lowers blood pressure, and reduces the risk of several serious diseases. Because it is gentle on the body, it can be done by people of almost any age and fitness level, from young children to the elderly. Unlike many forms of intense exercise, walking rarely causes injury, which means people are far more likely to keep doing it for years rather than giving up after only a few weeks.

Less obvious, but just as important, are the effects on the mind. Studies have found that walking, especially outdoors, can reduce stress, ease feelings of anxiety, and lift low moods. A short walk in a park or among trees appears to calm the mind in a way that walking along a busy street does not. For many people, a walk is not only exercise but also a chance to clear their heads and put their worries into perspective.

Walking may even make us think better. Several experiments have shown that people produce more creative ideas while walking than while sitting still, and that the boost often continues for a while after they sit back down again. This may be why so many writers, scientists, and thinkers throughout history have done their best thinking on foot. When we are stuck on a problem, the simple act of moving seems to set our thoughts moving too.

There is also a social and practical side to it. Walking can be shared with friends or family, turning ordinary exercise into a relaxed conversation. Choosing to walk instead of drive for short journeys saves money, reduces pollution, and quietly builds activity into an otherwise busy day. Small choices — taking the stairs, getting off the bus a stop early — add up over time in ways that are surprisingly easy to underestimate.

None of this means that walking can replace every other form of exercise, or cure every illness. But its sheer simplicity is exactly what makes it so valuable. In a world that often tells us that being healthy must be difficult, expensive, or painful, walking offers a quiet reminder that one of the very best things we can do for ourselves is also one of the easiest.

questions.question: What is the passage mainly about?
choices: Why gym memberships are a waste of money.
The many surprising benefits of walking.
How to train for a long-distance race.
Which trees are the best to walk among.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ทั้งบทเล่าประโยชน์ของการเดินที่น่าประหลาดใจ (กาย ใจ ความคิด สังคม) ส่วน A/C/D ไม่ใช่ใจความหลัก

questions.question: What physical benefit of walking is mentioned?
choices: It slowly makes most people grow a great deal taller.
It cures every serious disease completely.
It removes the need to ever sleep.
It strengthens the heart and lowers blood pressure.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — ย่อหน้าที่ 2 บอกว่าการเดินช่วยให้หัวใจแข็งแรงและลดความดันโลหิต ตัวเลือกอื่นเกินจริง

questions.question: Why are people likely to keep walking for years?
choices: Because it rarely causes injury.
Because it requires expensive gear.
Because it must be done very fast.
Because doctors force them to do it.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — ย่อหน้าที่ 2 บอกว่าการเดินแทบไม่ทำให้บาดเจ็บ คนจึงทำต่อได้นานหลายปีแทนที่จะเลิกในไม่กี่สัปดาห์

questions.question: In paragraph 3, the word "lift" (in "lift low moods") is closest in meaning to:
choices: to carry something very heavy
to raise straight into the air
to improve or make better
to steal without asking first
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — "lift low moods" หมายถึงทำให้อารมณ์ที่ตกต่ำดีขึ้น ตรงกับ "to improve or make better"

questions.question: What does the passage suggest about walking outdoors versus on a busy street?
choices: Busy streets are far better for the mind.
Walking among trees calms the mind more.
Walking outdoors has no mental benefits.
Streets and parks affect us in the same way.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ย่อหน้าที่ 3 บอกว่าการเดินในสวนหรือท่ามกลางต้นไม้ช่วยให้จิตใจสงบในแบบที่การเดินบนถนนพลุกพล่านทำไม่ได้

questions.question: What did experiments find about walking and thinking?
choices: People become unable to think while walking.
Walking makes good ideas disappear completely.
People produce more creative ideas while walking.
Sitting still always produces the best ideas.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — ย่อหน้าที่ 4 บอกว่าการทดลองพบว่าคนคิดไอเดียสร้างสรรค์ได้มากกว่าขณะเดินเทียบกับนั่งนิ่ง

questions.question: What is the writer's main purpose?
choices: To encourage readers by showing walking's value.
To warn that walking is secretly dangerous.
To sell a brand of expensive walking shoes.
To prove walking is better than all sports.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — บทมุ่งให้กำลังใจผู้อ่านด้วยการชี้คุณค่าของการเดิน ไม่ได้เตือนภัย ขายรองเท้า หรือพิสูจน์ว่าดีกว่ากีฬาทุกชนิด

questions.question: How is the passage mainly organized?
choices: It tells one walker's journey step by step.
It lists types of shoes from cheap to costly.
It gives instructions for joining a gym.
It describes several different benefits of walking.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — บทไล่อธิบายประโยชน์ของการเดินทีละด้าน (กาย → ใจ → ความคิด → สังคม/การปฏิบัติ) จึงเป็นการบรรยายหลายประโยชน์

passages.title: The Power of Sleep
passages.passage:
We spend about a third of our entire lives asleep, yet for centuries sleep remained one of the great mysteries of science. Why would nature design creatures to spend so many hours unconscious, vulnerable, and unable to find food or defend themselves? For a long time, sleep seemed like nothing more than wasted time. Today, research has revealed the opposite: far from being idle, the sleeping brain is astonishingly busy, and sleep turns out to be one of the most important activities of our entire lives.

One of the clearest discoveries is the link between sleep and memory. While we sleep, the brain replays and reorganises what we learned during the day, moving important information into long-term storage and quietly discarding what does not matter. Students who sleep well after studying remember far more than those who stay awake; in a very real sense, we learn not only while reading, but also while sleeping afterwards. A single late night before an exam can undo hours of hard work.

Sleep also repairs the body. During deep sleep, the body releases hormones that help muscles grow and tissues heal, which is why rest is so important after illness, injury, or hard exercise. The immune system, too, depends on sleep; people who sleep too little catch colds more easily and recover more slowly. Even the brain cleans itself during sleep, washing away waste products that have built up during the day.

Given all this, it is worrying that so many people, especially teenagers, do not get enough sleep. The reasons are familiar: late-night screens, early school start times, homework, and busy lives. Yet the effects are serious. Lack of sleep is linked to poor concentration, low mood, weaker memory, and even long-term health problems. A tired brain struggles to learn, to focus, and to control emotions — exactly the abilities that students most need.

The good news is that better sleep is often within reach. Keeping regular sleeping hours, avoiding bright screens late at night, and making the bedroom dark and quiet can all help a great deal. Short naps can be useful too, as long as they are not taken too late in the day. These small habits cost nothing, yet their effect on health and learning can be truly remarkable. Above all, going to bed and waking up at roughly the same time each day helps the body settle into a steady and healthy rhythm.

Sleep, then, is not a luxury or a sign of laziness, but a basic biological need, as essential as food and water. The hours we spend asleep are not lost; they are an investment, quietly preparing our minds and bodies for everything we hope to do while we are awake. To take sleep seriously is, in the end, to take our own health and learning seriously too.

questions.question: What is the passage mainly about?
choices: How to stay awake for as long as possible.
Why teenagers enjoy using screens at night.
Why sleep is essential for the mind and body.
How many hours each animal sleeps a day.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — ทั้งบทอธิบายว่าการนอนสำคัญต่อสมองและร่างกายอย่างไร ส่วน A/B/D ไม่ใช่ใจความหลัก

questions.question: What does the brain do with what we learned, while we sleep?
choices: It moves important information into long-term storage.
It quietly erases everything that we learned during that day.
It stops working completely until morning.
It keeps only the most unpleasant memories.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — ย่อหน้าที่ 2 บอกว่าระหว่างหลับ สมองย้ายข้อมูลสำคัญไปเก็บในความจำระยะยาวและทิ้งสิ่งที่ไม่จำเป็น

questions.question: How does sleep help repair the body?
choices: It makes the body grow much colder.
It stops the heart from beating fast.
It removes the need to eat any food.
It releases hormones that heal tissues.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — ย่อหน้าที่ 3 ระบุว่าระหว่างหลับลึก ร่างกายหลั่งฮอร์โมนที่ช่วยให้กล้ามเนื้อโตและเนื้อเยื่อซ่อมแซม

questions.question: In paragraph 1, the word "idle" is closest in meaning to:
choices: extremely useful and busy
doing nothing at all
full of very bright light
shared among many people
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — "far from being idle" หมายถึงไม่ได้อยู่เฉยๆ/ไม่ทำอะไร ดังนั้น idle = doing nothing at all

questions.question: What can be inferred about a late night before an exam?
choices: It helps students remember much more.
It has no effect on learning at all.
It can undo hours of earlier studying.
It makes the immune system stronger.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — ย่อหน้าที่ 2 บอกว่าการอดนอนคืนก่อนสอบ "can undo hours of hard work" จึงอนุมานได้ว่าทำให้สิ่งที่อ่านมาเสียเปล่า

questions.question: What is lack of sleep linked to, according to the passage?
choices: Poor concentration and weaker memory.
Faster learning and much better focus.
Stronger control over the emotions.
A far healthier immune system.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — ย่อหน้าที่ 4 ระบุว่าการนอนไม่พอเชื่อมโยงกับสมาธิแย่ อารมณ์ตก และความจำอ่อนลง

questions.question: What is the writer's main purpose?
choices: To warn that sleeping is a waste of time.
To sell a new kind of comfortable bed.
To prove that animals sleep more than humans.
To explain why sleep matters and how to improve it.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — บทมุ่งอธิบายความสำคัญของการนอนและวิธีนอนให้ดีขึ้น ไม่ได้เตือนว่าเสียเวลา ขายเตียง หรือพิสูจน์เรื่องสัตว์

questions.question: How is the passage mainly organized?
choices: It tells the story of one student's single night in order.
It corrects an old view, then explains sleep's roles.
It lists animals by how long they sleep.
It gives steps for building a bedroom.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — บทเริ่มด้วยการแก้ความเชื่อเดิม (นอน = เสียเวลา) แล้วอธิบายบทบาทของการนอน จึงเป็นโครงนี้

passages.title: Should Junk Food Advertising Be Banned?
passages.passage:
Bright colours, catchy songs, free toys, and smiling cartoon characters: the advertising of junk food is designed to be almost impossible to ignore, and children are very often its main target. As rates of childhood obesity rise around the world, governments face a difficult question: should the advertising of unhealthy food and drink be banned, at least when it is aimed directly at the young, who are least able to judge such messages for themselves?

Supporters of a ban argue that children cannot defend themselves against modern advertising. Young children, in particular, may not even understand that an advertisement is trying to sell them something; they simply absorb its cheerful message. Research suggests that children who see more junk food advertising tend to want, and to eat, more of it. If unhealthy diets in childhood lead to serious health problems later in life, supporters say, then protecting children from these advertisements is no different from protecting them from cigarettes or alcohol, which are already heavily restricted.

Opponents reply that banning advertising treats a symptom rather than the disease. Children become unhealthy for many reasons — lack of exercise, oversized portions, family habits, and the low price of unhealthy food — and advertising is only one small part of a much bigger picture. A ban, they argue, may make politicians look busy while actually changing very little. There are concerns about freedom too: companies have a right to advertise legal products, and television channels, sports events, and websites often depend on advertising money in order to survive.

A further difficulty is deciding exactly where to draw the line. What truly counts as "junk food"? Many everyday foods contain sugar, salt, or fat, and rules that seem simple can quickly become confusing. Should an advertisement for a sweet breakfast cereal be banned, while one for a fruit juice that contains just as much sugar is allowed? Critics warn that poorly designed rules may catch the wrong products while completely missing the worst offenders.

Between these views, many experts favour a middle path. Rather than a total ban, some countries restrict junk food advertising only at certain times, such as during children's television, or only in certain places, such as near schools. Combined with better education and clearer food labels, such targeted rules may protect children without unfairly punishing businesses or treating advertising as the single cause of a deeply complex problem.

In the end, the debate is not really about whether advertising influences children — it clearly does — but about how far a society should go in order to protect the young, and who should carry the cost. Like many questions about health and freedom, it has no simple answer, only a series of difficult and necessary choices.

questions.question: What is the central question of the passage?
choices: Whether children enjoy watching cartoons.
Whether junk food advertising should be banned.
Whether fruit juice is healthier than cereal.
Whether TV channels make enough money.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ทั้งบทถกประเด็น "ควรแบนโฆษณาอาหารขยะที่มุ่งเป้าเด็กไหม" ตัวเลือกอื่นเป็นรายละเอียดย่อย

questions.question: Why do supporters say children cannot defend themselves?
choices: Because children never watch any advertisements.
Because children always ignore advertisements.
Because young children may not know an ad sells something.
Because children dislike colourful cartoon characters.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — ย่อหน้าที่ 2 บอกว่าเด็กเล็กอาจไม่เข้าใจว่าโฆษณากำลังขายของ จึงรับสารเข้าไปเฉยๆ

questions.question: What do opponents say a ban treats?
choices: A symptom rather than the real disease.
The single most important cause of obesity.
A problem that does not really exist.
The needs of advertising companies only.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — ย่อหน้าที่ 3 บอกว่าฝ่ายค้านมองว่าการแบนโฆษณาแก้ที่ "อาการ" ไม่ใช่ "โรค" เพราะความอ้วนมีหลายสาเหตุ

questions.question: In paragraph 2, the word "restricted" is closest in meaning to:
choices: completely free to use
sold at very low prices
made much larger in size
limited by strict rules
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — "heavily restricted" หมายถึงถูกจำกัดด้วยกฎเข้มงวด (เช่น บุหรี่/แอลกอฮอล์) ตรงกับ "limited by strict rules"

questions.question: Why does the writer mention sweet cereal and fruit juice?
choices: To prove that fruit juice is always healthy.
To show that defining "junk food" is hard.
To argue that cereal should never be sold.
To explain how juice is made from fruit.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ย่อหน้าที่ 4 ยกตัวอย่างนี้เพื่อแสดงว่าการนิยามว่าอะไรคือ "อาหารขยะ" เป็นเรื่องยากและสับสน

questions.question: What targeted rule do some countries use?
choices: Banning all food advertising forever.
Forcing children to stop watching TV.
Restricting junk food ads during children's TV.
Closing every television channel at night.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — ย่อหน้าที่ 5 บอกว่าบางประเทศจำกัดโฆษณาอาหารขยะเฉพาะบางช่วง เช่น ระหว่างรายการเด็ก หรือบางสถานที่ เช่น ใกล้โรงเรียน

questions.question: In paragraph 5, "such targeted rules" refers to rules that:
choices: limit ads at certain times or places
ban every kind of advertisement
lower the price of all healthy food
force schools to teach cooking
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — ประโยคก่อนหน้าอธิบายการจำกัดโฆษณาเฉพาะบางเวลา/สถานที่ ดังนั้น such targeted rules จึงหมายถึงกฎที่จำกัดตามเวลาหรือสถานที่

questions.question: How would you best describe the writer's overall position?
choices: Certain that a total ban solves everything.
Sure that advertising has no effect at all.
Uninterested in the health of children.
Balanced, favouring targeted rules over a total ban.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — ผู้เขียนเสนอทั้งสองฝ่ายแล้วเอนไปทางกฎแบบเจาะจง (ไม่ใช่แบนทั้งหมด) จึงเป็นจุดยืนสมดุล

questions.question: How is the passage mainly organized?
choices: It tells the story of one child's diet.
It gives arguments for, against, then a middle path.
It lists junk foods from sweetest to saltiest.
It explains how to make an advertisement.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — บทเสนอฝ่ายสนับสนุน → ฝ่ายค้าน → ปัญหาการนิยาม → แล้วสรุปทางสายกลาง จึงเป็นโครงนี้

passages.title: How Volcanoes Shape Our World
passages.passage:
When most people picture a volcano, they imagine destruction: rivers of glowing lava, clouds of ash, and villages buried beneath the rock. Volcanoes can indeed be deadly, and their dangers should never be underestimated. Yet this is only half of the story. Over millions of years, volcanoes have also shaped our planet in ways that make life itself possible, building new land, enriching the soil, and even helping to create the air we breathe.

Volcanoes form where the hot, partly molten rock beneath the Earth's surface forces its way upward. When this molten rock, called magma, finally reaches the surface, it erupts as lava, ash, and gas. As the lava cools and hardens, it builds new rock and, over time, entirely new land. Many of the world's islands, including whole chains in the middle of the oceans, were created by underwater volcanoes that erupted again and again until they slowly rose above the waves.

Far from ruining the land, volcanoes can also make it much richer. Volcanic ash and rock are full of the minerals that plants need, and over thousands of years they break down into some of the most fertile soil on Earth. This is why, despite the obvious dangers, people have always chosen to farm and to live on the slopes of volcanoes. The very mountains that threaten them also feed them, producing rich harvests that ordinary soil simply cannot match. In many parts of the world, the greenest and most productive fields of all lie quietly in the shadow of a volcano.

Volcanoes have shaped the planet on an even grander scale. Scientists believe that, early in the Earth's history, gases released by countless erupting volcanoes helped to form the atmosphere and the oceans. In other words, the air we breathe and the water we drink owe a debt to volcanic activity that took place long before any living thing existed. Without volcanoes, the Earth might be a very different, and far less welcoming, world.

Today, scientists study volcanoes closely, not only to understand the distant past but to protect people in the present. By measuring small earthquakes, escaping gases, and tiny changes in the shape of a mountain, they can sometimes warn communities before an eruption, giving them precious time to escape. This knowledge does not make volcanoes safe, but it can and does save many lives every year, especially in countries where millions of people live close to active mountains.

Volcanoes, then, are neither simply enemies nor simply friends. They are among the most powerful forces shaping our planet, capable of both terrible destruction and astonishing creation. To understand them is to see the Earth as it truly is: a living, restless world, still being shaped and reshaped beneath our very feet.

questions.question: What is the passage mainly about?
choices: How to escape from an erupting volcano.
Why volcanoes are only ever dangerous.
How volcanoes both destroy and create.
Which volcano is the largest on Earth.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — ทั้งบทชี้ว่าภูเขาไฟทั้งทำลายและสร้าง (สร้างแผ่นดิน ดินอุดม อากาศ) ส่วน A/B/D ไม่ใช่ใจความหลัก

questions.question: How were many ocean islands created?
choices: By underwater volcanoes erupting repeatedly.
By rivers carrying rock out to the sea.
By strong winds piling up loose sand.
By people slowly building them by hand.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — ย่อหน้าที่ 2 บอกว่าเกาะกลางมหาสมุทรหลายแห่งเกิดจากภูเขาไฟใต้น้ำที่ปะทุซ้ำๆ จนโผล่พ้นน้ำ

questions.question: Why is volcanic soil good for farming?
choices: It is always cool and very wet.
It contains no minerals at all.
It is normally found only very close to the sea.
It is full of minerals that plants need.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — ย่อหน้าที่ 3 ระบุว่าเถ้าและหินภูเขาไฟเต็มไปด้วยแร่ธาตุที่พืชต้องการ จึงกลายเป็นดินอุดม

questions.question: In paragraph 3, the word "fertile" is closest in meaning to:
choices: dry and full of stones
good for growing plants
dangerous and very hot
empty of all living things
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — "the most fertile soil" หมายถึงดินที่อุดมสมบูรณ์ เหมาะแก่การเพาะปลูก ตรงกับ "good for growing plants"

questions.question: Why do people live on the slopes of volcanoes despite the danger?
choices: Because volcanoes never actually erupt.
Because the law forces them to live there.
Because the rich soil gives good harvests.
Because volcanoes keep away all enemies.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — ย่อหน้าที่ 3 บอกว่าภูเขาที่คุกคามก็ "feed them" ด้วยดินอุดมและผลผลิตดี จึงอนุมานได้ว่าคนอยู่เพราะดินดี

questions.question: What did volcanoes help form early in the Earth's history?
choices: The atmosphere and the oceans.
The first computers and machines.
The light from the distant sun.
The gravity that holds us down.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — ย่อหน้าที่ 4 บอกว่าก๊าซจากภูเขาไฟยุคแรกช่วยก่อตัวบรรยากาศและมหาสมุทร

questions.question: What is the writer's main purpose?
choices: To frighten readers away from all mountains.
To sell guided tours to famous volcanoes.
To prove that volcanoes are completely safe.
To show that volcanoes both destroy and create.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — บทมุ่งแสดงสองด้านของภูเขาไฟ (ทำลายและสร้าง) ไม่ได้ขู่ ขายทัวร์ หรือบอกว่าปลอดภัยสนิท

questions.question: How is the passage mainly organized?
choices: It carefully follows one volcanic eruption minute by minute.
It challenges a one-sided view, then explains both sides.
It lists volcanoes from largest to smallest.
It gives steps for predicting an eruption.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — บทเริ่มด้วยการท้าทายภาพด้านเดียว (ภูเขาไฟ = ทำลายอย่างเดียว) แล้วอธิบายทั้งสองด้าน จึงเป็นโครงนี้

passages.title: The Value of Learning a Second Language
passages.passage:
In a world increasingly connected by travel, trade, and the internet, the ability to speak more than one language has never been more useful. For many people, learning a second language is simply a practical skill, opening doors to jobs and friendships across borders. Yet research suggests that the benefits of speaking another language reach far deeper than communication alone, changing the brain itself in surprising and valuable ways.

The most obvious advantage is, of course, communication. A second language allows us to talk with people we could never otherwise understand, to read books and watch films in their original form, and to travel with confidence in unfamiliar places. In many careers, from business to science to tourism, knowing another language can be the difference between an ordinary opportunity and an extraordinary one. As the world grows steadily smaller, these advantages only become greater.

Less obvious are the effects on the mind. Learning and using a second language appears to strengthen the brain in much the same way that exercise strengthens the body. People who speak two languages are often better at focusing their attention, switching between tasks, and ignoring distractions. The constant mental effort of choosing the right language, while holding back the other, seems to act like a daily workout for the brain.

There may even be benefits later in life. Several studies suggest that people who speak two or more languages show signs of certain age-related memory problems later than those who speak only one. Speaking another language is certainly not a magic cure, and scientists are careful not to promise too much, but it does appear to help keep the ageing brain flexible and active for longer.

Learning a language also opens a window onto another culture. Words and expressions often carry ideas that may not exist in our own language, and understanding them can quietly change the way we see the world. A single word in another language can sometimes capture a feeling that would take a whole sentence to explain in our own. People who learn a second language frequently report that they come to understand not only another culture, but their own more clearly, by seeing it from the outside for the very first time.

None of this means that learning a language is easy; it takes time, patience, and the courage to make plenty of mistakes. But the rewards are unusually rich. A second language is not just a tool for talking to others, but a way of training the mind, opening the heart to other cultures, and seeing the world through more than one pair of eyes. Few other skills offer so much in return for the time and effort that they require of us.

questions.question: What is the passage mainly about?
choices: Why English is the hardest language to learn.
The many benefits of learning a second language.
How to travel cheaply in foreign countries.
Which language has the most speakers.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ทั้งบทเล่าประโยชน์ของการเรียนภาษาที่สอง (สื่อสาร สมอง วัยชรา วัฒนธรรม) ส่วน A/C/D ไม่ใช่ใจความหลัก

questions.question: What is the most obvious advantage of a second language?
choices: It makes people grow taller and stronger.
It removes the need to study at school.
It guarantees everyone a very high salary.
It lets us communicate with more people.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — ย่อหน้าที่ 2 บอกว่าข้อได้เปรียบชัดที่สุดคือการสื่อสาร พูดคุยกับคนที่ปกติเราจะไม่เข้าใจได้

questions.question: How does a second language affect the mind?
choices: It strengthens attention and focus.
It makes the brain work much slower.
It removes all of our memories.
It stops us from learning new things.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — ย่อหน้าที่ 3 บอกว่าผู้พูดสองภาษามักจดจ่อ สลับงาน และตัดสิ่งรบกวนได้ดีกว่า เหมือนสมองได้ออกกำลัง

questions.question: In paragraph 3, the phrase "two languages" describes people who are:
choices: living in a foreign country
unable to speak at all
able to speak two languages
studying at a university
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — บริบทพูดถึง "people who speak two languages" ดังนั้นจึงหมายถึงคนที่พูดได้สองภาษา

questions.question: What can be inferred about speaking two languages and ageing?
choices: It completely cures all memory problems.
It may delay some age-related memory issues.
It makes the brain age much faster.
It has no effect on the ageing brain.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ย่อหน้าที่ 4 บอกว่าผู้พูดหลายภาษาแสดงอาการความจำตามวัยช้ากว่า (แต่ไม่ใช่ยาวิเศษ) จึงอนุมานว่าอาจชะลอปัญหาบางอย่างได้

questions.question: How does learning a language affect our view of culture?
choices: It slowly makes us forget all about our own culture.
It proves all cultures are the same.
It helps us understand our own culture better.
It stops us from travelling abroad.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — ย่อหน้าที่ 5 บอกว่าผู้เรียนมักเข้าใจวัฒนธรรมของตัวเองชัดขึ้น จากการได้มองจากภายนอกเป็นครั้งแรก

questions.question: What is the writer's attitude toward learning a second language?
choices: Positive — the rewards are rich and worth the effort.
Negative — it is a complete waste of time.
Uninterested — the topic does not matter.
Fearful — it is somehow dangerous for the human brain.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — ย่อหน้าสุดท้ายบอกว่ารางวัล "unusually rich" และคุ้มกับความพยายาม จึงเป็นท่าทีบวก

questions.question: How is the passage mainly organized?
choices: It tells one learner's story in time order.
It lists languages from easiest to hardest.
It gives steps for passing a language exam.
It describes several benefits of a second language.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — บทไล่อธิบายประโยชน์ทีละด้าน (สื่อสาร → สมอง → วัยชรา → วัฒนธรรม) จึงเป็นการบรรยายหลายประโยชน์

passages.title: How Habits Are Formed
passages.passage:
Think about the first thing you did this morning. Did you reach for your phone, brush your teeth, or make a cup of coffee — almost without deciding to? Much of daily life runs on habits: actions we repeat so often that they become automatic, requiring little or no conscious thought. Scientists estimate that a large share of our daily behaviour is habitual, which raises an interesting question: how exactly do habits form, and why are they so very hard to change?

Researchers often describe a habit as a simple loop with three parts. First comes a cue, a trigger that tells the brain to start a particular behaviour — the sound of an alarm, the sight of a sofa, a certain time of day. Next comes the routine, the behaviour itself. Finally comes the reward, the good feeling that tells the brain the behaviour was worth repeating. Each time the loop is completed, the link between cue and routine grows a little stronger, until eventually the behaviour happens almost entirely by itself.

This loop explains why habits are so powerful. By turning repeated actions into automatic routines, the brain saves an enormous amount of effort. Imagine having to think carefully about every step of getting dressed or walking to school; we would be exhausted by the middle of the morning. Habits free the mind to focus on more important things. In this sense, habits are not a weakness but a clever and necessary feature of how the brain works.

The same system, however, makes bad habits difficult to break. Once a habit is wired into the brain, the link between cue and routine does not simply disappear, even when we know perfectly well that the habit is harmful. This is exactly why telling someone to "just stop" so rarely works. The cue is still there, and the brain still expects its familiar reward.

Understanding the habit loop, though, points towards far better strategies for change. Rather than trying to erase a habit completely, experts suggest replacing the routine while keeping the same cue and reward. Someone who reaches for sweets when stressed might, for example, keep the cue (stress) and the reward (a short break) but replace the routine with a walk or a glass of water. Changing the surroundings that provide the cues can also help, which is why people often find it easier to build new habits in a new place.

Habits, then, are neither good nor bad in themselves; they are simply the brain's way of being efficient. The real challenge is to shape them with care, building the routines that serve us and gently reshaping those that do not. With patience and a clear understanding of how the loop works, even habits that feel completely fixed can slowly be changed.

questions.question: What is the passage mainly about?
choices: A list of the worst habits people have.
Why coffee is the best morning drink.
How habits form and why they are hard to change.
How to wake up earlier every single morning.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — ทั้งบทอธิบายว่านิสัยก่อตัวอย่างไรและทำไมเปลี่ยนยาก ส่วน A/B/D ไม่ใช่ใจความหลัก

questions.question: What are the three parts of a habit loop?
choices: A cue, a routine, and a reward.
A goal, a plan, and a prize.
A sound, a light, and a smell.
A morning, a noon, and a night.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — ย่อหน้าที่ 2 ระบุว่าวงจรนิสัยมี 3 ส่วน: cue (สัญญาณ) → routine (พฤติกรรม) → reward (รางวัล)

questions.question: Why does the brain turn repeated actions into habits?
choices: To make us feel more tired by midday.
To stop us from learning anything new.
To make every small choice more difficult.
To save effort for more important things.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — ย่อหน้าที่ 3 บอกว่าการเปลี่ยนการกระทำซ้ำๆ ให้อัตโนมัติช่วยสมองประหยัดแรง เพื่อโฟกัสสิ่งสำคัญกว่า

questions.question: In paragraph 2, the word "trigger" is closest in meaning to:
choices: a reward given at the very end
something that starts a behaviour
a kind of slow and hard work
a place that is far from home
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — "a trigger that tells the brain to start a behaviour" ดังนั้น trigger = สิ่งที่กระตุ้นให้เริ่มพฤติกรรม

questions.question: Why does telling someone to "just stop" rarely work?
choices: Because the person enjoys being told what to do.
Because the reward has completely disappeared.
Because the cue is still there expecting a reward.
Because habits are impossible to change at all.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — ย่อหน้าที่ 4 บอกว่าสัญญาณ (cue) ยังอยู่และสมองยังรอรางวัล การสั่งให้ "หยุดเฉยๆ" จึงไม่ค่อยได้ผล

questions.question: What do experts suggest for changing a habit?
choices: Replacing the routine while keeping cue and reward.
Erasing the habit completely in a single day.
Ignoring both the cue and the reward together.
Repeating the bad habit even more often.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — ย่อหน้าที่ 5 แนะนำให้เปลี่ยน "routine" โดยคงสัญญาณและรางวัลเดิมไว้ แทนการลบนิสัยทั้งหมด

questions.question: What is the writer's main purpose?
choices: To warn that all habits are dangerous.
To sell a programme for breaking habits.
To prove the brain is lazy and weak.
To explain how habits work and can change.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — บทมุ่งอธิบายกลไกนิสัยและวิธีเปลี่ยน ไม่ได้เตือนว่าอันตราย ขายโปรแกรม หรือบอกว่าสมองขี้เกียจ

questions.question: How is the passage mainly organized?
choices: It tells one person's whole life story.
It explains the habit loop, then how to use it.
It lists habits from the best to the worst.
It gives steps for making strong coffee.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — บทอธิบายวงจรนิสัย (cue–routine–reward) แล้วชี้ว่าจะใช้ความเข้าใจนี้เปลี่ยนนิสัยอย่างไร จึงเป็นโครงนี้

passages.title: Should We Have a Four-Day Working Week?
passages.passage:
For more than a century, the standard working week in many countries has been five days. Recently, however, a growing number of companies and even governments have begun to test a bold alternative: a four-day week, in which employees work one day less but receive exactly the same pay. Supporters call it the future of work; critics call it unrealistic. So which side is right?

Those in favour point to a surprising body of evidence. In several trials around the world, companies that moved to a four-day week reported that productivity stayed the same, or even rose, despite the shorter hours. Workers, they argue, often waste time during long weeks, and a shorter week pushes everyone to focus and to cut out unnecessary meetings. Just as importantly, employees in these trials reported less stress, better health, and far more time for family, rest, and life outside work. Happier, healthier workers, supporters insist, are also better workers.

There are wider benefits too. With one fewer day of commuting, traffic and pollution both fall. Parents gain more time with their children, and people have more hours to exercise, to volunteer, or simply to rest. Some supporters argue that, as machines and computers do more and more of our work, sharing the remaining work over fewer hours is a natural and fair step forward for society.

Critics, however, raise serious doubts. Not every job, they point out, fits neatly into four days. A hospital, a shop, or a factory cannot simply close for an extra day, and squeezing the same amount of work into fewer days may leave some workers more stressed, not less. In jobs where output depends directly on the hours worked, a shorter week may mean lower production or higher costs. There is also the real danger that "four days" quietly becomes four very long and exhausting days instead.

The truth is that the four-day week may suit some industries far better than others. An office where work can be measured by results is very different from a job where someone must be physically present at every moment. Rather than forcing a single rule on everyone, many experts suggest that the real lesson of these trials is broader: that the link between long hours and good work is far weaker than we once believed, and that there may be many different ways to organise work more wisely.

Whether or not the four-day week ever becomes common, the debate itself is valuable. It forces us to ask a question that is easy to forget in our busy lives: not simply how much we work, but why we work, and what kind of life all of that work is actually supposed to support.

questions.question: What is the central question of the passage?
choices: Whether workers should be paid less money.
Whether we should have a four-day working week.
Whether hospitals should close on weekends.
Whether machines should do all of our work.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ทั้งบทถกประเด็น "ควรมีสัปดาห์ทำงาน 4 วันไหม" ตัวเลือกอื่นเป็นรายละเอียดย่อย

questions.question: What did several trials report about productivity?
choices: It fell sharply in every single company.
It could not be measured at all.
It stayed the same or even rose.
It only mattered in large factories.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — ย่อหน้าที่ 2 บอกว่าบริษัทที่ทดลอง 4 วันรายงานว่าผลิตภาพเท่าเดิมหรือเพิ่มขึ้น แม้ชั่วโมงน้อยลง

questions.question: What wider benefit of a four-day week is mentioned?
choices: Less commuting reduces traffic and pollution.
Workers earn far more money each month.
Companies can hire far fewer people.
Shops are able to stay open much longer.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — ย่อหน้าที่ 3 บอกว่าการเดินทางน้อยลงหนึ่งวันช่วยลดการจราจรและมลพิษ

questions.question: In paragraph 4, "squeezing the same work into fewer days" — "squeezing" is closest in meaning to:
choices: paying for something very costly
relaxing in a comfortable way
removing the work completely
forcing into a smaller space
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — "squeezing the same work into fewer days" หมายถึงอัดงานเท่าเดิมให้ลงในวันที่น้อยลง ตรงกับ "forcing into a smaller space"

questions.question: Why might a four-day week not suit a hospital?
choices: Because hospital workers dislike having free time.
Because a hospital cannot simply close for a day.
Because hospitals already work only four days.
Because patients prefer to visit on weekends.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ย่อหน้าที่ 4 บอกว่าโรงพยาบาล ร้านค้า หรือโรงงานปิดเพิ่มอีกวันไม่ได้ จึงอนุมานได้ว่าสัปดาห์ 4 วันไม่เหมาะกับงานแบบนี้

questions.question: What danger do critics warn about?
choices: That workers will refuse to take any breaks.
That companies will close down forever.
That four days may become very long, exhausting days.
That nobody at all will ever want to do any work again.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — ย่อหน้าที่ 4 เตือนว่า "four days" อาจกลายเป็นสี่วันที่ยาวและเหนื่อยล้าแทน

questions.question: In paragraph 5, "a single rule on everyone" refers to applying the four-day week to:
choices: all industries in exactly the same way
only hospitals and large factories
workers who simply want more pay
machines and computers all alone
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — บริบทบอกว่าไม่ควรบังคับกฎเดียวกับทุกคน เพราะแต่ละงานต่างกัน ดังนั้น a single rule จึงหมายถึงใช้กฎเดียวกับทุกอุตสาหกรรมเหมือนกันหมด

questions.question: How would you best describe the writer's overall position?
choices: Certain that the four-day week suits every job.
Sure that the idea will always fail completely.
Uninterested in how people organise their work.
Balanced, noting it suits some jobs more than others.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — ผู้เขียนเสนอทั้งสองฝ่ายแล้วสรุปว่าสัปดาห์ 4 วันเหมาะกับบางงานมากกว่างานอื่น จึงเป็นจุดยืนสมดุล

questions.question: How is the passage mainly organized?
choices: It tells one worker's day hour by hour.
It gives arguments for, against, then a balanced view.
It lists jobs from the easiest to the hardest.
It explains how to start a brand-new company.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — บทเสนอฝ่ายสนับสนุน → ประโยชน์ในวงกว้าง → ฝ่ายค้าน → แล้วสรุปมุมมองสมดุล จึงเป็นโครงนี้

passages.title: Why Cities Need Parks and Green Spaces
passages.passage:
As more and more of the world's people move into cities, space becomes precious, and every patch of land seems to invite a new building. In the rush to grow, parks and green spaces can look like a luxury — pleasant, perhaps, but not truly essential. A closer look, however, suggests almost the opposite. Far from being a waste of valuable land, green spaces may be one of the things that a healthy city needs most of all.

The most obvious benefits are physical. Trees and plants clean the air, removing some of the pollution produced by traffic and factories, and they provide cool shade in hot weather. In large cities, areas full of concrete and glass can become dangerously hot, while parks stay noticeably cooler, offering relief during the heatwaves that are becoming more common. Green spaces also give people room to walk, run, and play, encouraging the kind of exercise that crowded streets simply do not.

Just as important, though harder to see, are the benefits to the mind. A growing body of research links time spent in nature with lower stress, better mood, and improved concentration. Even a short walk among trees, or a few minutes sitting in a quiet garden, appears to calm the mind in a way that busy streets cannot. For people living in small flats, far from the countryside, a local park may be their only easy escape from the noise and pressure of city life.

Parks also bring people together. They are among the very few places in a city where strangers of every age and background share the same space freely, without having to buy anything at all. Children play, families picnic, old friends meet, and communities slowly form. In a world where people increasingly stay indoors, glued to their screens, these shared green spaces quietly hold a city's social life together.

There are challenges, of course. Parks cost money to build and to maintain, and in crowded cities there is constant pressure to use the land for housing or business instead. Yet many experts argue that this is a false choice. A city packed with buildings but empty of green spaces may save a little money in the short term while becoming hotter, unhealthier, and far lonelier in the long run.

In the end, parks are not a decoration to be added once everything else has been built. They are part of what makes a city livable in the first place. A wise city plans its green spaces just as carefully as it plans its roads and houses. Trees planted along a street, a small playground, or a quiet square can all make a crowded neighbourhood feel far more human, reminding us that people need not only somewhere to live and to work, but also somewhere to breathe.

questions.question: What is the passage mainly about?
choices: Why cities should stop building any houses.
Why cities need parks and green spaces.
How to grow plants inside a small flat.
Which city has the largest public park.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ทั้งบทอธิบายว่าทำไมเมืองจึงต้องการสวนและพื้นที่สีเขียว ส่วน A/C/D ไม่ใช่ใจความหลัก

questions.question: What physical benefit do trees and plants provide?
choices: They make the streets much noisier.
They raise the temperature in summer.
They block all sunlight from the city.
They clean the air and give cool shade.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — ย่อหน้าที่ 2 บอกว่าต้นไม้ฟอกอากาศ (ลดมลพิษ) และให้ร่มเงาเย็น ตัวเลือกอื่นขัดกับบท

questions.question: What does research link with time spent in nature?
choices: Lower stress and a better mood.
Higher stress and a worse mood.
A complete loss of all memory.
A need to stay indoors much more.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — ย่อหน้าที่ 3 ระบุว่างานวิจัยเชื่อมเวลาในธรรมชาติกับความเครียดที่ลดลง อารมณ์ดีขึ้น และสมาธิดีขึ้น

questions.question: In paragraph 2, the word "relief" is closest in meaning to:
choices: a sudden and very loud noise
a very heavy and large payment of money
a comfort from something unpleasant
a tall and very shady building
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — "offering relief during heatwaves" หมายถึงการบรรเทาจากความร้อนที่ไม่สบาย ตรงกับ "a comfort from something unpleasant"

questions.question: Why might a local park matter especially to people in small flats?
choices: Because flats are always close to the countryside.
Because it may be their only easy escape from city noise.
Because parks are the cheapest place to buy food.
Because their flats have no windows of any kind.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ย่อหน้าที่ 3 บอกว่าสำหรับคนในแฟลตเล็กที่ไกลจากชนบท สวนใกล้บ้านอาจเป็นที่หลบหนีจากเสียงและความกดดันของเมืองเพียงแห่งเดียว

questions.question: How do parks bring people together?
choices: By charging everyone an entrance fee.
By keeping all strangers far apart.
By offering a shared space for all ages.
By replacing every school in the city.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — ย่อหน้าที่ 4 บอกว่าสวนเป็นที่ที่คนแปลกหน้าทุกวัยทุกภูมิหลังใช้พื้นที่ร่วมกันได้ฟรี

questions.question: What does the writer argue about parks and land?
choices: Choosing between parks and buildings is a false choice.
Parks should always be removed to build houses.
Parks are only a decoration with no real use.
Cities should never build any new houses at all.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — ย่อหน้าที่ 5 บอกว่าการต้องเลือกระหว่างสวนกับอาคารเป็น "false choice" เพราะเมืองที่ไร้พื้นที่สีเขียวจะร้อน ไม่สุขภาพดี และเหงาในระยะยาว

questions.question: How is the passage mainly organized?
choices: It tells the story of one city park's history.
It lists parks from the largest to the smallest.
It gives steps for planting a public garden.
It describes several benefits, then answers an objection.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — บทไล่ประโยชน์ของสวน (กาย → ใจ → สังคม) แล้วตอบข้อโต้แย้งเรื่องค่าใช้จ่าย/ที่ดิน จึงเป็นโครงนี้

passages.title: The Hidden Journey of the Internet
passages.passage:
When you send a message to a friend on the other side of the world, it can feel almost magical: a single tap on a screen, and your words appear thousands of kilometres away in less than a second. Many people imagine that this information travels through the air, or perhaps through satellites high up in space. The truth is far stranger and more surprising. Almost all of the world's internet traffic travels through thin cables lying on the dark floor of the ocean.

These undersea cables form a vast hidden network connecting the continents. Although they carry an almost unimaginable amount of information, the cables themselves are surprisingly thin — many are no thicker than a garden hose. Inside each one lie delicate threads of glass, thinner than a single human hair, through which information travels as rapid flashes of light. A single cable can carry the internet traffic of entire countries at the very same time.

Laying these cables is an enormous task. Special ships sail slowly across the ocean, carefully unrolling cable onto the seabed, sometimes burying it to protect it near the shore. The cables must cross deep trenches, underwater mountains, and wide plains beneath the water, often at depths where no human being could ever go. Building and repairing this network is difficult, expensive, and largely invisible to the billions of people who quietly depend on it every single day.

Because so much depends on these cables, damage to them can cause serious problems. Ships' anchors, undersea landslides, and even sharks have been known to harm the cables, occasionally cutting off or slowing the internet for whole regions. When this happens, repair ships must find the broken section, lift it carefully from the seabed, and mend it — a slow and delicate operation carried out far out at sea. Finding a single fault among thousands of kilometres of cable on the dark ocean floor is a remarkable feat of engineering in itself.

It is strange to think that something as modern as the internet depends on physical cables resting silently on the ocean floor. We picture our digital world as something that floats in a "cloud," yet it is in fact tied to the planet by very real and fragile threads. Satellites do play a part, especially in remote areas, but they carry only a tiny fraction of the world's traffic compared with these undersea giants.

The next time a message flies instantly across the world, it is worth remembering the hidden journey that it has taken: down into the depths of the ocean, along a thread of glass, and up again on a distant shore. The invisible network beneath the waves is one of the great wonders of the modern age, quietly holding our connected world together.

questions.question: What is the passage mainly about?
choices: How a person can send a quick message to a friend.
Why satellites control the whole internet.
How the internet travels through undersea cables.
Which country has the fastest internet.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — ทั้งบทอธิบายว่าอินเทอร์เน็ตเดินทางผ่านสายเคเบิลใต้ทะเล ส่วน A/B/D ไม่ใช่ใจความหลัก

questions.question: How does information travel inside the cables?
choices: As flashes of light through threads of glass.
As sound carried along thin metal wires.
As water flowing through plastic pipes.
As heat passing through thick rubber.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — ย่อหน้าที่ 2 บอกว่าข้อมูลเดินทางเป็นแสงวาบผ่านเส้นใยแก้วบางกว่าเส้นผม

questions.question: How thick are many of the undersea cables?
choices: As thick as a large tree trunk.
As thick as a tall city building.
As thick as a heavy iron gate.
No thicker than a garden hose.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — ย่อหน้าที่ 2 ระบุว่าสายจำนวนมากหนาไม่เกินสายยางรดน้ำในสวน

questions.question: In paragraph 2, the word "delicate" is closest in meaning to:
choices: extremely heavy and strong
easily damaged or fragile
brightly coloured and loud
very cheap and easy to make
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — "delicate threads of glass" หมายถึงเส้นใยแก้วที่บอบบาง เสียหายง่าย ตรงกับ "easily damaged or fragile"

questions.question: Why can damage to the cables be a serious problem?
choices: Because the cables are easy to replace at once.
Because satellites can carry all the traffic instead.
Because it can cut off the internet for whole regions.
Because sharks always avoid the undersea cables.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — ย่อหน้าที่ 4 บอกว่าความเสียหายอาจตัดหรือทำให้อินเทอร์เน็ตช้าทั้งภูมิภาค จึงเป็นปัญหาร้ายแรง

questions.question: What role do satellites play, according to the passage?
choices: They carry only a tiny fraction of traffic.
They carry almost all of the world's traffic.
They replace every undersea cable completely.
They are used only by undersea repair ships.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — ย่อหน้าที่ 5 บอกว่าดาวเทียมมีบทบาทบ้าง (โดยเฉพาะพื้นที่ห่างไกล) แต่รับส่งข้อมูลเพียงเศษเสี้ยวเทียบกับสายใต้ทะเล

questions.question: What is the writer's main purpose?
choices: To warn that the internet will soon stop working.
To sell a faster kind of internet connection.
To prove that satellites are better than cables.
To reveal the hidden way the internet travels.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — บทมุ่งเผยเส้นทางที่ซ่อนอยู่ของอินเทอร์เน็ต (สายใต้ทะเล) ไม่ได้เตือนว่าจะล่ม ขายเน็ต หรือพิสูจน์เรื่องดาวเทียม

questions.question: How is the passage mainly organized?
choices: It tells one message's story minute by minute.
It corrects a common belief, then explains the real system.
It lists several countries in order of their internet speed.
It gives steps for laying an undersea cable.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — บทเริ่มด้วยการแก้ความเชื่อ (ข้อมูลลอยในอากาศ/ดาวเทียม) แล้วอธิบายระบบจริง (สายใต้ทะเล) จึงเป็นโครงนี้

passages.title: Should Public Transport Be Free?
passages.passage:
In most cities, using a bus or a train means paying a fare. Recently, however, a striking idea has gained attention around the world: what if public transport were completely free, paid for by the government rather than by passengers? A handful of cities have already tried it, and the results have reopened an old debate about how a city should move its people.

Supporters of free transport offer several arguments. The first concerns the environment. If buses and trains cost nothing, they argue, more people will leave their cars at home, reducing traffic, pollution, and the noise that fills modern cities. The second concerns fairness. Transport fares take a far larger share of income from the poor than from the rich, and free transport would help those who most need to reach jobs, schools, and hospitals. Removing fares also speeds up journeys, since buses no longer wait while passengers pay, and it saves the considerable cost of printing tickets and operating machines.

Critics, however, warn that the word "free" is misleading. The money must come from somewhere, usually from taxes, including taxes paid by people who never use public transport at all. They worry that without fare income, transport systems may run short of money for repairs and improvements, leading to older, dirtier, and less reliable services. A free but crumbling bus network, they argue, helps almost no one. Some critics also point out that fares help to manage demand, keeping the busiest services from becoming dangerously overcrowded.

There is also a question of who actually changes their behaviour. Studies of cities that made transport free found that many new passengers were not former drivers, but people who would otherwise have walked or cycled. If that is true, free transport may do less for the environment than its supporters hope, while costing a great deal. Some experts suggest that the same money might achieve far more if it were spent on making services faster and more frequent, rather than simply free.

Between these views lies a more careful position. Free transport may work well in smaller cities, or for certain groups such as students, the elderly, or the poor, without being offered to absolutely everyone. Combined with investment in better routes and cleaner vehicles, such targeted schemes may capture many of the benefits while avoiding the worst of the costs.

In the end, the debate over free transport is about far more than the price of a ticket. It asks what we believe public services are really for, and how a fair society should share both their benefits and their costs. Whether or not buses and trains ever become free, asking the question forces us to think more clearly about the kind of cities we truly want to build.

questions.question: What is the central question of the passage?
choices: Whether cities should build more roads for cars.
Whether public transport should be free.
Whether trains are faster than buses.
Whether students should walk to school.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ทั้งบทถกประเด็น "ขนส่งสาธารณะควรฟรีไหม" ตัวเลือกอื่นเป็นรายละเอียดย่อย

questions.question: What environmental argument do supporters make?
choices: Free transport would make cities much noisier.
Free transport would increase the number of cars.
Free transport could reduce traffic and pollution.
Free transport would use far more electricity.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — ย่อหน้าที่ 2 บอกว่าถ้ารถเมล์/รถไฟฟรี คนจะทิ้งรถไว้บ้านมากขึ้น ลดการจราจรและมลพิษ

questions.question: Why do critics say the word "free" is misleading?
choices: The money must come from taxes instead.
Buses would become much too crowded.
Tickets are actually very cheap to print.
Trains would stop running in the evening.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — ย่อหน้าที่ 3 บอกว่าเงินต้องมาจากที่ไหนสักแห่ง มักคือภาษี รวมถึงภาษีของคนที่ไม่เคยใช้ขนส่งสาธารณะ

questions.question: In paragraph 3, the word "crumbling" is closest in meaning to:
choices: brand new and very clean
extremely fast and modern
crowded with happy passengers
falling apart from neglect
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — "a free but crumbling bus network" หมายถึงเครือข่ายที่ทรุดโทรม/ผุพังจากการขาดการดูแล ตรงกับ "falling apart from neglect"

questions.question: What did studies of free-transport cities find about new passengers?
choices: They were almost all former car drivers.
Many would otherwise have walked or cycled.
They refused to use the free buses at all.
They were all visitors from other countries.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ย่อหน้าที่ 4 บอกว่าผู้โดยสารใหม่จำนวนมากไม่ใช่อดีตคนขับรถ แต่เป็นคนที่ปกติจะเดินหรือปั่นจักรยาน

questions.question: What targeted approach do some experts suggest?
choices: Making transport free for absolutely everyone.
Removing all buses from the city centre.
Free transport for groups like students or the poor.
Charging much higher fares to all passengers.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — ย่อหน้าที่ 5 เสนอว่าฟรีเฉพาะบางกลุ่ม (นักเรียน ผู้สูงอายุ คนจน) อาจได้ประโยชน์โดยไม่ต้องฟรีทุกคน

questions.question: In paragraph 4, "its supporters" refers to the supporters of:
choices: free public transport
building more new roads
higher transport fares
private car ownership
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — บริบทพูดถึงสิ่งที่ฝ่ายหนุน "free transport" หวังไว้ ดังนั้น its supporters จึงหมายถึงผู้สนับสนุนขนส่งสาธารณะแบบฟรี

questions.question: How would you best describe the writer's overall position?
choices: Certain that free transport is always the best idea.
Sure that free transport can never work anywhere.
Uninterested in how cities move their people.
Balanced, suggesting targeted schemes may work best.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — ผู้เขียนเสนอทั้งสองฝ่ายแล้วเอนไปทางมาตรการแบบเจาะจง (targeted) จึงเป็นจุดยืนสมดุล

questions.question: How is the passage mainly organized?
choices: It tells one passenger's daily journey in order.
It gives arguments for, against, then a middle path.
It lists cities by the size of their bus networks.
It explains how to buy a ticket for the train.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — บทเสนอฝ่ายสนับสนุน → ฝ่ายค้าน → แล้วสรุปทางสายกลาง จึงเป็นโครงนี้

passages.title: How Vaccines Protect Us
passages.passage:
For most of human history, infectious diseases were among the greatest dangers people faced. Illnesses that are now rare once killed millions, especially children. Today, many of these diseases have been controlled or even wiped out, and much of the credit goes to one of the most important inventions in the whole history of medicine: the vaccine. Yet many people remain unsure how vaccines actually work, or why they matter so much.

To understand vaccines, it helps first to understand the immune system, the body's natural defence against disease. When harmful germs enter the body, the immune system learns to recognise them and produces special weapons, called antibodies, to destroy them. The first time the body meets a new germ, this process is slow, and the person may become seriously ill before their defences are ready. But the immune system has a remarkable feature: it remembers. If the same germ ever returns, the body can fight it off quickly, often before any illness even appears.

A vaccine takes clever advantage of this memory. It introduces a harmless version of a germ, or a tiny piece of one, into the body. This cannot cause the disease, but it is enough to teach the immune system what the real germ looks like. The body produces antibodies and, crucially, remembers how to make them again. Later, if the person meets the real germ, their immune system is already prepared and can defeat it before it does any harm. In effect, a vaccine is a safe practice run for the immune system.

Vaccines protect more than just the individual. When most people in a community are vaccinated, a disease finds it very hard to spread, because there are too few people left for it to infect. This effect, known as herd immunity, protects even those who cannot be vaccinated, such as newborn babies or people who are already seriously ill. In this way, getting vaccinated is not only a personal choice but also a way of protecting others.

Like all medicines, vaccines can have side effects, though serious ones are extremely rare, and scientists test them carefully for years before they are approved. The small risks of vaccination are vastly outweighed by the dangers of the diseases they prevent. In fact, the illnesses that vaccines guard against have caused far more harm than the vaccines themselves ever could. Thanks to widespread vaccination, illnesses that once filled hospitals are now, in many places, almost forgotten.

Vaccines are one of science's quiet triumphs. They work invisibly, preventing illnesses that simply never happen, which can make their importance very easy to overlook. Yet behind their simple injection lies a clever idea: teaching the body to defend itself, and in doing so, protecting whole communities from diseases that once seemed completely unstoppable.

questions.question: What is the passage mainly about?
choices: Why infectious diseases are no longer dangerous.
How to avoid ever going to the hospital.
How vaccines work and why they matter.
Which disease has killed the most people.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — ทั้งบทอธิบายว่าวัคซีนทำงานอย่างไรและสำคัญเพราะอะไร ส่วน A/B/D ไม่ใช่ใจความหลัก

questions.question: What does the immune system produce to destroy germs?
choices: Special weapons called antibodies.
A kind of harmless vaccine.
New germs to fight the old ones.
A type of sweet healing medicine.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — ย่อหน้าที่ 2 บอกว่าระบบภูมิคุ้มกันสร้าง "antibodies" เพื่อทำลายเชื้อโรค

questions.question: What does a vaccine introduce into the body?
choices: A large dose of a deadly germ.
A powerful new kind of antibody.

questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — ย่อหน้าที่ 3 ระบุว่าวัคซีนใส่เชื้อแบบไม่อันตรายหรือชิ้นส่วนเล็กๆ ของเชื้อเข้าไป เพื่อสอนภูมิคุ้มกัน

questions.question: In paragraph 2, the word "remarkable" is closest in meaning to:
choices: very ordinary and dull
worth noticing; impressive
extremely dangerous indeed
difficult to understand fully
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — "a remarkable feature" หมายถึงคุณสมบัติที่น่าทึ่ง/น่าสังเกต ตรงกับ "worth noticing; impressive"

questions.question: Why is a vaccine called "a safe practice run"?
choices: Because it makes the person very ill first.
Because it is done while the person runs.
Because it lets the body prepare without real danger.
Because it must be repeated every single day.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — ย่อหน้าที่ 3 อธิบายว่าวัคซีนให้ร่างกายซ้อมรับมือเชื้อโดยไม่ก่อโรคจริง จึงเป็น "การซ้อมที่ปลอดภัย"

questions.question: What is herd immunity?
choices: When a disease cannot spread easily in a vaccinated community.
When everyone in a community catches a disease.
When only farm animals out on a farm are given vaccines.
