questions.question: What is the writer's attitude toward vaccines?
choices: Doubtful — vaccines are probably useless.
Fearful — vaccines are extremely dangerous.
Uninterested — the topic is rather boring.
Positive — vaccines are a quiet triumph.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — ย่อหน้าสุดท้ายเรียกวัคซีนว่า "one of science's quiet triumphs" จึงเป็นท่าทีบวก

questions.question: How is the passage mainly organized?
choices: It tells one single patient's whole illness day by day.
It explains the immune system, then how vaccines use it.
It lists diseases from deadliest to mildest.
It gives steps for making a vaccine at home.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — บทอธิบายระบบภูมิคุ้มกันก่อน แล้วจึงอธิบายว่าวัคซีนใช้ประโยชน์จากมันอย่างไร จึงเป็นโครงนี้

passages.title: The Rise of Remote Work
passages.passage:
A few years ago, the idea that millions of people might work from their homes, rather than travelling to an office each day, seemed unlikely to many employers. Offices were where work happened; home was where it stopped. Then, almost overnight, circumstances forced a vast experiment upon the world, and huge numbers of people began working remotely. The experiment is not over, and it has changed the way many of us think about work itself.

For employees, the appeal of remote work is easy to see. Without a daily commute, people gain back hours of their lives, along with the money once spent on travel. They can often arrange their day with more freedom, fitting work around family, exercise, or rest. Many report feeling less stressed and more productive when they can focus away from a noisy office, and some are even able to live far from expensive city centres, in places they could never afford if they had to commute every day.

Employers, too, have found advantages. Companies can save large sums on office space, and they can hire talented people from almost anywhere in the world, rather than only those who happen to live nearby. Some studies have found that remote workers are just as productive as office workers, and occasionally even more so. A worker in a small town can now join a company based in a city hundreds of kilometres away. For many businesses, a workforce spread across different cities, or even countries, has quietly become normal.

Yet remote work also has clear drawbacks. Working alone at home can be lonely, and the line between work and personal life easily blurs, so that people struggle to switch off in the evening. New employees, in particular, may find it harder to learn, to build relationships, and to feel part of a team when they rarely meet their colleagues in person. The casual conversations that spark ideas and friendships are hard to replace through a screen.

Because of these mixed results, many companies are now settling on a middle path known as hybrid work, in which employees split their time between home and the office. This approach tries to keep the freedom and focus of remote work while preserving the connection and teamwork of the office. Getting the balance right is far from simple, and different jobs and different people may need quite different arrangements.

Whatever shape it finally takes, the world of work has changed. The old assumption that work must happen in one place, at fixed hours, has been broken, perhaps for good. The challenge now is to build new ways of working that take the best of both worlds — combining the flexibility that people have come to value with the human connection that no screen can ever fully provide.

questions.question: What is the passage mainly about?
choices: Why offices should be closed forever.
The rise of remote work and its effects.
How to find a job in a faraway city.
Which company has the largest office.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ทั้งบทเล่าการเติบโตของการทำงานทางไกลและผลกระทบ ส่วน A/C/D ไม่ใช่ใจความหลัก

questions.question: What appeal of remote work do employees see?
choices: They must travel much further to work.
They spend more money on commuting.
They have far less free time at home.
They gain back hours once lost to commuting.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — ย่อหน้าที่ 2 บอกว่าการไม่ต้องเดินทางไปทำงานทำให้ได้เวลาชีวิตคืนมา พร้อมประหยัดเงินค่าเดินทาง

questions.question: What advantage have employers found?
choices: They can hire talented people from almost anywhere.
They are forced to rent offices that are much larger.
They can only hire people who live nearby.

questions.question: In paragraph 4, the word "blurs" (the line easily blurs) is closest in meaning to:

disappears completely forever
becomes hard to see clearly

questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — "the line ... easily blurs" หมายถึงเส้นแบ่งงานกับชีวิตเริ่มไม่ชัด ตรงกับ "becomes hard to see clearly"

questions.question: Why might new employees find remote work harder?
choices: Because they already know everyone very well.
Because it is harder to learn and build relationships.
Because they are paid far more than others.
Because home is always noisier than an office.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ย่อหน้าที่ 4 บอกว่าพนักงานใหม่เรียนรู้ สร้างความสัมพันธ์ และรู้สึกเป็นส่วนหนึ่งของทีมได้ยากกว่าเมื่อแทบไม่ได้เจอเพื่อนร่วมงาน

questions.question: What is hybrid work?
choices: Working only from the office every day.
Working only from home every single day.
Splitting time between home and the office.
Working two completely different jobs at once.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — ย่อหน้าที่ 5 บอกว่า hybrid work คือการแบ่งเวลาระหว่างทำงานที่บ้านและที่ออฟฟิศ

questions.question: How would you best describe the writer's approach?
choices: Balanced, weighing the benefits and drawbacks.
Angry at everyone who works in an office.
Certain that remote work is always best.
Sure that remote work never works at all.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — ผู้เขียนชั่งน้ำหนักทั้งข้อดีและข้อเสียของการทำงานทางไกล จึงเป็นแนวทางสมดุล

questions.question: How is the passage mainly organized?
choices: It tells one worker's single day in order.
It lists companies from largest to smallest.
It gives steps for setting up a home office.
It weighs benefits and drawbacks, then a middle path.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — บทไล่ข้อดี (พนักงาน/นายจ้าง) → ข้อเสีย → แล้วเสนอทางสายกลาง (hybrid) จึงเป็นโครงนี้

passages.title: Should Students Be Allowed to Use AI for Their Schoolwork?
passages.passage:
Within a remarkably short time, powerful artificial intelligence tools have appeared that can write essays, solve problems, and answer almost any question in seconds. For students, these tools are both a temptation and a source of confusion. Schools and universities are now wrestling with a difficult question: should students be allowed to use AI for their schoolwork, and if so, where exactly should the line be drawn?

Those who welcome AI in education point to its potential as a tool for learning. Used well, they argue, AI can act like a patient private tutor, explaining difficult ideas in different ways, offering instant feedback, and helping students who are stuck. It can translate, summarise, and suggest, freeing students to focus on understanding rather than on routine tasks. In the working world that students will soon enter, these tools are already common, so learning to use them wisely, supporters say, is itself an important skill.

Opponents, however, fear that AI may damage learning rather than support it. If a student can produce a finished essay with a single command, they may never develop the ability to think, write, and argue for themselves. Skills are built through effort and struggle, and a tool that removes the struggle may also quietly remove the learning. There is also the problem of honesty: if work produced by a machine is presented as a student's own, how can a teacher judge what the student has actually learned?

A further difficulty is fairness and trust. Students who use AI heavily may earn higher marks than those who do all the work themselves, which seems deeply unfair. Yet detecting AI use is notoriously unreliable, and wrongly accusing innocent students is a serious risk. Banning the tools completely is tempting, but probably impossible, since students can simply use them at home where no one is watching.

Most thoughtful educators are therefore searching for a balanced approach rather than a simple ban or a complete free-for-all. They suggest teaching students when AI is genuinely helpful and when it is not, designing tasks that require real thinking, and being open about what kind of help is allowed. Some assessments may be done in class, without devices, while others may openly invite the use of AI, as long as students explain exactly how they used it. Above all, they want students to see these tools as an aid to thinking, not as a substitute for it.

In the end, the arrival of AI forces schools to ask what education is really for. If it is only about producing finished answers, then machines can already do much of the work. But if it is about developing minds that can think, question, and create, then how students use these powerful new tools — and how honestly — may matter more than ever before.

questions.question: What is the central question of the passage?
choices: Whether AI tools can solve maths problems.
Whether students should be allowed to use AI for schoolwork.
Whether teachers should use computers in class.
Whether long essays are really harder than written exams.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ทั้งบทถกประเด็น "ควรให้นักเรียนใช้ AI ทำงานเรียนไหม และขีดเส้นตรงไหน" ตัวเลือกอื่นเป็นรายละเอียดย่อย

questions.question: What do supporters say AI can act like?
choices: A strict and unfriendly examiner.
A replacement for every teacher.
A patient private tutor.
A machine that never explains anything.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — ย่อหน้าที่ 2 บอกว่าฝ่ายสนับสนุนมองว่า AI ทำหน้าที่เหมือนติวเตอร์ส่วนตัวที่ใจเย็น อธิบายได้หลายแบบ

questions.question: What do opponents fear AI may damage?
choices: Students' ability to think and write for themselves.
The speed at which essays are produced.
The number of computers in each school.
The total cost of running a single AI tool each year.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — ย่อหน้าที่ 3 บอกว่าฝ่ายค้านกลัวว่านักเรียนจะไม่พัฒนาความสามารถในการคิดและเขียนด้วยตนเอง

questions.question: In paragraph 3, "the struggle" (a tool that removes the struggle) is closest in meaning to:
choices: a comfortable and very easy time
a friendly classroom discussion
a kind of written examination
the difficult effort of learning
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — "Skills are built through effort and struggle" ดังนั้น the struggle = ความพยายาม/ความยากของการเรียนรู้

questions.question: Why is banning AI tools completely described as "probably impossible"?
choices: Because schools cannot afford to buy them.
Because students can use them at home unwatched.
Because teachers enjoy using them too much.
Because the tools work only inside schools.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ย่อหน้าที่ 4 บอกว่าห้ามทั้งหมดแทบเป็นไปไม่ได้ เพราะนักเรียนใช้ที่บ้านได้โดยไม่มีใครเห็น

questions.question: What problem with detecting AI use is mentioned?
choices: It is fairly cheap but also extremely slow to do.
It always names the wrong subject.
It is unreliable and may accuse innocent students.
It can only ever be carried out once a year.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — ย่อหน้าที่ 4 บอกว่าการตรวจจับการใช้ AI ไม่น่าเชื่อถือ และเสี่ยงกล่าวหานักเรียนที่บริสุทธิ์ผิด

questions.question: In the last paragraph, "these powerful new tools" refers to:
choices: AI tools used by students
ordinary school textbooks
written class examinations
the rules made by teachers
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — ทั้งบทพูดถึงเครื่องมือ AI ดังนั้น these powerful new tools จึงหมายถึงเครื่องมือ AI ที่นักเรียนใช้

questions.question: How would you best describe the writer's overall position?
choices: Certain that AI should be completely banned.
Sure that students should use AI for everything.
Uninterested in how students learn.
Balanced, favouring careful and honest use.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — ผู้เขียนเสนอทั้งสองฝ่ายแล้วเอนไปทางการใช้อย่างรอบคอบและซื่อสัตย์ จึงเป็นจุดยืนสมดุล

questions.question: How is the passage mainly organized?
choices: It tells the story of one student's essay.
It gives arguments for, against, then a balanced approach.
It lists various AI tools from the oldest to the newest.
It explains how to write an essay quickly.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — บทเสนอฝ่ายสนับสนุน → ฝ่ายค้าน → ปัญหาความเป็นธรรม → แล้วสรุปแนวทางสมดุล จึงเป็นโครงนี้

passages.title: Should Animals Be Used in Scientific Research?
passages.passage:
For more than a century, much of modern medicine has been built on a practice that many people find deeply uncomfortable: testing on animals. New medicines, surgical techniques, and treatments are often tried on mice, rabbits, or monkeys before they are ever used on humans. Supporters say this has saved countless lives; opponents call it cruel and increasingly unnecessary. The result is one of the most difficult ethical debates in all of science.

Those who defend animal research point firmly to its results. Many of the medical advances we now take for granted — vaccines, antibiotics, treatments for cancer and heart disease — were developed with the help of animal testing. Because animals such as mice share much of their biology with humans, supporters argue, studying them allows scientists to understand diseases and test new treatments safely, before risking any human lives. To abandon animal research entirely, they warn, could slow medical progress and ultimately cost human lives.

Opponents reply that the suffering of animals cannot be ignored simply because it happens to be useful to us. Animals in laboratories may be kept in cages, exposed to disease, or caused real pain, and they cannot consent or understand why. Critics also question how reliable the results truly are: a drug that is safe in mice may still prove dangerous in humans, since the two are far from identical. If animal tests are sometimes misleading, they ask, how much suffering can they really justify?

Much of the modern debate now focuses on alternatives. Advances in technology allow scientists to grow human cells in the laboratory, to model the body using powerful computers, and to test some substances without any animals at all. Supporters of these methods argue that they are often cheaper, faster, and more relevant to humans. Yet most scientists agree that, for now, such methods cannot completely replace the study of a living, whole body, in which organs and systems interact in ways too complex to copy. Around the world, researchers continue to search for new ways to reduce their reliance on animals.

Between these positions, many countries have adopted a careful compromise, often summarised as the "three Rs": replace animals with other methods wherever possible, reduce the number of animals used, and refine experiments to cause as little suffering as possible. Under this approach, animal research is neither freely allowed nor completely banned, but tightly controlled and constantly questioned.

In the end, the debate over animal research asks an uncomfortable question that has no easy answer: how should we weigh the welfare of animals against the health of human beings? As alternatives improve, the hope is that fewer animals will be needed over time. Until then, societies must keep deciding, case by case, where the line between necessary and unacceptable should be drawn.

questions.question: What is the central question of the passage?
choices: Whether mice and humans share the same biology.
Whether animals should be used in scientific research.
Whether computers are cheaper than animals.
Whether vaccines are safe for human beings.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ทั้งบทถกประเด็น "ควรใช้สัตว์ในการวิจัยทางวิทยาศาสตร์ไหม" ตัวเลือกอื่นเป็นรายละเอียดย่อย

questions.question: What do defenders of animal research point to?
choices: The low cost of keeping animals in cages.
The fact that animals enjoy being studied.
Medical advances developed with animal testing.
The speed of growing cells in a laboratory.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — ย่อหน้าที่ 2 บอกว่าฝ่ายสนับสนุนชี้ผลลัพธ์ เช่น วัคซีน ยาปฏิชีวนะ การรักษามะเร็ง/หัวใจ ที่พัฒนาได้ด้วยการทดลองในสัตว์

questions.question: What do opponents say about animal suffering?
choices: It cannot be ignored just because it is useful.
It is necessary for every kind of research.
It never actually happens in laboratories.
It matters far less than the cost of research.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — ย่อหน้าที่ 3 บอกว่าฝ่ายค้านมองว่าความทุกข์ของสัตว์ละเลยไม่ได้เพียงเพราะมันมีประโยชน์ต่อเรา

questions.question: In paragraph 3, the word "consent" is closest in meaning to:
choices: feel any kind of pain
escape from the cage
understand a disease
agree to take part
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — "they cannot consent or understand why" หมายถึงสัตว์ไม่สามารถยินยอม/ตกลงเข้าร่วมได้ ตรงกับ "agree to take part"

questions.question: Why do critics question how reliable animal results are?
choices: Because animals are exactly the same as humans.
Because a drug safe in mice may harm humans.
Because animals always refuse to take medicines.
Because scientists never test drugs on humans.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ย่อหน้าที่ 3 บอกว่ายาที่ปลอดภัยในหนูอาจอันตรายในคน เพราะทั้งสองไม่เหมือนกันทุกอย่าง จึงตั้งคำถามถึงความน่าเชื่อถือ

questions.question: What alternatives to animal testing are mentioned?
choices: Testing only on very large wild animals.
Banning all forms of medical research.
Growing human cells and using computer models.
Asking patients to test new drugs at home.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — ย่อหน้าที่ 4 ระบุทางเลือก เช่น เลี้ยงเซลล์มนุษย์ในห้องแล็บ และจำลองร่างกายด้วยคอมพิวเตอร์

questions.question: The "three Rs" refers to a plan to:
choices: replace, reduce, and refine animal use
remove every animal from the world
repeat experiments as often as possible
reward scientists who use more animals
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — ย่อหน้าที่ 5 อธิบาย "three Rs" ว่า replace (แทนที่), reduce (ลดจำนวน), refine (ปรับให้ทรมานน้อยลง)

questions.question: How would you best describe the writer's overall position?
choices: Certain that animal research must be banned at once.
Completely sure that animals feel no pain at all.
Completely uninterested in the welfare of any animals at all.
Balanced, presenting a difficult question with no easy answer.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — ผู้เขียนเสนอทั้งสองฝ่ายและทางประนีประนอม แล้วยอมรับว่าเป็นคำถามยากที่ไม่มีคำตอบง่ายๆ จึงเป็นจุดยืนสมดุล

questions.question: How is the passage mainly organized?
choices: It tells the long story of one single laboratory mouse.
It gives arguments for, against, alternatives, and a compromise.
It lists various medicines from the oldest to the newest.
It explains how to care for animals at home.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — บทเสนอฝ่ายสนับสนุน → ฝ่ายค้าน → ทางเลือก → ทางประนีประนอม (three Rs) จึงเป็นโครงนี้

passages.title: Why Saving Money Is So Hard
passages.passage:
Almost everyone agrees that saving money is sensible. We know we should put something aside for the future, for emergencies, or for the things we hope to buy one day. Yet for many people, saving is far easier said than done. Money seems to slip away, and good intentions rarely survive to the end of the month. Why is something we all agree is wise so surprisingly difficult to actually do?

Part of the answer lies in the way our minds are built. Humans naturally pay far more attention to the present than to the future. A pleasure we can enjoy today — a meal out, a new phone, a holiday — feels real and immediate, while the benefit of saving lies far away, vague and uncertain. Scientists call this tendency "present bias," and it means we often choose a small reward now over a larger reward later, even when we know perfectly well that the later reward is better for us.

Modern life makes the problem worse. Spending money has never been easier: a single tap on a phone can buy almost anything, with no cash to count and no coins to hand over. Advertising surrounds us, constantly suggesting new things to want, while easy credit allows us to spend money we do not yet have. Online shops even remember our card details, so buying something again takes only a moment. Each purchase feels small, but together they quietly drain whatever we had hoped to save.

Understanding these forces, however, points towards practical solutions. One of the most effective is to make saving automatic, so that a fixed amount moves into savings as soon as money arrives, before there is any chance to spend it. Because the money is gone before it can be missed, willpower is hardly needed at all. People who save automatically tend to save far more than those who rely on good intentions alone.

Other simple tricks can help too. Setting a clear goal — a specific sum, for a specific purpose — makes saving feel meaningful rather than painful. Waiting a day or two before any large purchase gives the present bias time to fade, so that fewer things are bought on impulse. Even keeping spending money separate from savings, in a different account, can make a surprising difference over time. Small, regular amounts can quietly grow into a surprisingly large sum over the years.

Saving, then, is hard not because people are foolish or weak, but because it goes against the natural pull of the present and the constant pressures of modern life. The good news is that, once we understand why saving is difficult, we can design our habits to work with our nature rather than against it — and so make a wise choice the easy one.

questions.question: What is the passage mainly about?
choices: Why everyone should spend more money.
How banks make a profit from savers.
Why saving is hard and how to make it easier.
Which things are the most expensive to buy.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — ทั้งบทอธิบายว่าทำไมการเก็บเงินจึงยาก และจะทำให้ง่ายขึ้นได้อย่างไร ส่วน A/B/D ไม่ใช่ใจความหลัก

questions.question: What is "present bias"?
choices: Choosing a small reward now over a larger one later.
Saving all of one's money for the distant future.
Spending money only on important emergencies.
Ignoring every advertisement we happen to see.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — ย่อหน้าที่ 2 อธิบายว่า present bias คือการเลือกรางวัลเล็กในตอนนี้แทนรางวัลใหญ่กว่าในอนาคต

questions.question: How does modern life make saving harder?
choices: It makes spending money slow and difficult.
It removes all advertising from our lives.
It forces everyone to pay only in coins.
It makes spending easy with a single tap.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — ย่อหน้าที่ 3 บอกว่าการใช้เงินง่ายขึ้นมาก แค่แตะโทรศัพท์ครั้งเดียวก็ซื้อได้แทบทุกอย่าง

questions.question: In paragraph 3, the word "drain" is closest in meaning to:
choices: slowly fill right up
gradually use up
safely protect
carefully count
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — "quietly drain whatever we had hoped to save" หมายถึงค่อยๆ ใช้เงินจนหมด ตรงกับ "gradually use up"

questions.question: Why does making saving automatic work so well?
choices: Because it requires a great deal of willpower.
Because banks force people to save money.
Because the money is gone before it is missed.
Because it makes people spend much more.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — ย่อหน้าที่ 4 บอกว่าเงินถูกโอนไปก่อนจะมีโอกาสใช้ จึงแทบไม่ต้องใช้ความตั้งใจ (willpower) เลย

questions.question: What trick helps reduce impulse buying?
choices: Waiting a day or two before a large purchase.
Buying things as quickly as possible.
Keeping all money in a single account.
Avoiding setting any savings goal.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — ย่อหน้าที่ 5 บอกว่าการรอสักวันสองวันก่อนซื้อของชิ้นใหญ่ช่วยให้ present bias จางลง ซื้อตามอารมณ์น้อยลง

questions.question: What is the writer's main message about people who struggle to save?
choices: People who cannot save are simply foolish.
Saving money is quite impossible for almost everyone alive.
Only the very rich are ever truly able to save.
Saving is hard for natural reasons, but habits can help.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — ย่อหน้าสุดท้ายบอกว่าการเก็บเงินยากเพราะธรรมชาติของจิตใจและชีวิตสมัยใหม่ ไม่ใช่เพราะคนโง่ และนิสัยที่ดีช่วยได้

questions.question: How is the passage mainly organized?
choices: It tells one person's shopping trip in order.
It explains why saving is hard, then offers solutions.
It lists prices from cheapest to most expensive.
It gives steps for opening a bank account.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — บทอธิบายสาเหตุที่เก็บเงินยาก (present bias + ชีวิตสมัยใหม่) แล้วเสนอวิธีแก้ จึงเป็นโครงนี้

passages.title: Should Schools Start Later in the Morning?
passages.passage:
In many countries, the school day begins early, often before eight o'clock in the morning. For generations, this has simply been accepted as normal. Recently, however, scientists and educators have begun to question it, pointing to a growing body of evidence about teenage sleep. Their conclusion is striking: starting school so early may be working against the very students it is meant to help. What once seemed a simple matter of routine has slowly become a genuine scientific question.

The argument rests on biology. During the teenage years, the body's internal clock naturally shifts, making young people feel sleepy later at night and want to wake later in the morning. This is not laziness; it is a real biological change. Yet most schools demand that teenagers wake very early, often after far too little sleep. Tired students, research shows, struggle to concentrate, remember less, and feel more anxious. Forcing them to learn early in the morning, supporters of change argue, is rather like asking them to work in the middle of their natural night.

Several schools that have delayed their start times report encouraging results. Students arrive more rested and alert, attendance improves, and in some cases grades rise. There may be safety benefits too, since sleepy teenagers are more likely to have accidents. Teachers, too, often notice that lessons run more smoothly later in the day. To supporters, the evidence points clearly in one direction: a later start would mean healthier, happier, and more successful students.

Critics, however, raise practical concerns. A later school day means a later finish, which can clash with sports, part-time jobs, and after-school activities. For families, changing school hours can create serious difficulties: parents who must leave early for work may have no one to look after younger children. School buses, often shared between schools at different times, would need to be reorganised, and younger children at the same school may need different timing, adding to the difficulty. Some argue that the cost and disruption outweigh the benefits.

There is also a question of habits. Some critics argue that teenagers stay up late mainly because of phones and screens, not biology, and that the real solution is better sleep habits rather than later school hours. Supporters reply that, while screens certainly play a part, the underlying biological shift is real and cannot simply be switched off by willpower.

In the end, the debate over school start times is about more than timetables. It asks whether schools should be designed around tradition and convenience, or around what we now understand about how young people actually learn and grow. As the evidence continues to build, more and more communities are deciding that the question is at least worth asking seriously.

questions.question: What is the central question of the passage?
choices: Whether teenagers use their phones too much.
Whether schools should start later in the morning.
Whether sports are more important than sleep.
Whether school buses should be made larger.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ทั้งบทถกประเด็น "โรงเรียนควรเริ่มเรียนสายขึ้นไหม" ตัวเลือกอื่นเป็นรายละเอียดย่อย

questions.question: What happens to the teenage body clock?
choices: It stops working during the teenage years.
It makes teenagers want to sleep much earlier.
It shifts, so they feel sleepy later at night.
It becomes exactly the same as an adult's.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — ย่อหน้าที่ 2 บอกว่านาฬิกาชีวภาพของวัยรุ่นเลื่อน ทำให้ง่วงดึกขึ้นและอยากตื่นสายขึ้น

questions.question: What do schools with later start times report?
choices: Students arrive more rested and alert.
Students fall asleep during every lesson.
Grades drop sharply in every subject.
Attendance becomes much worse than before.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — ย่อหน้าที่ 3 บอกว่าโรงเรียนที่เลื่อนเวลาเข้าเรียนรายงานว่านักเรียนพักผ่อนพอและตื่นตัวมากขึ้น

questions.question: In paragraph 4, the phrase "clash with" is closest in meaning to:
choices: agree perfectly with
pay money for
make more room for
conflict with
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — "a later finish ... can clash with sports" หมายถึงไปชนกับ/ขัดกับกิจกรรมอื่น ตรงกับ "conflict with"

questions.question: Why does the writer compare early lessons to "the middle of their natural night"?
choices: Because most teenagers simply sleep all day long.
Because early mornings feel like night to a teenager's clock.
Because schools are very dark in the morning.
Because the lessons there are taught long after midnight.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ย่อหน้าที่ 2 เปรียบว่าการบังคับเรียนเช้ามากเหมือนให้ทำงานกลางดึกตามนาฬิกาชีวภาพของวัยรุ่น

questions.question: What practical concern do critics raise?
choices: That students would learn far too much.
That teenagers would stop using phones.
That a later finish clashes with other activities.
That schools would have to close completely.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — ย่อหน้าที่ 4 บอกว่าการเลิกเรียนสายขึ้นชนกับกีฬา งานพิเศษ และกิจกรรมหลังเลิกเรียน

questions.question: In paragraph 5, "the underlying biological shift" refers to:
choices: the change in the teenage body clock
the cost of running school buses
the time that sports practice begins
the habit of using phones at night
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — บริบทย้อนกลับไปที่การเปลี่ยนของนาฬิกาชีวภาพวัยรุ่นในย่อหน้าที่ 2 ดังนั้นจึงหมายถึงสิ่งนั้น

questions.question: How would you best describe the writer's overall position?
choices: Certain that school hours should never change.
Sure that phones are the only real problem.
Completely uninterested in how teenagers really learn.
Balanced, but leaning toward taking the evidence seriously.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — ผู้เขียนเสนอทั้งสองฝ่ายแต่เอนไปทางว่าควรพิจารณาหลักฐานอย่างจริงจัง จึงเป็นจุดยืนสมดุลแต่เอนข้าง

questions.question: How is the passage mainly organized?
choices: It tells one student's morning hour by hour.
It gives arguments for, against, then a wider question.
It lists various schools by their different starting times.
It explains how to build a school timetable.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — บทเสนอฝ่ายสนับสนุน → ฝ่ายค้าน → แล้วยกคำถามที่กว้างขึ้น (โรงเรียนควรออกแบบรอบอะไร) จึงเป็นโครงนี้

passages.title: The Art of Learning from Failure
passages.passage:
From an early age, most of us are taught to fear failure. At school, mistakes are marked in red and met with disappointment; in life, we learn to hide our errors and to envy those who seem to succeed effortlessly. Yet a closer look at how people actually learn, grow, and create suggests something surprising: failure, far from being the opposite of success, is often a necessary step on the way to it.

Consider how we learn almost any difficult skill. A child learning to walk falls countless times; a musician plays endless wrong notes; a scientist runs experiment after experiment that does not work. In each case, the failures are not wasted. They provide information, showing what does not work and pointing the way towards what might. Every wrong note teaches the fingers a little more about where the right one lies. Without the freedom to fail, learning would slow to a crawl, because we would be far too afraid to ever try anything new.

History is full of famous examples. Many celebrated inventors, writers, and entrepreneurs failed repeatedly before they finally succeeded, and some of their greatest achievements grew directly out of earlier mistakes. What set them apart was not that they never failed, but how they responded when they did: treating each failure as a lesson rather than a verdict, and refusing to let it define them.

Psychologists have found that the way we think about failure matters enormously. People who see ability as fixed — believing they are simply "good" or "bad" at something — tend to give up when they fail, taking it as proof that they lack talent. Those who see ability as something that can grow, by contrast, treat failure as a natural part of improving. This second attitude, often called a "growth mindset," tends to lead to greater success over time.

None of this means that failure is pleasant, or that all failure is good. Careless or repeated failure, with no attempt to learn, helps no one at all. The point is rather that failure, handled wisely, is one of our most powerful teachers. Each setback, examined honestly, brings a small lesson that easy success rarely offers. The real danger is not in failing, but in fearing failure so much that we never take the risks that learning and creativity require.

Perhaps, then, we should change the way we talk about failure, especially with young people. Instead of treating every mistake as something shameful to be hidden, we might see it as evidence that someone is trying, stretching, and learning. A life with no failures is not a sign of success; far more often, it is a sign that someone has never dared to try anything difficult at all.

questions.question: What is the passage mainly about?
choices: Why students should never make mistakes.
How failure can be a necessary part of learning.
Which inventors were the most successful.
How to avoid failing at anything in life.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ทั้งบทชี้ว่าความล้มเหลวมักเป็นขั้นตอนจำเป็นของการเรียนรู้ ส่วน A/C/D ไม่ใช่ใจความหลัก

questions.question: Why are failures in learning not wasted?
choices: Because they always feel very pleasant.
Because they prove we have no talent.
Because they should be hidden from others.
Because they show what does not work.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — ย่อหน้าที่ 2 บอกว่าความล้มเหลวให้ข้อมูล แสดงสิ่งที่ใช้ไม่ได้ และชี้ทางไปสู่สิ่งที่อาจได้ผล

questions.question: What set successful people apart, according to the passage?
choices: How they responded when they failed.
The fact that they never failed at all.
Their refusal to ever try new things.
Their habit of hiding every mistake.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — ย่อหน้าที่ 3 บอกว่าสิ่งที่ทำให้คนสำเร็จต่างออกไปคือวิธีที่พวกเขาตอบสนองเมื่อล้มเหลว (มองเป็นบทเรียน)

questions.question: In paragraph 3, the word "verdict" is closest in meaning to:
choices: a helpful piece of advice
a brand-new experiment
a final judgement
a small reward
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — "a lesson rather than a verdict" เทียบบทเรียนกับ "คำตัดสินขั้นสุดท้าย" ดังนั้น verdict = a final judgement

questions.question: What is a "growth mindset"?
choices: Believing you are simply good or bad at things.
Believing ability can grow through effort.
Believing failure should always be avoided.
Believing talent is completely fixed from birth.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ย่อหน้าที่ 4 อธิบายว่า growth mindset คือการเชื่อว่าความสามารถเติบโตได้ผ่านความพยายาม

questions.question: What kind of failure does the writer say helps no one?
choices: A mistake made while trying something hard.
A failure that is treated as a useful lesson.
Careless failure with no attempt to learn.
A first attempt that does not quite work.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — ย่อหน้าที่ 5 ระบุว่าความล้มเหลวที่สะเพร่าหรือซ้ำๆ โดยไม่พยายามเรียนรู้ ไม่ช่วยอะไรเลย

questions.question: What does the writer suggest about talking to young people?
choices: We should treat mistakes as evidence of trying.
We should mark every mistake in red ink.
We should hide all failures from children.
We should expect children never to fail.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — ย่อหน้าสุดท้ายเสนอให้มองความผิดพลาดเป็นหลักฐานว่าเด็กกำลังพยายามและเรียนรู้ ไม่ใช่เรื่องน่าอาย

questions.question: How is the passage mainly organized?
choices: It tells one inventor's life story in order.
It lists famous failures from worst to best.
It gives steps for passing a difficult exam.
It argues a point, gives examples, then a lesson.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — บทเสนอแนวคิด (ความล้มเหลวจำเป็นต่อการเรียนรู้) → ยกตัวอย่าง → สรุปข้อคิด จึงเป็นโครงนี้

passages.title: The Intelligence of Octopuses
passages.passage:
Of all the creatures in the ocean, few are as strange or as fascinating as the octopus. With eight arms, three hearts, and blue blood, it looks almost like a creature from another planet. Yet what truly astonishes scientists is not the octopus's unusual body, but its mind. Despite being a distant relative of the snail, the octopus is remarkably intelligent — perhaps the cleverest of all the animals that have no backbone.

The evidence for octopus intelligence is impressive. In laboratories, octopuses have learned to open jars to reach food, to find their way through mazes, and even to recognise individual human faces, behaving differently towards keepers they like and dislike. Some have been seen using tools, carrying coconut shells across the seabed to build themselves a shelter — behaviour once thought to belong only to humans and a few clever mammals and birds. They have even been known to escape from sealed tanks, squeezing their soft bodies through gaps barely larger than an eye.

What makes this intelligence so surprising is that it developed in a completely different way from our own. The octopus's last common ancestor with humans lived hundreds of millions of years ago and had almost no brain to speak of. This means that octopus intelligence is not inherited from a shared clever ancestor; it evolved entirely separately. Studying it offers scientists a rare chance to see what intelligence can look like when it arises on a totally different branch of life.

The octopus's nervous system is unusual too. Unlike humans, who keep most of their nerve cells in the brain, the octopus has the majority spread throughout its arms. In a sense, each arm can think and act partly on its own, tasting and touching and even reacting to its surroundings without always waiting for instructions from the central brain. An octopus is, in a way, a creature whose intelligence is shared across its entire body.

This remarkable mind, however, comes with a strange limit: octopuses live very short lives, often only a year or two, and they do not raise their young or pass on what they have learned. Each octopus, however clever, must work everything out alone, within a single brief lifetime. For such a clever animal, this short and solitary life can seem almost a cruel trick of nature. Scientists sometimes wonder what such animals might achieve if only they lived longer and could teach the next generation.

The octopus reminds us that intelligence is not a single ladder with humans standing at the top. It can take many forms, appear in unexpected places, and evolve more than once. In the quiet, alien mind of the octopus, we catch a rare glimpse of how very different — and how varied — intelligence on our planet can truly be.

questions.question: What is the passage mainly about?
choices: How to keep an octopus as a pet.
Why the ocean is full of strange animals.
The surprising intelligence of the octopus.
Which sea creature has the most arms.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — ทั้งบทพูดถึงความฉลาดอันน่าทึ่งของปลาหมึก ส่วน A/B/D ไม่ใช่ใจความหลัก

questions.question: What evidence of octopus intelligence is given?
choices: They open jars and solve mazes.
They sleep for most of the day.
They live for many long years.
They have eight arms and blue blood.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — ย่อหน้าที่ 2 บอกว่าปลาหมึกเรียนเปิดฝาขวดและหาทางออกจากเขาวงกตได้ ตัวเลือกอื่นไม่ใช่หลักฐานความฉลาด

questions.question: What tool-using behaviour have octopuses been seen doing?
choices: Building a nest out of feathers.
Writing messages in the sand.
Catching fish with a long net.
Carrying coconut shells for shelter.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — ย่อหน้าที่ 2 ระบุว่าปลาหมึกบางตัวขนเปลือกมะพร้าวไปสร้างที่กำบัง ซึ่งเป็นพฤติกรรมการใช้เครื่องมือ

questions.question: In paragraph 3, the word "ancestor" is closest in meaning to:
choices: a young baby animal
a relative from long ago
a fierce and very dangerous enemy
a kind of ocean food
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — "last common ancestor" หมายถึงบรรพบุรุษ/ญาติร่วมจากอดีตอันไกลโพ้น ตรงกับ "a relative from long ago"

questions.question: Why is octopus intelligence so surprising to scientists?
choices: Because it copies human intelligence exactly.
Because octopuses cannot learn anything new.
Because it evolved separately from ours.
Because all sea animals are equally clever.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — ย่อหน้าที่ 3 บอกว่าความฉลาดของปลาหมึกวิวัฒนาการแยกจากของมนุษย์โดยสิ้นเชิง จึงน่าทึ่ง

questions.question: Where are most of the octopus's nerve cells found?
choices: Spread throughout its arms.
Stored only in its brain.
Found inside its three hearts.
Located in its blue blood.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — ย่อหน้าที่ 4 บอกว่าเซลล์ประสาทส่วนใหญ่ของปลาหมึกกระจายอยู่ทั่วแขนทั้งแปด ต่างจากมนุษย์ที่รวมอยู่ในสมอง

questions.question: Why do scientists wonder what octopuses might achieve if they lived longer?
choices: Because they would grow much larger.
Because they could teach the next generation.
Because they would lose their intelligence.
Because they would stop using tools.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ย่อหน้าที่ 5 บอกว่าปลาหมึกไม่ถ่ายทอดสิ่งที่เรียนรู้ จึงอนุมานได้ว่าถ้าอายุยืนและสอนรุ่นต่อไปได้ อาจทำได้มากกว่านี้

questions.question: How is the passage mainly organized?
choices: It tells one single octopus's whole life day by day.
It lists sea animals from largest to smallest.
It gives clear steps for studying a live octopus.
It introduces a claim, then gives evidence and reflections.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — บทเสนอข้ออ้าง (ปลาหมึกฉลาด) → ให้หลักฐาน → แล้วสะท้อนความหมาย จึงเป็นโครงนี้

passages.title: Should Sugary Drinks Be Taxed?
passages.passage:
Across the world, more and more people are drinking sweet, sugary drinks, from fizzy sodas to sweetened juices and energy drinks. At the same time, rates of obesity, tooth decay, and diabetes are rising. In response, a growing number of governments have introduced a controversial measure: a special tax on sugary drinks, designed to make them more expensive and to encourage people to drink less. Does such a tax really work, and is it fair?

Supporters argue that the case is strong. Sugary drinks, they point out, offer almost no nutrition while adding large amounts of sugar to our diets, and they are especially harmful to children's teeth and health. A tax, they say, gently pushes people towards healthier choices, just as taxes on cigarettes have helped to reduce smoking. Where such taxes have been introduced, sales of sugary drinks have often fallen, and some companies have responded by reducing the sugar in their products to avoid the tax — a change that benefits everyone.

There is also a question of cost to society. Illnesses linked to too much sugar, such as diabetes, are expensive to treat, and much of that cost falls on public health systems paid for by everyone. Supporters argue that it is only fair for the products causing the harm to help pay for it, and that the money raised can be spent on health education or on sport. When a hospital bed is filled by an illness that good habits might have prevented, the loss is felt by the whole community.

Critics, however, are not convinced. A sugar tax, they argue, falls hardest on the poor, who spend a larger share of their income on such drinks and may simply pay more without changing what they buy. They also question whether a single tax can solve a problem with so many causes, from diet to exercise to lifestyle. Why single out sugary drinks, critics ask, when so many other foods are also unhealthy?

Others worry about the principle itself. Should governments use taxes to control what people eat and drink at all? Some see it as sensible public health; others see it as interfering with personal freedom and treating adults like children. There is also the practical point that determined people will simply buy sugary drinks anyway, tax or no tax.

Most evidence suggests that sugar taxes can reduce consumption, especially when they are combined with clear labels and good education, but that they are not a complete solution on their own. The debate, in the end, is about how far a society should go to protect people's health, and where the line between helpful guidance and unwanted control should be drawn. Where exactly that line should fall is something different societies answer in very different ways.

questions.question: What is the central question of the passage?
choices: Whether fizzy drinks taste better than juice.
Whether sugary drinks should be taxed.
Whether children drink too much water.
Whether smoking is worse than sugar.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ทั้งบทถกประเด็น "ควรเก็บภาษีเครื่องดื่มที่มีน้ำตาลไหม" ตัวเลือกอื่นเป็นรายละเอียดย่อย

questions.question: What do supporters compare a sugar tax to?
choices: A new tax placed on fresh, healthy vegetables.
A reward for drinking more soda.
A tax on cigarettes that reduced smoking.
A free gift for every customer.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — ย่อหน้าที่ 2 เปรียบภาษีน้ำตาลกับภาษีบุหรี่ที่ช่วยลดการสูบบุหรี่

questions.question: How have some companies responded to the tax?
choices: By reducing the sugar in their products.
By doubling the sugar in their drinks.
By closing all of their factories.
By giving the drinks away for free.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — ย่อหน้าที่ 2 บอกว่าบางบริษัทลดน้ำตาลในผลิตภัณฑ์เพื่อเลี่ยงภาษี ซึ่งเป็นผลดีต่อทุกคน

questions.question: In the final paragraph, the word "consumption" is closest in meaning to:
choices: the price of a product
a kind of serious illness
a clear food label
the amount people use
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — "reduce consumption" หมายถึงลดปริมาณการบริโภค/การดื่ม ตรงกับ "the amount people use"

questions.question: Why do critics say the tax falls hardest on the poor?
choices: Because the poor never drink sugary drinks.
Because they spend a larger share of income on them.
Because rich people pay the tax instead.
Because the tax only applies to the wealthy.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ย่อหน้าที่ 4 บอกว่าคนจนใช้สัดส่วนรายได้กับเครื่องดื่มเหล่านี้มากกว่า จึงอาจจ่ายแพงขึ้นโดยไม่เปลี่ยนพฤติกรรม

questions.question: What worry about principle is mentioned?
choices: That the new tax makes the drinks taste much worse.
That companies will earn too much money.
That governments should not control what people eat.
That sugary drinks will disappear forever.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — ย่อหน้าที่ 5 ยกประเด็นหลักการว่ารัฐควรใช้ภาษีควบคุมสิ่งที่ประชาชนกินดื่มหรือไม่ บางคนมองว่าเป็นการก้าวก่ายเสรีภาพ

questions.question: In paragraph 3, "the products causing the harm" refers to:
choices: sugary drinks
public health systems
cigarettes and alcohol
healthy foods
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — บริบทพูดถึงสินค้าที่ก่อโรค (น้ำตาล/เบาหวาน) ควรช่วยจ่ายค่ารักษา ดังนั้นจึงหมายถึงเครื่องดื่มที่มีน้ำตาล

questions.question: How would you best describe the writer's overall position?
choices: Certain that a sugar tax solves everything.
Completely sure that taxes never change behaviour.
Completely uninterested in ordinary people's health.
Balanced, noting taxes help but are not a complete solution.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — ผู้เขียนเสนอทั้งสองฝ่ายแล้วสรุปว่าภาษีช่วยลดการบริโภคได้แต่ไม่ใช่ทางแก้สมบูรณ์ จึงเป็นจุดยืนสมดุล

questions.question: How is the passage mainly organized?
choices: It tells one person's shopping trip in order.
It gives arguments for, against, then a balanced view.
It lists drinks from sweetest to least sweet.
It explains, step by step, how to make a fizzy drink.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — บทเสนอฝ่ายสนับสนุน → ฝ่ายค้าน → แล้วสรุปมุมมองสมดุล จึงเป็นโครงนี้

passages.title: The Science of Happiness
passages.passage:
What makes us happy? For centuries, this was a question for philosophers and poets. In recent decades, however, it has also become a subject for science. Researchers around the world have begun to study happiness carefully, asking thousands of people about their lives and looking for patterns. Their findings are sometimes surprising, and they challenge many of the things we tend to assume about what a good life requires.

One of the clearest discoveries concerns money. Having enough money to meet our basic needs — food, shelter, safety — does make a real difference to happiness. Beyond that point, however, extra money matters far less than most people expect. Once we are comfortable, becoming richer brings only small gains in happiness, and chasing wealth for its own sake often leaves people no happier than before. Yet many of us continue to believe that just a little more money would finally make us content.

If money is not the answer, what is? Again and again, the research points to the importance of relationships. People with strong connections to family and friends tend to be happier, healthier, and even longer-lived than those who are isolated. Time spent with people we care about appears to be one of the most reliable sources of happiness there is — far more valuable than possessions, and yet surprisingly easy to neglect in a busy life. A lonely life, the research suggests, can harm our health almost as much as some physical illnesses do.

Other findings are equally striking. Helping others, it turns out, tends to make us happier than spending on ourselves. Gratitude — simply noticing and appreciating the good things we already have — has been shown to lift our mood. And experiences, such as travel or time with friends, generally bring more lasting happiness than buying objects, perhaps because they become memories we treasure and stories we share.

Science also suggests that happiness is partly a skill, not just a stroke of luck. Habits such as regular exercise, enough sleep, time outdoors, and helping others can all raise our general sense of wellbeing. None of these is a magic cure, and life will always bring difficulty and sadness. But the research suggests that, to a greater degree than we often realise, our happiness lies partly in our own hands. Even small daily choices, repeated over months and years, can quietly shape how content we feel.

Perhaps the deepest lesson of this research is also the simplest. The things that truly make us happy — close relationships, kindness, gratitude, meaningful activity — are often not the things we spend our lives chasing. Understanding the science of happiness will not solve every problem, but it may gently remind us to value what matters most, before we look right past it.

questions.question: What is the passage mainly about?
choices: Why money is the key to a happy life.
What science reveals about human happiness.
How philosophers defined happiness long ago.
Which country is the happiest in the world.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ทั้งบทเล่าว่างานวิจัยทางวิทยาศาสตร์เผยอะไรบ้างเกี่ยวกับความสุข ส่วน A/C/D ไม่ใช่ใจความหลัก

questions.question: What does research say about money beyond basic needs?
choices: It is the only thing that brings happiness.
It makes people much sadder over time.
It must be given away to bring joy.
It matters far less than people expect.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — ย่อหน้าที่ 2 บอกว่าเมื่อพ้นความจำเป็นพื้นฐานแล้ว เงินที่เพิ่มขึ้นสำคัญน้อยกว่าที่คนส่วนใหญ่คาด

questions.question: What does the research repeatedly point to as important?
choices: Strong relationships with family and friends.
Owning as many possessions as possible.
Working all alone for very long hours each day.
Avoiding contact with other people.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — ย่อหน้าที่ 3 บอกว่างานวิจัยชี้ซ้ำๆ ว่าความสัมพันธ์กับครอบครัวและเพื่อนสำคัญต่อความสุข

questions.question: In paragraph 4, the word "gratitude" is closest in meaning to:
choices: a feeling of deep anger
a deep and constant fear of the future
appreciating the good things we have
a strong wish for more money
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — "Gratitude — simply noticing and appreciating the good things" ดังนั้น gratitude = การเห็นคุณค่าสิ่งดีๆ ที่เรามี

questions.question: Why might experiences bring more lasting happiness than objects?
choices: Because they are always much cheaper.
Because they become treasured memories.
Because they can be sold again later.
Because they never involve other people.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ย่อหน้าที่ 4 บอกว่าประสบการณ์กลายเป็นความทรงจำที่เราหวงแหนและเรื่องเล่าที่แบ่งปัน จึงให้ความสุขที่ยืนยาวกว่า

questions.question: What does the passage say about happiness as a skill?
choices: It is purely a matter of luck.
It cannot be changed by our habits.
Habits like exercise can raise wellbeing.
Only wealthy people can ever learn it.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — ย่อหน้าที่ 5 บอกว่าความสุขเป็นทักษะส่วนหนึ่ง นิสัยอย่างการออกกำลังกายช่วยยกระดับความเป็นอยู่ที่ดีได้

questions.question: What is the writer's main message?
choices: We should value relationships, kindness, and gratitude.
We should spend all our energy earning money.
We should ignore what scientists say about happiness.
We should expect life to be happy at all times.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — ย่อหน้าสุดท้ายเตือนให้เห็นคุณค่าของความสัมพันธ์ ความเมตตา และความกตัญญู ซึ่งคือสิ่งที่ทำให้สุขจริง

questions.question: How is the passage mainly organized?
choices: It tells one happy person's life story.
It lists various countries by their level of happiness.
It gives clear steps for becoming very wealthy.
It presents several research findings, then a lesson.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — บทเสนอผลการวิจัยหลายเรื่อง (เงิน ความสัมพันธ์ การให้ ความกตัญญู) แล้วปิดด้วยข้อคิด จึงเป็นโครงนี้

passages.title: How Earthquakes Happen
passages.passage:
Without warning, the ground begins to shake. Buildings tremble, objects fall, and in the most severe cases whole cities can be damaged in moments. Earthquakes are among the most frightening and destructive of all natural events. Yet behind their sudden violence lies a slow and steady process, deep beneath our feet, that has been shaping the Earth for billions of years. For thousands of years, people explained these terrifying events with myths and legends, long before science could offer a real answer.

To understand earthquakes, we must look at the outer shell of our planet. The Earth's surface is not a single solid piece, but is broken into enormous sections called tectonic plates, which fit together rather like the pieces of a giant cracked eggshell. These plates are not still. Driven by heat from deep within the Earth, they move very slowly — only a few centimetres a year, about as fast as your fingernails grow — but with truly unimaginable force.

Most earthquakes happen at the edges, where two plates meet. As the plates push against, pull away from, or slide past one another, enormous pressure builds up at the boundary. For years, the rough edges of the plates may lock together, holding firm while the strain steadily increases. Then, suddenly, the rock can take no more. The plates slip, the stored energy is released all at once, and the ground shakes as that energy travels outward in waves. The largest earthquakes can release more energy in a few seconds than a whole city uses in a year.

This is exactly why earthquakes are so hard to predict. The slow build-up of pressure may take decades or even centuries, and the moment of release can come at almost any time. Scientists can identify which areas are most at risk, because they lie along the boundaries between plates, but they still cannot say exactly when an earthquake will strike. This uncertainty makes earthquakes especially dangerous in crowded cities built in risky zones.

Although we cannot prevent earthquakes, we can prepare for them. In countries where earthquakes are common, engineers design buildings to sway and absorb the shaking rather than collapse, and people practise what to do when the ground moves. Such preparations cannot stop an earthquake, but they can mean the difference between a frightening event and a deadly disaster. Simple steps, such as knowing where to shelter, can save lives in the crucial moments before help arrives.

Earthquakes remind us of a truth that is easy to forget: the ground beneath us is not as solid and still as it seems. We live on the thin, moving surface of a restless planet, whose vast forces shaped the mountains and oceans long before we ever existed, and continue quietly to shape them today.

questions.question: What is the passage mainly about?
choices: How to build a strong house that can never fall.
Why large cities should never be built anywhere at all.
How earthquakes happen and why they are hard to predict.
Which single earthquake was the most powerful one ever recorded.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — ทั้งบทอธิบายว่าแผ่นดินไหวเกิดอย่างไรและทำไมจึงทำนายยาก ส่วน A/B/D ไม่ใช่ใจความหลัก

questions.question: What is the Earth's surface broken into?
choices: Enormous sections called tectonic plates.
One single, completely solid piece of hard rock.
Thousands of small grains of sand.
Several deep and very wide oceans.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — ย่อหน้าที่ 2 บอกว่าพื้นผิวโลกแตกเป็นแผ่นใหญ่ๆ เรียกว่า tectonic plates

questions.question: How fast do the tectonic plates move?
choices: As fast as a person running.
Faster than the speed of sound.
They do not move at all.
About as fast as fingernails grow.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — ย่อหน้าที่ 2 ระบุว่าแผ่นเปลือกโลกขยับช้ามาก ไม่กี่เซนติเมตรต่อปี ราวๆ ความเร็วที่เล็บงอก

questions.question: In paragraph 3, the word "strain" is closest in meaning to:
choices: a kind of soft rock
pressure or tension
a sudden loud noise
a deep ocean wave
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — "the strain steadily increases" หมายถึงแรงกด/ความตึงเครียดที่สะสมเพิ่มขึ้น ตรงกับ "pressure or tension"

questions.question: Why are earthquakes so hard to predict?
choices: Because plates never actually move.
Because scientists ignore risky areas.
Because the moment of release can come anytime.
Because earthquakes only happen in deserts.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — ย่อหน้าที่ 4 บอกว่าการสะสมแรงกินเวลานานและจังหวะปลดปล่อยเกิดได้แทบทุกเมื่อ จึงทำนายเวลาที่แน่นอนไม่ได้

questions.question: How do engineers design buildings in earthquake zones?
choices: To sway and absorb the shaking.
To be as tall as they possibly can.
To have no windows at all.
To collapse quickly and safely.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — ย่อหน้าที่ 5 บอกว่าวิศวกรออกแบบอาคารให้โยกและดูดซับแรงสั่นแทนที่จะพังถล่ม

questions.question: What is the writer's main purpose?
choices: To frighten readers away from all cities.
To sell earthquake insurance.
To prove the Earth is perfectly still.
To explain how earthquakes happen.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — บทมุ่งอธิบายว่าแผ่นดินไหวเกิดขึ้นได้อย่างไร ไม่ได้ขู่ ขายประกัน หรือบอกว่าโลกนิ่งสนิท

questions.question: How is the passage mainly organized?
choices: It follows one earthquake second by second.
It explains the cause, then prediction and preparation.
It lists earthquakes from strongest to weakest.
It gives detailed steps for building a whole new city.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — บทอธิบายสาเหตุ (แผ่นเปลือกโลก) → ความยากในการทำนาย → การเตรียมพร้อม จึงเป็นโครงนี้

passages.title: Should Everyone Learn to Code?
passages.passage:
As computers and software shape more and more of modern life, a bold claim has become popular: that everyone should learn to code. Some governments have added computer programming to school timetables, and many people now describe coding as a basic skill for the future, as important as reading or arithmetic. But is this really true? Should learning to code be something every student does, whether they want to or not?

Supporters of teaching coding to all offer several arguments. We live, they point out, in a world run by software, from phones to banks to hospitals, and understanding how that software works helps people make sense of the world around them. Coding, they add, teaches valuable ways of thinking: breaking big problems into smaller steps, being precise, and learning from mistakes. Even students who never become programmers, they argue, will benefit from these skills, and many well-paid jobs now require at least some knowledge of code. Understanding even a little about how programs are built, supporters add, makes people less helpless when technology goes wrong.

Critics, however, question the idea that everyone needs to code. Not every student enjoys it or finds it useful, just as not everyone needs to master chemistry or music to a high level. There is a risk, they warn, of forcing a difficult subject on students who would gain more from other things, and of treating coding as a magic key to success when, in reality, most jobs do not require it. Time in school is limited, and every hour spent coding is an hour not spent on something else.

There is also the question of what "learning to code" really means. Writing a few simple programs is very different from becoming a skilled software engineer, and some worry that brief, compulsory lessons may leave students with neither real ability nor real interest. Technology, too, changes quickly: the exact languages students learn today may be outdated by the time they finish school, even if the underlying ways of thinking remain useful. What truly lasts, many argue, is not any single language but the habit of clear, logical thinking.

A more balanced view is that all students benefit from understanding the basics of how computers and software work, without everyone needing to become an expert programmer. Just as we teach basic science to everyone while only some go on to become scientists, schools might give every student a foundation in computational thinking, while allowing those who love it to go much further.

In the end, the debate over coding is really a debate about education itself: what skills truly matter for the future, and how far we can predict that future at all. Whatever the answer, learning how to think clearly about problems — with or without a computer — is unlikely ever to go out of date.

questions.question: What is the central question of the passage?
choices: Whether computers are better than books.
Whether everyone should learn to code.
Whether chemistry is harder than music.
Whether schools should buy more computers.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ทั้งบทถกประเด็น "ทุกคนควรเรียนเขียนโค้ดไหม" ตัวเลือกอื่นเป็นรายละเอียดย่อย

questions.question: What ways of thinking do supporters say coding teaches?
choices: Memorising long lists of facts.
Avoiding all kinds of mistakes.
Breaking big problems into smaller steps.
Always working as slowly as one possibly can.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — ย่อหน้าที่ 2 บอกว่าการเขียนโค้ดสอนการแยกปัญหาใหญ่เป็นขั้นเล็กๆ ความแม่นยำ และการเรียนรู้จากความผิดพลาด

questions.question: What do critics say about forcing coding on students?
choices: Some would gain more from other things.
Everyone enjoys it equally.
It is the only path to success.
It should replace every other subject.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — ย่อหน้าที่ 3 บอกว่าฝ่ายค้านเตือนว่าการบังคับวิชายากกับเด็กที่จะได้ประโยชน์จากสิ่งอื่นมากกว่า เป็นความเสี่ยง

questions.question: In paragraph 4, the word "compulsory" is closest in meaning to:
choices: enjoyable and very easy
extremely expensive
completely optional
required for everyone
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — "brief, compulsory lessons" หมายถึงบทเรียนภาคบังคับ ตรงกับ "required for everyone"

questions.question: Why might the exact languages students learn become a problem?
choices: Because they are far too easy to learn.
Because technology changes quickly.
Because no jobs ever use them.
Because students forget how to read.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ย่อหน้าที่ 4 บอกว่าเทคโนโลยีเปลี่ยนเร็ว ภาษาที่เรียนวันนี้อาจล้าสมัยเมื่อเรียนจบ

questions.question: What balanced view does the writer describe?
choices: Everyone must become an expert programmer.
No student should ever study computers.
All learn the basics; some go further.
Only the cleverest students may code.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — ย่อหน้าที่ 5 เสนอว่าทุกคนเรียนพื้นฐานการคิดเชิงคำนวณ ส่วนคนที่รักก็ไปต่อได้ลึกกว่า

questions.question: In paragraph 5, "those who love it" refers to students who love:
choices: coding
chemistry
music
reading
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — ทั้งย่อหน้าพูดถึงการเขียนโค้ด ดังนั้น those who love it จึงหมายถึงนักเรียนที่รักการเขียนโค้ด

questions.question: How would you best describe the writer's overall position?
choices: Certain that everyone must learn to code.
Sure that coding is a complete waste of time.
Completely uninterested in what the schools teach.
Balanced, favouring basics for all and depth for some.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — ผู้เขียนเสนอทั้งสองฝ่ายแล้วเอนไปทางให้ทุกคนเรียนพื้นฐานและบางคนเรียนลึก จึงเป็นจุดยืนสมดุล

questions.question: How is the passage mainly organized?
choices: It tells the story of one student's coding lesson.
It gives arguments for, against, then a balanced view.
It lists programming languages by age.
It explains how to write a computer program.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — บทเสนอฝ่ายสนับสนุน → ฝ่ายค้าน → ปัญหานิยาม → แล้วสรุปมุมมองสมดุล จึงเป็นโครงนี้

passages.title: The Power of Curiosity
passages.passage:
Why is the sky blue? How do birds know where to fly? What lies at the bottom of the sea? Children ask such questions constantly, driven by a force so familiar that we rarely stop to think about it: curiosity. This simple desire to know, to understand, and to explore is one of the most powerful features of the human mind, and it has shaped our history in ways we are only beginning to appreciate.

Curiosity is, above all, the engine of learning. When we are curious about something, our brains become more alert and more ready to absorb information. Studies have shown that people remember facts far better when they are curious about the answer, even when those facts have nothing to do with the original question. In other words, curiosity does not just make learning more enjoyable; it actually makes our minds work better. Curiosity, in this sense, prepares the mind to receive whatever comes next, rather like soil made ready for seeds.

Curiosity has also driven nearly every great discovery in history. Scientists do not usually begin with a useful goal in mind; more often, they simply want to understand something that puzzles them. Many of the inventions that now shape our daily lives, from electricity to medicine, grew out of someone's pure desire to know how the world works. A great deal of human progress, it seems, began with nothing more than a single curious question. The simple urge to ask 'why' has carried humanity all the way from the first stone tools to spacecraft and beyond.

Yet curiosity can fade. Young children are endlessly curious, but as people grow older, many become less willing to ask questions, perhaps from a fear of looking foolish, or simply from the habits of busy adult life. Schools, too, can sometimes reward giving the right answer more than asking a good question, quietly teaching students that curiosity matters less than it really does.

The good news is that curiosity can be encouraged and kept alive. Asking questions, trying new things, reading widely, and allowing ourselves to wonder all keep the mind curious and flexible. Treating "I don't know" not as something shameful but as the exciting beginning of learning can reopen a door that many adults have quietly allowed to close. Reawakening that early sense of wonder, many believe, is well worth the effort.

In a fast-changing world, where the facts we learn today may be out of date tomorrow, curiosity may be more valuable than any single piece of knowledge. The willingness to keep asking, exploring, and wondering is what allows us to keep learning throughout our whole lives. Far from being a childish trait to be outgrown, curiosity is one of the finest qualities a human mind can possibly keep.

questions.question: What is the passage mainly about?
choices: Why the sky is blue and birds fly.
The power and value of human curiosity.
How children ask far too many questions.
Which scientist made the best discovery.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ทั้งบทพูดถึงพลังและคุณค่าของความอยากรู้อยากเห็น ส่วน A/C/D ไม่ใช่ใจความหลัก

questions.question: What have studies shown about curiosity and memory?
choices: Curious people forget facts more quickly.
Curiosity has no effect on learning.
Only children can remember new facts.
People remember facts better when curious.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — ย่อหน้าที่ 2 บอกว่าคนจดจำข้อมูลได้ดีกว่ามากเมื่อรู้สึกอยากรู้คำตอบ

questions.question: How does the passage say scientists usually begin?
choices: By wanting to understand something puzzling.
With a clear and useful goal in mind.
By copying older inventions exactly.
With no interest in the world at all.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — ย่อหน้าที่ 3 บอกว่านักวิทยาศาสตร์มักเริ่มจากความอยากเข้าใจสิ่งที่ทำให้พวกเขาฉงน ไม่ใช่เป้าหมายที่เป็นประโยชน์

questions.question: In paragraph 4, the word "fade" is closest in meaning to:
choices: grow much stronger
stay exactly the same
gradually weaken
become very bright
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — "curiosity can fade" หมายถึงความอยากรู้ค่อยๆ จางหายหรืออ่อนลง ตรงกับ "gradually weaken"

questions.question: Why might adults become less curious?
choices: Because they already know everything.
Partly from a fear of looking foolish.
Because curiosity is harmful to them.
Because children answer all their questions.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ย่อหน้าที่ 4 บอกว่าผู้ใหญ่อยากถามน้อยลง ส่วนหนึ่งเพราะกลัวดูโง่ และจากนิสัยของชีวิตที่ยุ่ง

questions.question: What keeps the mind curious, according to the passage?
choices: Avoiding any kind of new experience.
Refusing to ever ask a question.
Asking questions and reading widely.
Accepting that "I don't know" is shameful.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — ย่อหน้าที่ 5 บอกว่าการตั้งคำถาม ลองสิ่งใหม่ และอ่านอย่างกว้างขวาง ช่วยรักษาความอยากรู้และความยืดหยุ่นของจิตใจ

questions.question: What is the writer's view of curiosity?
choices: It is a valuable quality worth keeping for life.
It is a childish trait we should outgrow.
It is dangerous and should be controlled.
It matters only to professional scientists.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — ย่อหน้าสุดท้ายบอกว่าความอยากรู้เป็นคุณสมบัติที่ดีที่สุดอย่างหนึ่งที่ควรรักษาไว้ตลอดชีวิต ไม่ใช่นิสัยเด็กที่ต้องทิ้ง

questions.question: How is the passage mainly organized?
choices: It tells the whole life story of one famous scientist.
It lists many questions in order from easy to hard.
It gives clear steps for passing a difficult exam.
It explains curiosity's value, how it fades, and how to keep it.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — บทอธิบายคุณค่าของความอยากรู้ → การที่มันจางลง → วิธีรักษาไว้ จึงเป็นโครงนี้

passages.title: Why the Sky Is Blue
passages.passage:
It is one of the first questions many children ask: why is the sky blue? The answer seems obvious until we try to give it, and then we realise we have no idea. After all, the air is not blue; we can see straight through it. Sunlight, too, looks white or yellow, not blue. So where does the colour of the sky actually come from? The explanation, worked out by scientists over a century ago, is a beautiful piece of physics hidden in plain sight above our heads.

The first surprise is that sunlight, which appears white, is in fact a mixture of all the colours of the rainbow. When sunlight passes through a glass prism, or through raindrops, it spreads out into red, orange, yellow, green, blue, and violet. Each colour is really a wave of light, and these waves are not all the same. Red light travels in long, gentle waves, while blue light travels in much shorter, tighter waves.

As sunlight pours into the Earth's atmosphere, it collides with countless tiny molecules of gas in the air. When light hits these molecules, it scatters, bouncing off in all directions. Here is the key fact: shorter waves of light scatter far more strongly than longer ones. Blue light, with its short waves, is scattered all across the sky, while red and yellow light, with their longer waves, mostly travel straight through. As a result, when we look up, we see blue light arriving from every direction at once.

This same simple idea explains another everyday mystery: why sunsets are red. At sunset, the sun is low, and its light must pass through a much thicker layer of atmosphere to reach our eyes. Along this long path, almost all of the blue light is scattered away long before it arrives. What remains is the red and orange light, which travels more easily through the air — and so the setting sun glows in warm, fiery colours.

It is remarkable that something as ordinary as the colour of the sky hides such an elegant explanation. The blue overhead and the red of the sunset are not really properties of the air itself, but the result of sunlight, waves, and tiny molecules interacting in ways our eyes can see but never directly notice. The science was first explained in detail in the nineteenth century, and it still amazes those who learn it for the first time.

The next time you look up at a clear blue sky, or watch the sun sink in a blaze of orange, you are not simply seeing the weather. You are watching physics in action — a daily, silent display of how light behaves, performed above our heads from the moment the sun rises until long after it sets.

questions.question: What is the passage mainly about?
choices: How children learn to ask questions.
Why the air is full of gas molecules.
Why the sky is blue and sunsets are red.
How to use a glass prism at home.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — ทั้งบทอธิบายว่าทำไมท้องฟ้าจึงเป็นสีฟ้าและพระอาทิตย์ตกจึงเป็นสีแดง ส่วน A/B/D ไม่ใช่ใจความหลัก

questions.question: What is white sunlight really made of?
choices: A mixture of all the colours of the rainbow.
A single colour that never changes.
Only the two colours red and orange together.
A kind of invisible blue gas.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — ย่อหน้าที่ 2 บอกว่าแสงอาทิตย์สีขาวจริงๆ เป็นส่วนผสมของทุกสีในสายรุ้ง

questions.question: How does blue light differ from red light?
choices: Blue light cannot travel through air.
Blue light is much warmer than red.
Blue light moves far more slowly.
Blue light travels in much shorter waves.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — ย่อหน้าที่ 2 บอกว่าแสงสีแดงเป็นคลื่นยาว ส่วนแสงสีฟ้าเป็นคลื่นสั้นกว่ามาก

questions.question: In paragraph 3, the word "scatters" is closest in meaning to:
choices: stops moving completely
bounces off in all directions
turns a very bright red
disappears completely forever
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — "it scatters, bouncing off in all directions" ดังนั้น scatter = กระเจิง/สะท้อนไปทุกทิศ ตรงกับ "bounces off in all directions"

questions.question: Why do we see blue when we look up?
choices: Because the air itself is painted blue.
Because red light scatters most strongly.
Because blue light scatters across the whole sky.
Because the sun gives off only blue light.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — ย่อหน้าที่ 3 บอกว่าแสงสีฟ้าคลื่นสั้นกระเจิงไปทั่วท้องฟ้า เราจึงเห็นสีฟ้ามาจากทุกทิศเมื่อแหงนมอง

questions.question: Why are sunsets red?
choices: Blue light is scattered away on its long path.
The sun gives off only red light at night.
The air becomes much thinner at sunset.
Red light scatters more than any other colour.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — ย่อหน้าที่ 4 บอกว่าตอนตะวันตก แสงผ่านชั้นบรรยากาศหนาขึ้น แสงสีฟ้าถูกกระเจิงหายไปก่อน เหลือแสงสีแดง/ส้ม

questions.question: What is the writer's main purpose?
choices: To warn readers not to look at the sun.
To sell special glass prisms to young children.
To prove that the air is coloured blue.
To explain the science behind the sky's colours.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — บทมุ่งอธิบายฟิสิกส์เบื้องหลังสีของท้องฟ้า ไม่ได้เตือนภัย ขายปริซึม หรือบอกว่าอากาศมีสีฟ้า

questions.question: How is the passage mainly organized?
choices: It tells one sunset minute by minute.
It poses a question, then explains the physics.
It lists colours from brightest to darkest.
It gives steps for building a prism.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — บทตั้งคำถาม (ทำไมท้องฟ้าฟ้า) แล้วอธิบายหลักฟิสิกส์ (แสง คลื่น การกระเจิง) จึงเป็นโครงคำถาม–คำอธิบาย

passages.title: Should We Spend Money Exploring Space?
passages.passage:
Every year, governments and companies spend vast sums of money exploring space: launching rockets, building telescopes, sending robots to distant planets, and dreaming of journeys to Mars and beyond. To some, this is humanity at its most inspiring. To others, it is a scandalous waste, spending fortunes on the stars while serious problems remain unsolved here on Earth. Should we really be spending so much money on space?

Critics of space spending make a powerful point. Our own planet, they argue, faces enormous challenges: poverty, hunger, disease, and the growing threat of climate change. The billions spent on space, they say, could feed the hungry, build hospitals and schools, or help to protect the environment. How can we justify sending robots to Mars, they ask, when millions of people on Earth still lack clean water and basic healthcare?

Supporters of space exploration reply that this is a false choice. The money spent on space is, in fact, a tiny fraction of what governments spend overall, and cutting it would not magically solve the world's problems. More importantly, they argue, space exploration brings real benefits back to Earth. Many technologies we now rely on every day — from weather forecasting and satellite navigation to advances in medicine and materials — grew directly out of space research.

There is also the value of knowledge itself. Exploring space helps us understand our place in the universe, monitor our own planet from above, and watch for dangers such as asteroids. Studying other worlds even teaches us about our own: much of what we know about climate change, for instance, comes partly from comparing Earth with its neighbours. Satellites circling the Earth now warn us of storms, track our changing forests, and connect distant communities. To stop exploring, supporters argue, would be to close our eyes to the wider universe we are part of.

Perhaps the deepest argument is about what kind of species we wish to be. Throughout history, humans have always explored, crossing oceans and mapping unknown lands, and that curiosity has driven much of our progress. Space, supporters say, is simply the next frontier. To abandon that ancient urge to explore, they add, would be to turn our backs on something deeply human. Critics reply that exploration is a luxury we can only afford once everyone's basic needs are met — and that, for now, those needs must surely come first.

In the end, the debate is not really about whether space matters, but about priorities: how a society should balance the urgent problems of today against the discoveries and dreams of tomorrow.
