Most people fall somewhere between the two extremes, agreeing that space is worth exploring, while disagreeing about exactly how much we can afford to spend reaching for the stars.

questions.question: What is the central question of the passage?
choices: Whether humans will ever live on Mars.
Whether we should spend money exploring space.
Whether rockets are faster than telescopes.
Whether the universe is truly infinite.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ทั้งบทถกประเด็น "ควรใช้เงินสำรวจอวกาศไหม" ตัวเลือกอื่นเป็นรายละเอียดย่อย

questions.question: What do critics of space spending argue?
choices: That space travel is simply far too slow.
That building the rockets is rather dangerous.
That the money could help solve Earth's problems.
That telescopes are a waste of glass.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — ย่อหน้าที่ 2 บอกว่าฝ่ายค้านมองว่าเงินหลายพันล้านที่ใช้กับอวกาศควรเอาไปแก้ปัญหาบนโลก เช่น ความหิวโหยและสาธารณสุข

questions.question: What everyday technologies grew out of space research?
choices: Weather forecasting and satellite navigation.
Cooking, cleaning, and gardening tools.
Ordinary paper books and wooden furniture.
Bicycles and ordinary farm tools.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — ย่อหน้าที่ 3 บอกว่าเทคโนโลยีที่ใช้ทุกวัน เช่น การพยากรณ์อากาศและระบบนำทางด้วยดาวเทียม มาจากการวิจัยอวกาศ

questions.question: In paragraph 3, the phrase "a false choice" suggests the two options are:
choices: extremely expensive to take
perfectly fair and equal
very dangerous to make
not genuinely opposed
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — "a false choice" หมายถึงการบังคับให้เลือกระหว่างสองสิ่งที่จริงๆ ไม่ได้ขัดกัน ตรงกับ "not genuinely opposed"

questions.question: Why do supporters say cutting space spending would not solve world problems?
choices: Because space spending is the largest cost of all.
Because it is only a tiny fraction of spending.
Because the world has no problems at all.
Because robots cost nothing at all to build.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ย่อหน้าที่ 3 บอกว่าเงินที่ใช้กับอวกาศเป็นเพียงเศษเสี้ยวของงบทั้งหมด ตัดไปก็ไม่ได้แก้ปัญหาโลกอย่างน่าอัศจรรย์

questions.question: How does studying other worlds help us, according to the passage?
choices: It lets us escape from the Earth forever.
It proves that the universe is empty.
It teaches us about our own planet.
It makes space travel completely free.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — ย่อหน้าที่ 4 บอกว่าการศึกษาดาวดวงอื่นสอนเราเรื่องโลกของเราเอง เช่น ความรู้เรื่องการเปลี่ยนแปลงภูมิอากาศ

questions.question: In paragraph 5, "the next frontier" refers to:
choices: space
the deep ocean
ancient maps
distant islands
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — บริบทบอกว่ามนุษย์เคยสำรวจมหาสมุทรและดินแดน และอวกาศคือ "the next frontier" ดังนั้นจึงหมายถึงอวกาศ

questions.question: How would you best describe the writer's overall position?
choices: Certain that all space spending must stop now.
Sure that Earth's problems do not matter.
Uninterested in the future of humanity.
Balanced, framing the debate as one of priorities.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — ผู้เขียนเสนอทั้งสองฝ่ายแล้วสรุปว่าประเด็นคือเรื่อง "ลำดับความสำคัญ" จึงเป็นจุดยืนสมดุล

questions.question: How is the passage mainly organized?
choices: It tells the full story of one single rocket launch.
It gives arguments against, for, then a balanced view.
It lists planets from nearest to farthest.
It explains how to build a space telescope.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — บทเสนอฝ่ายค้าน → ฝ่ายสนับสนุน → แล้วสรุปมุมมองสมดุล (เรื่องลำดับความสำคัญ) จึงเป็นโครงนี้

passages.title: Why Gardening Is Good for Us
passages.passage:
Gardening might seem like a quiet, old-fashioned hobby, something for grandparents with time on their hands. Yet in recent years, doctors, scientists, and city planners have begun to take it surprisingly seriously. A growing body of research suggests that growing plants — whether in a large garden, a small balcony pot, or a shared community plot — can be remarkably good for both our bodies and our minds.

The physical benefits are real, if easy to overlook. Gardening involves digging, lifting, bending, and carrying, providing gentle but genuine exercise that strengthens muscles and keeps joints moving. Unlike a session at the gym, this activity does not feel like exercise at all, which means people are often happy to keep doing it for hours. For older people in particular, regular gardening can help maintain strength, balance, and independence. Even a short spell of weeding or watering gets the body moving after hours of sitting indoors.

The benefits to the mind may be even more striking. Spending time among plants has been shown to lower stress, lift mood, and ease feelings of anxiety. Focusing on a simple, hopeful task — planting a seed, pulling a weed, watering a row of vegetables — can quiet a busy, worried mind in much the same way as meditation. Many gardeners describe their time outdoors as the most peaceful part of their whole day. Some doctors now even suggest gardening to patients recovering from illness or stress.

Gardening also teaches patience and rewards care. A seed does not become a flower or a vegetable overnight; it grows slowly, over weeks and months, demanding regular attention. In a world of instant messages and fast results, this slow rhythm can be surprisingly satisfying. There is a quiet pleasure, too, in eating food you have grown yourself, or in watching a plant you cared for finally bloom. Caring for a living thing, day after day, can give a gentle sense of purpose that is easy to miss elsewhere.

There are wider benefits beyond the individual gardener. Community gardens, where neighbours share a piece of land, bring people together and strengthen friendships, especially in crowded cities where green space is scarce. Gardens also help the environment, providing food for bees and other insects, cooling hot streets, and even growing fresh food close to where people live, reducing the distance it must travel.

None of this means that gardening is a cure for every problem, or that everyone must take it up. But its combination of gentle exercise, calm, patience, and connection — to nature, to food, and to other people — makes it unusually valuable. In a fast, noisy, indoor world, the simple act of helping something grow may be one of the quietest and most rewarding things we can do.

questions.question: What is the passage mainly about?
choices: Why gardening is only for grandparents.
How gardening benefits our bodies and minds.
Which vegetables are the easiest to grow.
How to design a very large garden.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ทั้งบทพูดถึงประโยชน์ของการทำสวนต่อร่างกายและจิตใจ ส่วน A/C/D ไม่ใช่ใจความหลัก

questions.question: What physical benefit does gardening provide?
choices: It makes people much taller over time.
It removes the need for any sleep.
It completely cures every kind of serious disease.
It gives gentle exercise that strengthens muscles.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — ย่อหน้าที่ 2 บอกว่าการทำสวน (ขุด ยก ก้ม แบก) ให้การออกกำลังกายเบาๆ ที่เสริมสร้างกล้ามเนื้อ

questions.question: How does time among plants affect the mind?
choices: It lowers stress and lifts mood.
It makes people far more anxious.
It has no effect on the mind.
It causes people to feel angry.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — ย่อหน้าที่ 3 บอกว่าการอยู่ท่ามกลางต้นไม้ช่วยลดความเครียดและทำให้อารมณ์ดีขึ้น

questions.question: In paragraph 5, the word "scarce" is closest in meaning to:
choices: rather dirty and quite unsafe
free for everyone
hard to find; not enough
very brightly coloured
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — "green space is scarce" หมายถึงพื้นที่สีเขียวหายาก/มีไม่พอ ตรงกับ "hard to find; not enough"

questions.question: Why might the slow rhythm of gardening be satisfying today?
choices: Because plants grow in a single day.
Because the world is full of instant results.
Because gardening requires no attention.
Because food never tastes good when grown.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ย่อหน้าที่ 4 บอกว่าในโลกที่ทุกอย่างรวดเร็ว จังหวะช้าๆ ของการทำสวนกลับน่าพอใจ จึงอนุมานได้ว่าเพราะโลกเต็มไปด้วยผลลัพธ์ทันใจ

questions.question: How do community gardens help cities?
choices: By making green space in the cities much rarer.
By keeping all neighbours far apart.
By bringing people together and feeding insects.
By removing all the trees from streets.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — ย่อหน้าที่ 5 บอกว่าสวนชุมชนทำให้เพื่อนบ้านมาเจอกันและเป็นอาหารให้ผึ้งกับแมลง

questions.question: What is the writer's view of gardening?
choices: It is unusually valuable for body and mind.
It is an old-fashioned waste of time.
It is dangerous for older people.
It matters only to professional farmers.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — ย่อหน้าสุดท้ายบอกว่าการทำสวนมีคุณค่าเป็นพิเศษต่อกายและใจ จึงเป็นท่าทีบวก

questions.question: How is the passage mainly organized?
choices: It tells one gardener's day in order.
It lists plants from easiest to hardest.
It gives steps for planting a seed.
It describes several benefits of gardening.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — บทไล่อธิบายประโยชน์ของการทำสวนทีละด้าน (กาย → ใจ → ความอดทน → ชุมชน/สิ่งแวดล้อม) จึงเป็นการบรรยายหลายประโยชน์

passages.title: Should Exams Be Replaced by Coursework?
passages.passage:
For generations, the final exam has been the standard way of measuring what students have learned. Yet exams have always had their critics, and recently the debate has grown louder. Some argue that traditional exams should be replaced, or at least balanced, by coursework — projects, essays, and assignments completed over time. Which approach really gives the truest picture of a student's ability?

Those who favour coursework point to the weaknesses of exams. A single test, taken on a single day, may not reflect what a student truly knows. Nervous students can freeze, and a moment of bad luck — illness, a difficult night's sleep, an unfamiliar question — can ruin months of hard work. A student who understands a subject deeply may still panic and underperform on the day. Coursework, by contrast, allows students to show their understanding gradually, to think deeply, to research, and to improve their work, much as people do in real jobs. It rewards steady effort rather than performance under pressure.

Supporters of exams, however, defend them firmly. An exam, they argue, is fair precisely because every student faces the same questions, in the same conditions, at the same time, with no outside help. Coursework, they warn, is far easier to cheat at: a student may receive help from parents, friends, or, increasingly, from technology, making it hard to know whose work is really being marked. Exams also teach valuable skills — working under pressure, managing time, and thinking quickly — that matter throughout life.

There is also the question of practicality. Exams are relatively quick and cheap to organise and to mark, while good coursework takes a great deal of teachers' time to set, supervise, and assess fairly. In large schools with limited resources, replacing every exam with carefully judged coursework could place an enormous burden on already busy teachers.

Faced with these competing strengths, many education systems have chosen not to pick one or the other, but to combine them. A final grade might rest partly on coursework completed during the year and partly on an exam at the end, so that students are judged both on steady effort and on what they can do under pressure. In this way, the weaknesses of each method are balanced by the strengths of the other. Such mixed systems try to be fair to many different kinds of learner at once.

In the end, the debate reflects a deeper question: what are we really trying to measure? If we want to test memory and quick thinking, exams may serve well; if we value research, creativity, and persistence, coursework may be better. Perhaps the wisest schools are those that remember no single method can capture everything a young person is capable of becoming.

questions.question: What is the central question of the passage?
choices: Whether students should study at night.
Whether exams should be replaced by coursework.
Whether teachers are paid enough money.
Whether essays are longer than tests.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ทั้งบทถกประเด็น "ควรเปลี่ยนจากการสอบเป็นงานที่ทำต่อเนื่อง (coursework) ไหม" ตัวเลือกอื่นเป็นรายละเอียดย่อย

questions.question: What weakness of exams do their critics point to?
choices: They are far too easy for everyone.
They take many months to complete.
A single bad day can ruin months of work.
They reward research and deep thinking.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — ย่อหน้าที่ 2 บอกว่าการสอบวันเดียวอาจไม่สะท้อนความรู้จริง โชคร้ายวันเดียว (ป่วย/นอนไม่พอ) ทำลายความพยายามหลายเดือนได้

questions.question: Why do supporters say exams are fair?
choices: Every student faces the same questions and conditions.
Students can get help from their friends as well.
They usually take a very long time to mark.
They openly allow the use of any technology.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — ย่อหน้าที่ 3 บอกว่าการสอบยุติธรรมเพราะทุกคนเจอคำถามเดียวกัน เงื่อนไขเดียวกัน เวลาเดียวกัน โดยไม่มีตัวช่วย

questions.question: In paragraph 4, the word "burden" is closest in meaning to:
choices: a pleasant reward
a quick and easy task
a kind of long holiday
a heavy load to carry
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — "an enormous burden on already busy teachers" หมายถึงภาระหนักที่ครูต้องแบก ตรงกับ "a heavy load to carry"

questions.question: Why is coursework described as easier to cheat at?
choices: Because it is always done inside an exam hall.
Because students may get outside help at home.
Because teachers complete it for the students.
Because it is never marked by anyone.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ย่อหน้าที่ 3 บอกว่า coursework โกงง่ายกว่า เพราะนักเรียนอาจได้รับความช่วยเหลือจากพ่อแม่ เพื่อน หรือเทคโนโลยีที่บ้าน

questions.question: What practical advantage do exams have?
choices: They reward steady effort over time.
They allow students to improve their work.
They are relatively quick and cheap to mark.
They require no questions at all.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — ย่อหน้าที่ 4 บอกว่าการสอบจัดและตรวจได้ค่อนข้างเร็วและประหยัด ต่างจาก coursework ที่กินเวลาครูมาก

questions.question: In paragraph 5, "the strengths of the other" refers to the strengths of:
choices: the other method, whether exam or coursework
the students who actually sit the test
the parents who are helping out at home
the teachers who mark the work
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — ประโยคบอกว่าจุดอ่อนของวิธีหนึ่งถูกชดเชยด้วยจุดแข็งของอีกวิธี ดังนั้น the other จึงหมายถึงอีกวิธีหนึ่ง (สอบ หรือ coursework)

questions.question: How would you best describe the writer's overall position?
choices: Certain that exams should be abolished completely.
Sure that coursework can never be trusted.
Uninterested in how students are tested.
Balanced, supporting a combination of both methods.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — ผู้เขียนเสนอทั้งสองฝ่ายแล้วเอนไปทางการผสมทั้งสองวิธี จึงเป็นจุดยืนสมดุล

questions.question: How is the passage mainly organized?
choices: It tells one student's whole exam day in order.
It gives arguments for, against, then a combined approach.
It lists school subjects by their difficulty.
It explains, step by step, how to write a long essay.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — บทเสนอฝ่ายหนุน coursework → ฝ่ายหนุนสอบ → เรื่องการปฏิบัติจริง → แล้วสรุปแนวทางผสม จึงเป็นโครงนี้

passages.title: The History of Chocolate
passages.passage:
Today, chocolate is one of the most popular treats in the world, sold in shops on every continent and enjoyed by billions of people. Yet the sweet chocolate bar we know is a fairly recent invention. The story of chocolate stretches back thousands of years, and for most of that time it was not a cheap snack at all, but a rare and precious substance, valued more like money than like sweets.

Chocolate begins with the seeds, or beans, of the cacao tree, which grows in the warm forests of Central and South America. Long ago, ancient peoples such as the Maya and the Aztecs learned to roast and grind these beans to make a drink. But this early chocolate was nothing like ours. It was bitter, spicy, and unsweetened, often mixed with chilli, and it was usually drunk rather than eaten. To these civilisations, chocolate was sacred, used in important ceremonies and offered to the gods.

Cacao beans were so valuable that they were used as a form of money. A person could buy food, goods, or even pay workers with a handful of beans. In some places, even a small number of beans could be exchanged for a basket of goods. Chocolate was a luxury of the rich and powerful, and ordinary people rarely tasted it. When European explorers first encountered chocolate, they were puzzled by its bitter taste, but they soon carried it back across the ocean, where it would slowly be transformed.

In Europe, people began adding sugar to chocolate, turning the bitter drink into a sweet and fashionable treat. For a long time, however, it remained a drink for the wealthy, sipped in elegant chocolate houses. Rich families would gather to drink it warm, treating it as a mark of taste and status. The chocolate we recognise today — solid, sweet, and affordable — did not appear until the nineteenth century, when new machines made it possible to produce smooth chocolate bars cheaply and in large amounts.

Only then did chocolate change from a luxury into something almost anyone could enjoy. Falling prices and clever advertising turned it into an everyday pleasure and a gift for special occasions, a role it still plays around the world today. What had once been the drink of emperors slowly became a small, everyday delight for ordinary families. The journey from a sacred, bitter drink to a sweet bar in a corner shop took many centuries and crossed several continents.

It is strange to think that the chocolate enjoyed so casually today was once treated as sacred, traded as money, and reserved for kings. Behind a simple, familiar pleasure lies a long and surprising history, connecting modern shoppers to ancient forests, distant civilisations, and the slow, winding journey of a single remarkable bean.

questions.question: What is the passage mainly about?
choices: How to make chocolate bars at home.
The long history of chocolate.
Why chocolate is bad for your health.
Which country eats the most chocolate.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ทั้งบทเล่าประวัติศาสตร์อันยาวนานของช็อกโกแลต ส่วน A/C/D ไม่ใช่ใจความหลัก

questions.question: What was early chocolate like?
choices: Sweet, solid, and very cheap.
A frozen dessert eaten with a spoon.
A drink mixed only with fresh milk.
Bitter, spicy, and usually drunk.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — ย่อหน้าที่ 2 บอกว่าช็อกโกแลตยุคแรกขม เผ็ด ไม่หวาน มักผสมพริก และดื่มมากกว่ากิน

questions.question: How did ancient peoples use cacao beans, besides as food?
choices: As a form of money.
As building material.
As a kind of clothing.
As fuel for their fires.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — ย่อหน้าที่ 3 บอกว่าเมล็ดคาเคามีค่ามากจนใช้เป็นเงิน ซื้อของหรือจ่ายค่าจ้างได้

questions.question: In paragraph 2, the word "sacred" is closest in meaning to:
choices: cheap and very common
sour and unpleasant
holy and special
cold and frozen
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — "chocolate was sacred, used in ceremonies and offered to the gods" ดังนั้น sacred = ศักดิ์สิทธิ์/พิเศษ ตรงกับ "holy and special"

questions.question: Why did chocolate become popular in Europe?
choices: Because Europeans kept it bitter and spicy.
Because people began adding sugar to it.
Because it was used only in ceremonies.
Because it was always given away for free.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ย่อหน้าที่ 4 บอกว่าในยุโรปคนเริ่มเติมน้ำตาล เปลี่ยนเครื่องดื่มขมให้เป็นของหวานที่นิยม จึงอนุมานได้ว่าเพราะการเติมน้ำตาล

questions.question: When did the solid, affordable chocolate bar appear?
choices: Thousands of years ago in the forest.
As soon as Europeans first tasted it.
In the nineteenth century, with new machines.
Only within the last very few years indeed.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — ย่อหน้าที่ 4 บอกว่าช็อกโกแลตแท่งแข็งราคาเข้าถึงได้เพิ่งเกิดในศตวรรษที่ 19 เมื่อมีเครื่องจักรใหม่

questions.question: What is the writer's main purpose?
choices: To trace how chocolate changed over time.
To warn that chocolate is dangerous.
To sell a particular brand of chocolate.
To prove ancient chocolate tasted best.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — บทมุ่งเล่าว่าช็อกโกแลตเปลี่ยนแปลงอย่างไรตามกาลเวลา ไม่ได้เตือนภัย ขายยี่ห้อ หรือพิสูจน์เรื่องรสชาติ

questions.question: How is the passage mainly organized?
choices: It lists chocolate brands by their price.
It gives steps for making a chocolate drink.
It compares two modern chocolate shops.
It follows chocolate's history through time.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — บทเล่าตามลำดับเวลา จากเครื่องดื่มขมศักดิ์สิทธิ์ → ยุโรปเติมน้ำตาล → ช็อกโกแลตแท่งยุคใหม่ จึงเป็นโครงตามเวลา
